attributes.description: This section provides visualizations to help you assess compliance with CCI-001495, focusing on protecting audit tools from unauthorized deletion. You'll find visualizations designed to highlight any attempts to remove audit tool configurations, binaries, or logs. To perform a component-level compliance assessment, utilize the global filters at the top of the Kibana dashboard. Apply filters like 'host.name: <your_component_name>' to focus on a specific system. Observe the trends and individual events in the visualizations for any anomalies or unauthorized deletions. Any deletion event not performed by an explicitly authorized user or process should be investigated thoroughly. This visualization now displays the health status for CCI-001495 based on the anomaly scores from associated ML jobs.
attributes.title: [CCI-001495] Protect Audit Tools from Unauthorized Deletion ML

attributes.description:
This section provides Machine Learning (ML) powered visualizations to help assess compliance with CCIs 001494 and 001495, which mandate the protection of audit information and tools from unauthorized modification and deletion. This visualization analyzes anomaly scores from an ML job that detects unusual modification or deletion events.

To perform a component-level compliance assessment, use the global filters to focus on a specific component (e.g., 'influencers.host.name: <your_component_name>'). The ML model learns the baseline of normal file changes. High-score anomalies can reveal attempts to tamper with or destroy audit records that might be missed by signature-based tools, such as an attacker using a novel technique. Investigate any significant anomaly, paying close attention to the user, the file path, and the process involved.
attributes.title: [AU-9-CCIs-001494,1495] ML - Audit Modification and Deletion Anomalies
attributes.visState: {"aggs":[],"params":{"fontSize":12,"markdown":"# [AU-9] CCIs 001494 & 001495: Audit Modification/Deletion (ML)\n\n### Monitor for anomalous modification or deletion of audit information and tools.","openLinksInNewTab":false},"title":"[AU-9-CCIs-001494,1495] ML - Audit Modification and Deletion Anomalies","type":"markdown"}

attributes.description:
This visualization displays the total count of instances where unauthorized deletion attempts were made on audit tools, providing a high-level overview for **CCI-001495** (protecting audit tools from unauthorized deletion). 

**Why this visualization works:** It provides a quick, high-level understanding of the overall unauthorized activity directed at protecting audit tools. A non-zero count here immediately flags a potential compliance issue, signaling a need for deeper investigation into your deletion controls. 

**How an analyst can use it:** Use this metric in conjunction with the trend visualization to understand if a high number of unauthorized attempts is a recent spike or a persistent issue. This visualization acts as a quick health check for the deletion controls on audit tools. A continuously high count, even if not spiking, suggests a need for deeper investigation into why unauthorized users are attempting to delete audit functions. Remember to use global filters to focus on specific components (e.g., 'host.name: <your_component_name>').

query.query: (file.path:"/var/log/audit/*" OR file.path:"C:\Windows\System32\winevt\Logs\*" OR file.extension:(log OR evtx OR json OR xml OR conf OR cfg OR ini) OR process.name:(auditd OR wevtutil OR auditpol OR journalctl OR powershell.exe OR cmd.exe OR bash OR wmic OR sc.exe OR reg.exe OR net.exe OR taskkill.exe OR schtasks.exe OR at.exe OR psexec.exe OR mimikatz.exe OR certutil.exe OR bitsadmin.exe)) AND (event.category:(file OR process OR registry) OR event.type:(deletion OR file_delete OR process_termination OR registry_key_deletion)) AND NOT (user.name:(*system* OR *administrator* OR 'authorized_admin_group' OR "NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM" OR LocalSystem OR NetworkService) AND event.outcome:success) AND NOT (process.name:auditd AND event.action:file_delete AND event.outcome:success)

attributes.title: [AU-9, CCI-001495] Total Unauthorized Audit Tool Deletions

attributes.description:
This visualization displays the top audit files or processes that are subject to access, modification, or deletion attempts. This is a vital overview for the entire **AU-9** control, encompassing aspects of **CCI-000162, CCI-000163, CCI-000164, CCI-001494, and CCI-001495**. 

**Why this visualization works:** It helps analysts quickly identify the most frequently targeted audit resources (logs or tools). A sudden spike or consistent activity on a specific audit file or process, especially by unauthorized users, can indicate a focused attack or a critical misconfiguration. This allows for targeted investigation on the most critical audit components, ensuring comprehensive protection. 

**How an analyst can use it:** Monitor for any audit files or processes with unusually high activity. Drill down into specific 'file.path' or 'process.name' entries to see the detailed events. If unauthorized attempts are concentrated on a few critical audit resources, this visualization helps pinpoint the immediate areas requiring attention for AU-9 compliance across all related CCIs. Remember to use global filters to focus on a specific component (e.g., 'host.name: <your_component_name>').

query.query:
(
  (
    file.path : "/var/log/audit/*"
    OR file.path : "/var/log/secure"
    OR file.path : "/var/log/messages"
    OR file.path : "C:\\Windows\\System32\\winevt\\Logs\\*"
    OR file.path : "C:\\Windows\\System32\\Config\\*"
  )
  OR (event.module : (auditbeat OR winlogbeat OR filebeat OR sysmon OR osquery) AND file.extension : (evtx OR log))
)
AND (
  event.action : (file_deleted OR file_modified OR file_renamed OR file_moved OR file_created OR registry_value_deleted OR registry_value_set OR registry_key_deleted OR registry_key_created OR configuration_changed OR service_configuration_changed)
  OR event.category : (registry OR file)
)
AND NOT (
  user.name : ("NT AUTHORITY\\SYSTEM" OR "LocalSystem" OR NetworkService OR "NT AUTHORITY\\LOCAL SERVICE" OR administrator)
)
AND NOT process.name : (filebeat OR auditbeat OR metricbeat OR winlogbeat OR nxlog.exe OR rsyslogd OR systemd-journald)

attributes.title: [AU-9] Top Targeted Audit Resources (All AU-9 CCIs)

attributes.title: [CCI-000162/3/4, CCI-001494/5] Top Targeted Audit Resources

attributes.description:
This table provides a detailed breakdown of the most significant anomalies related to the modification or deletion of audit information and tools.

**Why this visualization works:**
It provides the critical details needed for a forensic investigation. By highlighting the influencers (user, file, process, action), it allows an analyst to quickly understand the context of an anomalous change and determine if it was a legitimate administrative action or a malicious act.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Filter for high anomaly scores (e.g., anomaly_score >= 75). Focus on the `influencers.file.path` to see what was changed and `influencers.event.action` to see how (modification or deletion). Cross-reference with `influencers.user.name` to verify authorization. This is your primary tool for investigating potential tampering events.
attributes.title: [AU-9-CCIs-001494,1495] ML - Detailed Audit Mod/Del Anomalies

attributes.description:
This table provides a detailed breakdown of individual events related to unauthorized deletions of audit tools, offering granular insights for **CCI-001495** (protecting audit tools from unauthorized deletion). 

**Why this visualization works:** It offers granular detail on each event, including the timestamp, host, user, process, and file involved, and the specific action attempted. This level of detail is crucial for in-depth investigation and pinpointing exact policy violations, allowing you to understand the 'who, what, and where' of potential audit tool availability failures. 

**How an analyst can use it:** Sort by 'user.name' or 'process.name' to identify common sources of unauthorized activity. Examine 'file.path' to pinpoint specific audit resources being targeted. The 'event.action' field will confirm the deletion attempt. This table is critical for understanding the context of each unauthorized deletion and verifying if your deletion controls are working as intended. Remember to apply global filters for specific components (e.g., 'host.name: <your_component_name>') to refine your view.

query.query: (file.path:"/var/log/audit/*" OR file.path:"C:\Windows\System32\winevt\Logs\*" OR file.extension:(log OR evtx OR json OR xml OR conf OR cfg OR ini) OR process.name:(auditd OR wevtutil OR auditpol OR journalctl OR powershell.exe OR cmd.exe OR bash OR wmic OR sc.exe OR reg.exe OR net.exe OR taskkill.exe OR schtasks.exe OR at.exe OR psexec.exe OR mimikatz.exe OR certutil.exe OR bitsadmin.exe)) AND (event.category:(file OR process OR registry) OR event.type:(deletion OR file_delete OR process_termination OR registry_key_deletion)) AND NOT (user.name:(*system* OR *administrator* OR 'authorized_admin_group' OR "NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM" OR LocalSystem OR NetworkService) AND event.outcome:success) AND NOT (process.name:auditd AND event.action:file_delete AND event.outcome:success)

attributes.title: [CCI-001495] CCI RMF Dashboard

attributes.description: This section provides visualizations to help you assess compliance with CCI-003876, focusing on the total number of security reports generated. This visualization displays the health status of total security reports generated based on anomaly scores from the associated ML job. To perform a component-level compliance assessment, utilize the global filters at the top of the Kibana dashboard. Apply filters like 'host.name: <your_component_name>' or 'kibana.report.name: <report_name>' to focus on specific systems or report types. Observe the trends and individual anomaly events. A high anomaly score indicates a significant deviation from the established baseline, which could signify unexpected increases or decreases in report generation, indicating potential issues with reporting mechanisms or unauthorized data manipulation.
attributes.title: [CA-7, CCI-003876] Total Security Reports Generated ML

attributes.description:
**Why and When:** This visualization provides a single, immediate metric for the total number of security reports generated within the selected time frame.
It's best used as a quick health check.

**How to Use:** Set the time range to your specific reporting period.
For example, if you are checking for a daily report, set the time range to 'Today'.
The metric should display '1'. If checking for the past week, set it to 'Last 7 days', and the count should ideally be '7'.
Any deviation from the expected number provides an instant signal to investigate further using the trend and table visualizations.

query.query: event.category:iam AND event.type:access AND event.action:(*report* OR *export* OR *download*) AND (kibana.report.name:(*security* OR *state* OR *compliance* OR *audit* OR *vulnerability*) OR process.args:(*--output* OR *--format*report* OR *--export*))

attributes.title: [CA-7 / CCI-003876] Total Security Reports Generated

attributes.description:
**Why this visualization works:** This visualization displays the maximum anomaly score over time for the job monitoring the total number of security reports generated. It helps to identify unusual patterns in the overall volume of security reports, which could signal a lack of compliance in terms of report generation frequency or completeness. A sudden drop might mean reports aren't being generated, while a massive, unexpected spike might indicate a system compromise or malicious activity. 

**How an analyst can use it:** Look for any spikes or dips in the anomaly score. A high score suggests an unexpected deviation from the normal volume of reports. Use the time selector to isolate these periods and investigate the specific `user.name`, `kibana.report.name`, `host.name`, and `event.outcome` associated with the anomaly to understand the cause of the unusual report generation activity and ensure compliance.
attributes.title: [CA-7, CCI-003876] ML - Total Security Reports Anomaly Trend

attributes.description:
**Why and When:** This table is crucial for forensic investigation and detailed verification.
When the trend chart shows a potential anomaly (a missed day or an unexpected spike), use this table to see the raw event details.
It helps you confirm *who* generated the report and *what* report was generated.

**How to Use:** Sort by '@timestamp' to review events in chronological order.
Check the 'user.name' column to ensure the user or service account is authorized to perform this action.
The 'kibana.report.name' column confirms the correct report was run. The 'event.outcome' column should be reviewed to ensure the report generation was successful.
This table provides the concrete evidence needed for compliance audits.

query.query: event.category:iam AND event.type:access AND event.action:(*report* OR *export* OR *download*) AND (kibana.report.name:(*security* OR *state* OR *compliance* OR *audit* OR *vulnerability*) OR process.args:(*--output* OR *--format*report* OR *--export*))

attributes.title: [CA-7 / CCI-003876] Detailed Report Generation Events

attributes.title: CA-7 CCI-003876

attributes.description: This visualization displays the health status for [CCI-000213] [AC.L1-3.1]  by monitoring anomaly scores from its associated Machine Learning job. The background color reflects the highest anomaly score related to unauthorized access attempts, providing a quick assessment of compliance. A high score indicates a significant deviation from normal user access patterns, signaling a potential policy violation that requires investigation.
attributes.title: [CCI-000213] Enforce Approved Authorizations ML

attributes.description:
This visualization displays the total count of logical access attempts by users not on the authorized list.

**Why this visualization works:**
It provides an immediate, high-level indicator of a potential compliance failure. A single count greater than zero means an unauthorized user has attempted to access a resource, which directly contradicts the access control policy.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Monitor this number as a primary health check. If the count is anything other than zero, it signifies that further investigation is required. Use this as a starting point to pivot to the trend and detailed event visualizations to understand the scope and nature of the policy violation.

query.query: (event.action: ("session_start" OR "user_login" OR "user_authentication" OR "sudo_start") OR event.category: (authentication OR session) OR winlog.event_id: (4624 OR 4625 OR 4648 OR 4672)) AND NOT user.name: (root OR *system* OR *administrator* OR "NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM" OR "-" OR *service_account* OR *authorized_user*)

attributes.title: [CCI-000213] Total Unauthorized Access Attempts Number

attributes.description:
This visualization displays a time-series trend of logical access attempts from unauthorized users.

**Why this visualization works:**
It helps an analyst understand the magnitude and timeline of unauthorized access. A sudden spike might indicate a brute-force attack or a misconfiguration, while a low, consistent level of attempts could point to a persistent but stealthy adversary or a recurring policy misunderstanding.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Look for spikes or any consistent, non-zero baseline of activity. Hover over data points to see the count of unauthorized attempts at a specific time. Use the time range selector to zoom in on periods of high activity to correlate with other events or begin a more focused investigation.

query.query: (event.action: ("session_start" OR "user_login" OR "user_authentication" OR "sudo_start") OR event.category: (authentication OR session) OR winlog.event_id: (4624 OR 4625 OR 4648 OR 4672)) AND NOT user.name: (github OR ftpuser OR *admin* OR root OR *system* OR *administrator* OR "NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM" OR "-" OR *service_account* OR *authorized_user*)

attributes.title: [CCI-000213] Unauthorized Access Attempts Trend (converted)

attributes.description:
This visualization breaks down the unauthorized access attempts by the user accounts that initiated them.

**Why this visualization works:**
It immediately answers the question 'Who is attempting unauthorized access?' By visualizing the top offenders, it helps prioritize an investigation. A single user with many attempts is a different investigative path than many users with single attempts.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Review the chart to identify which user accounts are responsible for unauthorized access attempts. Pay special attention to any unexpected or highly active accounts. Use this information to guide your investigation, such as reviewing the permissions and recent activity of the identified users.

query.query: (event.action: ("session_start" OR "user_login" OR "user_authentication" OR "sudo_start") OR event.category: (authentication OR session) OR winlog.event_id: (4624 OR 4625 OR 4648 OR 4672)) AND NOT user.name: (github OR ftpuser OR *admin* OR root OR *system* OR *administrator* OR "NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM" OR "-" OR *service_account* OR *authorized_user*)

attributes.title: [CCI-000213] Top Unauthorized Users

attributes.description:
This table provides a detailed breakdown of individual unauthorized access events.

**Why this visualization works:**
It offers the most granular level of detail, which is essential for forensic investigation. An analyst can see the exact timestamp, source, destination, user, and process involved in a potential policy violation, which is crucial for determining root cause and impact.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Use this table as the final step in an investigation. Sort by '@timestamp' to see the most recent events. Filter by a specific 'user.name' or 'host.name' identified in the other visualizations. Examine the 'process.name', 'source.ip', and 'event.outcome' fields to understand precisely what happened, whether it was successful, and where it came from. This information is critical for any incident response or compliance reporting.

query.query: (event.action: ("session_start" OR "user_login" OR "user_authentication" OR "sudo_start") OR event.category: (authentication OR session) OR winlog.event_id: (4624 OR 4625 OR 4648 OR 4672)) AND NOT user.name: (github OR ftpuser OR *admin* OR root OR *system* OR *administrator* OR "NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM" OR "-" OR *service_account* OR *authorized_user*)

attributes.title: [CCI-000213] Detailed Unauthorized Access Events Table

attributes.description:
This table provides a detailed breakdown of individual unauthorized access events.

**Why this visualization works:**
It offers the most granular level of detail, which is essential for forensic investigation. An analyst can see the exact timestamp, source, destination, user, and process involved in a potential policy violation, which is crucial for determining root cause and impact.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Use this table as a control. Compare this table to the UNAUTHORIZED table and assure that you are looking at the correct unauthorizations. Sort by '@timestamp' to see the most recent events. Filter by a specific 'user.name' or 'host.name' identified in the other visualizations. Examine the 'process.name', 'source.ip', and 'event.outcome' fields to understand precisely what happened, whether it was successful, and where it came from. This information is critical for any incident response or compliance reporting.

query.query: (event.action: ("session_start" OR "user_login" OR "user_authentication" OR "sudo_start") OR event.category: (authentication OR session) OR winlog.event_id: (4624 OR 4625 OR 4648 OR 4672)) AND user.name: (github OR ftpuser OR *admin* OR root OR *system* OR *administrator* OR "NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM" OR "-" OR *service_account* OR *authorized_user*)

attributes.title: [CCI-000213] Detailed AUTHORIZED Access Events Table

attributes.description:
This section helps assess compliance with CCI-000213 by verifying that only approved, authorized users are accessing system resources. These visualizations are designed to detect and highlight any logical access attempts by users who are not explicitly on the approved list for a given component.

**How an analyst can use it:**
To perform a component-level compliance assessment, use the global filters at the top of the dashboard. For instance, apply a filter like 'host.name: <your_component_name>' to focus on a specific server or endpoint. Review the visualizations to identify any access events (successful or failed) initiated by users not defined in your access control policy. A non-zero count in the 'Total Unauthorized Access Attempts' visualization is an immediate indicator of a potential policy violation that requires investigation. Use the trend and detailed tables to understand the context and specifics of each unauthorized event.

attributes.title: [CCI-000213] [AC.L1-3.1]

attributes.description: This section provides visualizations to help you assess compliance with CCI-001848, focusing on ensuring that the actions of individual users can be uniquely traced back to them. This visualization now displays the health status of user action traceability for CCI-001848 based on the anomaly scores from the associated ML job.
attributes.title: [AU-4, CCI-001848] Audit Logging - User Action Traceability ML

attributes.description:
This visualization displays a time-series trend of auditable user activities over time. 

**Why this visualization works:**
It provides a quick visual of spikes or consistent activity patterns, allowing analysts to correlate user actions with specific time periods. This helps in understanding the normal rhythm of a system and spotting unusual behavior. It confirms that auditable events are being consistently logged.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Look for sudden increases or decreases in activity that are not aligned with normal business hours or expected behavior. Hover over data points to see the event count at a specific time. Use the time range selector to focus on periods of interest and see if the activity is related to an expected user or process. This helps in verifying that all user actions are being captured and that the auditing system is operational.

attributes.title: [AU-4, CCI-001848] User Activity Trend

attributes.description:
This visualization displays the maximum anomaly score over time for the job monitoring auditable user activities. 

**Why this visualization works:** This visualization is essential because it uses machine learning to establish a baseline of normal user activity and flags deviations from that norm. It can detect subtle, suspicious behaviors that might be missed by static rules. For instance, a user who normally only logs in during business hours suddenly accessing systems at 3 AM from an unusual location would generate a high anomaly score, indicating a potential compromise or insider threat. 

**How an analyst can use it:** Look for spikes in the anomaly score. A high score is a critical signal that warrants immediate investigation. You can use the time selector to isolate these periods and apply filters for 'influencers.user.name' or 'influencers.host.name' to pinpoint the specific user and system involved. This helps you determine if the activity was legitimate or if further action is required.
attributes.title: [AU-4, CCI-001848] ML - User Activity Anomaly Score Trend (converted)

attributes.description:
This section provides visualizations to help you assess compliance with CCI-001848, focusing on ensuring that the actions of individual users can be uniquely traced back to them.

You'll find a series of visualizations designed to highlight user-specific activity and provide granular detail on each event. To perform a component-level compliance assessment, utilize the global filters at the top of the Kibana dashboard. Apply filters like 'host.name: <your_component_name>' to focus on a specific system. Observe the distribution of events by user, the overall trend of activity, and the details in the event table to confirm that each auditable action has a corresponding user identity. A high volume of events from an unexpected user account, or a large number of events with a 'user.name' field that is empty or generic, should be investigated.
attributes.title: [AU-4, CCI-001848] Audit Logging - User Action Traceability
attributes.visState: {"title":"[AU-4, CCI-001848] Audit Logging - User Action Traceability","type":"markdown","aggs":[],"params":{"fontSize":12,"markdown":"# [CCI-001848] Audit Logging\n\n### Ensure user actions are uniquely traceable.","openLinksInNewTab":false}}

attributes.description:
This visualization displays the distribution of all user activity by 'user.name'.

**Why this visualization works:**
It quickly highlights which users are most active, allowing an analyst to verify that activity is coming from expected user accounts. A sudden appearance of a new user with high activity, or an unexpected concentration of activity from a single user, could be a sign of a compromised account or misconfigured system. This visual aid is perfect for identifying outliers quickly.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Use this pie chart to quickly scan for and identify unexpected or highly active users. Clicking on a slice of the pie chart will apply a filter for that specific user, allowing you to drill down into their activity in the other visualizations to verify if their actions are legitimate. This is a powerful tool for a quick and effective compliance check.

attributes.description:
This table provides a detailed breakdown of individual events related to user activities. 

**Why this visualization works:**
It offers granular detail on each event, including the user, host, and the specific action attempted. This level of detail is crucial for in-depth investigation and pinpointing exactly what a user did and when, directly supporting the core requirement of this CCI.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Sort by '@timestamp' to see the most recent events, or by 'user.name' to identify all actions taken by a specific user. Examine 'host.name' to see which component the action took place on. This table is essential for verifying that every user action is being logged and can be traced back to an individual, fulfilling the compliance requirement.

attributes.title: AU-4 CCI-001848

attributes.description: This section provides visualizations to help you assess compliance with CCI-003877, focusing on monitoring the status of security report generation. This visualization displays the health status of report generation based on anomaly scores from the associated ML job. To perform a component-level compliance assessment, utilize the global filters at the top of the Kibana dashboard. Apply filters like 'host.name: <your_component_name>' or 'kibana.report.name: <report_name>' to focus on specific systems or report types. Observe the trends and individual anomaly events. A high anomaly score indicates a significant deviation from the established baseline, which could signify unexpected failures, successes, or changes in the outcome of report generation, indicating potential issues with the report generation process itself or attempts to manipulate report integrity.
attributes.title: [CA-7, CCI-003877] Security Report Generation Status Monitoring ML

attributes.description:
This visualization provides a quick summary of the total number of security-related reports generated within the selected time frame.

**Why this visualization works:**
It gives an immediate, high-level count of reporting activity.
This metric serves as a quick health check; a zero or unexpectedly low count is an immediate red flag that the reporting process may be broken, directly impacting compliance with CCI-003877.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Use this number as a starting point.
If the count seems low for the selected time range (e.g., you expect 30 daily reports in a month but see only 5), use the trend and table visualizations to pinpoint when the reporting stopped or became inconsistent.
This metric is a good barometer for the overall health of your security reporting function.

kibanaSavedObjectMeta.searchSourceJSON: {"query":{"language":"kuery","query":"event.action:(*report* OR *export* OR *download* OR *email*) AND (event.category:iam OR event.module:kibana) AND kibana.report.name:(*security* OR *compliance* OR *state* OR *vulnerability* OR *audit*)"},"filter":[],"indexRefName":"kibanaSavedObjectMeta.searchSourceJSON.index"}

attributes.title: [CA-7 / CCI-003877] Total Security Reports Generated

attributes.description:
This visualization breaks down the generated reports by their names.

**Why this visualization works:**
It helps an analyst verify that the *correct* reports are being generated, not just that *some* report is running.
If your policy requires a 'Daily Vulnerability Summary' and a 'Weekly Compliance Overview', this chart will confirm if both are being created and in what proportion.
It helps ensure the substance of the reporting requirement is being met.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Review the pie chart slices to ensure all expected security reports are present.
If a critical report is missing from the chart, it means it hasn't been generated in the selected time frame.
Click on a slice to filter the entire dashboard for that specific report, allowing you to see its generation trend and detailed events.

attributes.description:
This table provides a detailed, event-by-event breakdown of each generated security report.

**Why this visualization works:**
It offers granular proof of compliance.
When an auditor asks for evidence that a specific report was generated on a particular day, this table provides the definitive answer.
It shows the exact timestamp, the report name, and the user who generated it, which is crucial for forensic analysis and audit validation.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Use this table to investigate specific events.
Sort by '@timestamp' to see the most recent reports. Sort by 'user.name' to see who is responsible for generating the most reports.
If the trend chart shows a gap, you can use this table to confirm the exact last time a report was successfully generated before the failure.
This is your ground-truth log for all reporting activity.

**Why this visualization works:**
It helps an analyst verify that the *correct* reports are being generated, not just that *some* report is running.
If your policy requires a 'Daily Vulnerability Summary' and a 'Weekly Compliance Overview', this chart will confirm if both are being created and in what proportion.
It helps ensure the substance of the reporting requirement is being met.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Review the pie chart slices to ensure all expected security reports are present.
If a critical report is missing from the chart, it means it hasn't been generated in the selected time frame.

**Why this visualization works:**
It offers granular proof of compliance.
When an auditor asks for evidence that a specific report was generated on a particular day, this table provides the definitive answer.
It shows the exact timestamp, the report name, and the user who generated it, which is crucial for forensic analysis and audit validation.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Use this table to investigate specific events.
Sort by '@timestamp' to see the most recent reports. Sort by 'user.name' to see who is responsible for generating the most reports.
If the trend chart shows a gap, you can use this table to confirm the exact last time a report was successfully generated before the failure.
This is your ground-truth log for all reporting activity.","enhancements":{"dynamicActions":{"events":[]}},"title":"[CA-7 / CCI-003877] Detailed Security Report Generation Events"},"panelIndex":"a058b678-0cf6-4bfc-8a26-9c77ff32190d","gridData":{"i":"a058b678-0cf6-4bfc-8a26-9c77ff32190d","y":30,"x":0,"w":48,"h":18}}]
attributes.title: CA-7 CCI-003877

attributes.description: This visualization displays the health status of CCI-000131 compliance based on anomaly scores from the associated ML job. High scores may indicate an anomalous drop in overall audit log volume, which could signal incomplete records.
attributes.title: [CCI-000131] Content of Audit Records ML

attributes.description:
**Why and When:** This table provides the actionable intelligence for [CCI-000131] [AU.L2-3.3] compliance. When the trend chart signals a drop in log volume, this table identifies the exact host(s) responsible for the anomaly.

**How to Use:** When an anomaly is detected, find the corresponding `host.name` in this table. This is your starting point for investigation. Check the health of the host, its logging agent, and network connectivity to determine the root cause of the interruption in logging.
attributes.title: [AU-3, CCI-000131] ML - Hosts with Low Log Volume

attributes.description:
This visualization provides a summary of the different types of audit events being generated.

**Why and when this visualization works:**
This chart directly supports the 'what' requirement of the CCI. It provides a quick, high-level confirmation that a diverse range of expected activities (e.g., login, file access, process execution) is being captured in the logs. Use this to get a baseline understanding of system activity before diving into the details.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Review the chart to ensure the event categories and actions align with the expected behavior of the system you are assessing. An absence of expected event types (like 'authentication' or 'process') could indicate a misconfiguration or logging gap. Click on a slice to filter the dashboard for a specific event type.

query.query: (event.module:(auditbeat OR winlogbeat OR filebeat OR o365audit OR gcp.audit OR osquery OR suricata OR zeek) OR cloud.service.name:("CloudTrail" OR "Azure Activity Logs" OR "GCP Audit Logs" OR "Kubernetes") OR log.file.path:("/var/log/*" OR "/var/log/audit/*" OR "C:\\Windows\\System32\\winevt\\Logs\\*") OR process.name:(auditd OR journald OR rsyslogd OR syslog-ng OR "Windows Event Log" OR wevtutil.exe) OR (event.category: * AND event.type: *))

attributes.title: [CCI-000131] Audit Event Types Breakdown (converted)

attributes.description:
This visualization displays the volume of audit records over time, broken down by outcome.

**Why and when this visualization works:**
This chart is essential for verifying the 'when' and 'outcome' aspects of the CCI. It provides assurance that logging is continuous and consistent. It's the first place to check if you suspect logs are missing or have stopped reporting for a component.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Look for unexpected drops in total log volume, which could indicate a logging failure and result in incomplete audit trails. Also, monitor for significant spikes in 'failure' outcomes, as this may point to security events that require deeper investigation using the detailed table. Use the time selector to isolate specific periods of interest.

query.query: (event.module:(auditbeat OR winlogbeat OR filebeat OR o365audit OR gcp.audit OR osquery OR suricata OR zeek) OR cloud.service.name:("CloudTrail" OR "Azure Activity Logs" OR "GCP Audit Logs" OR "Kubernetes") OR log.file.path:("/var/log/*" OR "/var/log/audit/*" OR "C:\\Windows\\System32\\winevt\\Logs\\*") OR process.name:(auditd OR journald OR rsyslogd OR syslog-ng OR "Windows Event Log" OR wevtutil.exe) OR (event.category: * AND event.type: *))

attributes.title: [CCI-000131] Audit Event Trend by Outcome (converted)

attributes.description:
To perform a component-level compliance assessment for CCI-000131, use the global filters at the top of the dashboard to isolate specific systems (e.g., 'host.name: <your_component_name>').

The goal is to verify that for any given event, the audit records contain six key pieces of information:
1.  **What type of event occurred:** Verified with the 'event.action' and 'event.category' fields.
2.  **When the event occurred:** Verified with the '@timestamp' field.
3.  **Where the event occurred:** Verified with fields like 'host.name', 'source.ip', and 'destination.ip'.
4.  **The source of the event:** Verified with fields like 'user.name', 'process.name', and 'source.ip'.
5.  **The outcome of the event:** Verified with the 'event.outcome' field.
6.  **The identity of any associated individuals:** Verified with the 'user.name' field.

Use the 'Detailed Audit Record Content' table as your primary tool. Scan the columns for any blank or missing values for the events generated by your filtered component. Any significant gaps indicate that the system is not generating complete audit records as required. The supporting visualizations help confirm that a healthy and consistent stream of logs is being generated for analysis.
attributes.title: [CCI-000131] Content of Audit Records

attributes.description: This visualization displays the health status for CCI-000265 based on the anomaly scores from the associated Machine Learning (ML) job 'ca-5-cci-000265-poam-findings-trend'. The ML job detects anomalous trends in security findings that should trigger Plan of Action and Milestones (POA&M) updates. The color of the visualization indicates the maximum anomaly score detected, with green representing normal activity (score >= 0), yellow indicating a minor anomaly (score >= 20), and red a critical anomaly (score >= 100) that requires immediate investigation.

90e3aedf-fb07-49ed-9635-b3a526d6029a.label: [CCI-000265] Plan of Action and Milestones Updates

attributes.title: [CCI-000265] Plan of Action and Milestones Updates ML

attributes.description:
This visualization provides an at-a-glance health status for your Plan of Action and Milestones (POA&M) process, based on anomaly scores from the underlying Machine Learning job. The score reflects deviations from the normal baseline of security findings that typically require POA&M updates, supporting compliance with CA-5 and related CCIs (CCI-000265, CCI-000266, CCI-000267).

### How to Assess Component Compliance:
To perform a component-level compliance assessment, utilize the global filters at the top of the Kibana dashboard. Apply a filter such as `host.name: "your_component_name"` to isolate the anomaly score for a specific system. An abnormally high score for a specific component indicates a potential surge in vulnerabilities, threats, or compliance failures that needs immediate attention and a potential POA&M update, as required by **CCI-000265**.

### Score Interpretation:
* **Green (0-24):** Normal operations.
* **Yellow (25-74):** Minor anomaly detected; warrants review.
* **Red (75+):** Critical anomaly detected; requires immediate investigation.
attributes.title: [CA-5] ML - POA&M Findings Anomaly Health

attributes.description:
**Why this works:** This table provides the ground-truth details for each anomaly detected. It allows for deep investigation into the 'who, what, and where' of anomalous security findings, which is crucial for creating, monitoring, and closing out POA&Ms (**CCI-000265, CCI-000266, CCI-000267**).

**How to use it:**
* Sort by `anomaly_score` in descending order to prioritize the most severe anomalies.
* Filter by `host.name` to investigate a specific system's unusual findings.
* The `by_field_name` column indicates if the anomaly was for a specific host (`host.name`) or an overall count (empty).
* The influencer columns (`user.name`, `rule.name`, `event.category`) provide context for why the anomaly was triggered. Use this information to inform your POA&M.

attributes.description: This section provides visualizations to help you assess compliance with CCI-001849, focusing on the review and update of audited events. By analyzing these visualizations, you can verify that the events being captured align with your organization's defined audit policy. This visualization now displays the health status of audited event reviews for CCI-001849 based on the anomaly scores from the associated ML job.
attributes.title: [AU-4 CCI-001849] Audited Events Review ML

attributes.description:
This visualization displays a time-series trend of all audited events, broken down by category or type. 

**Why this visualization works:** It helps analysts visualize the volume of different event types over time. By seeing trends, an analyst can identify if a certain type of event is suddenly being logged more or less frequently, which could indicate a change in system behavior or a modification to the audit configuration that needs to be reviewed. This is essential for verifying that the audit policy is being consistently applied. 

**How an analyst can use it:** Look for any unexpected spikes or drops in event types. Use this trend to confirm that the types of events you expect to be audited (e.g., file access, process creation) are being consistently captured across the monitored time period. You can drill down by selecting a specific time range to see the events in more detail in the table visualization. This helps ensure that the audit logging is stable and reliable.

query.query: (event.module:(auditd OR winlogbeat OR filebeat OR sysmon) OR event.dataset:(linux.auditd OR windows.eventlog OR cloudtrail OR gcp.audit))

attributes.title: [AU-4 CCI-001849] Audited Events Trend by Type

attributes.description:
This visualization displays the maximum anomaly score over time for the job monitoring audited events. 

**Why this visualization works:** This visualization is key for CCI-001849 as it monitors the volume and types of all audited events. A sudden drop in the number of audited events could indicate that an attacker has disabled or tampered with the audit system, while a large, unexplained spike could signify a Denial-of-Service attack on the audit log. The ML job learns the normal pattern of event volume, allowing it to flag these critical deviations. 

**How an analyst can use it:** Look for spikes or sudden drops in the anomaly score trend. Any significant change in the pattern of audited events should be investigated immediately. Use the global time range filter to narrow down the period of the anomaly and apply additional filters on 'influencers.host.name' or 'influencers.category' to identify the specific system or event type that is exhibiting anomalous behavior.
attributes.title: [AU-4, CCI-001849] ML - Audited Events Anomaly Score Trend (converted)

attributes.description: This section provides visualizations to help you assess compliance with CCI-001849, focusing on the review and update of audited events. By analyzing these visualizations, you can verify that the events being captured align with your organization's defined audit policy. To perform a component-level compliance assessment, utilize the global filters at the top of the Kibana dashboard. Apply filters like 'host.name: <your_component_name>' to focus on a specific system. Use this section to review the types and volume of audited events to ensure all required activities are being logged according to your organization's defined frequency.
attributes.title: [AU-4 CCI-001849] Audited Events Review
attributes.visState: {"title":"[AU-4 CCI-001849] Audited Events Review","type":"markdown","aggs":[],"params":{"markdown":"# [CCI-001849] Audited Events Review\n\n### Review and update the list of audited events.","fontSize":12,"openLinksInNewTab":false}}

attributes.description:
This visualization displays the total count of all audited events across your systems, providing a high-level overview of auditing activity. 

**Why this visualization works:** It gives an immediate health check on whether events are being actively logged. A zero count might indicate a misconfiguration or failure in your audit logging system. A high number shows that a large volume of events is being captured, which can be further investigated using other visualizations. 

**How an analyst can use it:** Use this number as a starting point. A low or zero count, especially over a period where activity is expected, should trigger an immediate investigation. This metric helps confirm that your audit logging system is operational and that the review process has a sufficient volume of events to analyze.

attributes.description:
This table provides a detailed, event-by-event breakdown of all audited events. It is a critical tool for the manual review process of CCI-001849. 

**Why this visualization works:** It offers granular detail on each event, including the host, user, process, and the specific action attempted. This is crucial for in-depth investigation and confirming that the data being logged is rich enough to satisfy audit requirements. You can see the exact values being captured for each event, which is necessary for making informed decisions about updating the list of audited events. 

**How an analyst can use it:** Sort by `@timestamp` to see the most recent events, or by `host.name` or `user.name` to focus on specific components or users. Examine `event.action` and `event.type` to verify that the specific activities your organization requires to be audited are being logged correctly. This table allows you to perform a final check on the data being captured before concluding whether the current audit configuration is compliant with the policy.

attributes.title: AU-4 CCI-001849

attributes.description: This visualization displays the health status of OS family diversity based on anomaly scores from the associated ML job. To perform a component-level compliance assessment, utilize the global filters at the top of the Kibana dashboard. Apply filters like 'host.os.family: <os_family_name>' to focus on a specific operating system family. Observe the trends and individual anomaly events. A high anomaly score indicates a significant deviation from the established baseline, which could signify unexpected changes in the types or volume of operating systems being monitored, potentially impacting the comprehensiveness of your security monitoring.
attributes.title: [CA-7, CCI-003878] OS Family Diversity Monitoring ML

attributes.description:
This visualization displays the total number of unique data sources (`event.provider`) sending logs into the system.

**Why and when this visualization works:**
This metric provides a direct, at-a-glance measurement of the breadth of your data collection, which is the foundation for correlation and analysis.
Use it as a quick health check to ensure your monitoring scope is as wide as you expect.
A higher number is generally better, indicating a more diverse set of inputs for security analysis.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Monitor this number over time.
A sudden decrease could indicate a significant failure in a logging pipeline or that a data source has gone offline, which would directly impact your ability to correlate data and meet this control.
attributes.title: [CA-7 / CCI-003878] Data Source Diversity

attributes.description:
This visualization displays the total number of unique Operating System families (`host.os.family`) from which logs are being collected.

**Why and when this visualization works:**
This metric confirms that your data correlation efforts span across different environments (e.g., Windows, Linux, macOS).
It's a key indicator that you have visibility across your entire operational landscape, not just a single type of system.
This is crucial for understanding the system's security state in heterogeneous environments.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Check this number to ensure all expected OS families are represented.
If your organization uses Windows, Linux, and cloud services, you should see a count of at least 3. If a family is missing, it points to a critical visibility gap that must be addressed.
attributes.title: [CA-7 / CCI-003878] OS Family Diversity

attributes.description:
This visualization shows the proportional breakdown of log records by their source (`event.provider`).

**Why and when this visualization works:**
This chart is essential for understanding the balance of your data sources.
While it's expected for some sources (like firewalls or endpoint agents) to be 'chatty', a healthy system will show a variety of active sources.
If one source completely dominates the view, it may indicate that other, less frequent sources are being overlooked in analysis.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Use this chart to visually confirm the presence of all critical data sources.
Hover over the slices to see the specific counts. If a critical source (e.g., 'cloudtrail', 'sysmon') represents a very small slice or is grouped into 'Other', you may need to investigate its logging configuration or use filters to create a more focused view.
attributes.title: [CA-7 / CCI-003878] Data Source Distribution

attributes.description:
This visualization provides a time-series trend of incoming logs, stacked by the data source (`event.provider`).

**Why and when this visualization works:**
This chart is crucial for verifying the *consistency* of your data sources. Correlation and analysis are only effective if the underlying data is reliable and continuous. This view makes it immediately obvious if a data source has stopped reporting, which would create a critical blind spot.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Look for stable, continuous bands of color. If a color representing a critical data source suddenly disappears, it signifies a logging outage that must be investigated. Use the time selector to zoom in on specific periods where you suspect an issue. A healthy trend shows all expected sources reporting without significant interruption.
attributes.title: [CCI-003878] Data Ingestion Trend (converted)

attributes.title: CA-7 CCI-003878

attributes.description: This visualization displays the health status of CCI-000132 compliance based on anomaly scores from the associated ML job. High scores may indicate hosts sending logs with timestamps not in UTC.
attributes.title: [CCI-000132] Content of Audit Records: UTC Timestamps ML

attributes.description:
This donut chart provides a high-level overview of UTC timestamp compliance across all monitored hosts.

**Why this visualization works:**
It offers an immediate visual summary of your compliance posture. The color-coded sections make it easy to see the proportion of compliant versus non-compliant systems at a glance, which is ideal for dashboards and executive reporting.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Use this chart to quickly assess the overall health of UTC compliance. The 'Compliant' slice (representing timezones '+00:00' or 'Z') should ideally be 100%. Any other slices represent non-compliant systems. Click on a non-compliant slice to filter the dashboard and see more details about the specific hosts in that category.
attributes.title: [CCI-000132] UTC Timestamp Compliance Overview (converted)

attributes.description:
This metric displays the total number of unique hosts that are NOT sending logs with a UTC timestamp.

**Why this visualization works:**
It provides a single, critical number that quantifies the scope of non-compliance. A value of zero indicates full compliance, while any other number shows exactly how many systems require immediate attention.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Monitor this number as a key performance indicator for UTC compliance. If the count is greater than zero, it confirms that there are non-compliant systems in the environment. Use this metric as a starting point to justify further investigation using the compliance matrix and other tools to identify and remediate the specific hosts.

query.query: event.timezone : * and not event.timezone : ("+00:00" or "Z" or "UTC")

attributes.title: [CCI-000132] Non-Compliant Host Count

attributes.description:
This data table provides a compliance matrix showing each host and its most recently reported timezone. It is the primary tool for identifying non-compliant systems.

**Why this visualization works:**
It offers a clear, actionable list of every component and its timestamp reporting status. This allows for precise identification of systems that need remediation to meet the UTC requirement.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Review the 'event.timezone' column. Any value other than '+00:00' or 'Z' indicates a non-compliant host. Sort by this column to group non-compliant systems together. You can then use the 'host.name' to begin your investigation and remediation process for each identified component.
attributes.title: [CCI-000132] Host Timezone Compliance Matrix (converted)

attributes.description:
**Why and When:** This table provides the detailed context needed to investigate timestamp anomalies identified by the ML job for CCI-000132. It pinpoints the exact hosts and the anomalous timezones they are reporting, allowing for targeted remediation.

**How to Use:** Review this table to identify hosts with high maximum anomaly scores. The `host.name` and `event.timezone` fields show which systems are deviating from their normal behavior. To investigate, filter the dashboard by a specific `host.name` from this table and examine the raw logs around the time of the anomaly to diagnose the configuration issue.
attributes.title: [AU-3, CCI-000132] ML - Top Non-UTC Timestamp Anomalies

attributes.description:
This section provides visualizations to help you assess compliance with CCI-000132, which requires that audit records contain timestamps in Coordinated Universal Time (UTC). You will find visualizations that identify systems not reporting in UTC and show the overall compliance status.

To perform a component-level compliance assessment, utilize the global filters at the top of the Kibana dashboard. Apply filters like 'host.name: <your_component_name>' to focus on a specific system. The primary goal is to ensure that the 'event.timezone' for all logs is either '+00:00' or 'Z'. Any host reporting a different timezone is non-compliant and requires investigation. The visualizations below will help you quickly identify these systems.
attributes.title: [CCI-000132] Content of Audit Records: UTC Timestamps

attributes.title: [AU-3 CCI-000132] [AU.L2-3.3]

attributes.description: This visualization displays the health status of central audit review for CCI-001910 based on the anomaly scores from associated ML jobs. High scores indicate spikes in failed activities or rare event actions, signaling unusual activity that requires review.
attributes.title: [CCI-001910] Central Audit Review and Analysis ML

attributes.description:
This chart provides a high-level breakdown of all generated audit events by their category. This supports the review and update process mentioned in CCI-001910.

**Why and When:** Use this visualization during periodic compliance reviews to understand your current audit scope. It helps answer the question, 'Are we logging the right types of events?'. For example, if 'network' events make up a very small slice of the pie, you may need to investigate if your network devices or hosts are configured to send the right logs to meet your organization's auditing requirements.

**How to Use:** Review the distribution of event categories. Does it align with your organization's risk profile and compliance needs? If you recently deployed a new web application, you would expect to see a corresponding increase in 'web' or 'network' category events. If the distribution looks wrong, it may be time to review and update the audit settings on your components.
attributes.title: [AU-12] Audited Events by Category

attributes.description:
This chart shows the distribution of different types of activities (event actions) occurring across your systems.
**Why and when this visualization works:**
It helps you understand the *character* of activity, not just the volume.

'Unusual activity' isn't always a massive spike in events; sometimes it's a subtle shift in what users or systems are doing.

This visualization makes those shifts visible. For example, a sudden increase in the percentage of 'file-deletion' or 'user-password-change' actions is highly suspicious, even if overall event counts remain stable.
**How an analyst can use it:**
- Establish a visual baseline of the normal distribution of event actions for a component.
- Look for any unexpected changes in the proportions.

- A slice of the pie that is normally small becoming large is a red flag.
- Click on a slice of the pie to filter the entire dashboard by that specific action, allowing you to investigate who or what is responsible for the change.

query.query: event.category:(authentication or authorization or configuration or database or driver or file or host or iam or intrusion_detection or network or package or process or registry or session or threat) or event.dataset:(*.audit* or "osquery.result" or *.zeek.* or *.suricata.* or "sysmon.event" or "windows.security" or "windows.system" or "k8s.audit" or "crowdstrike.event" or "carbonblack.event") or event.module:(auditbeat or winlogbeat or filebeat or sysmon or osquery or zeek or suricata or gcp or azure or aws or o365 or okta)

attributes.title: [CCI-001910] Breakdown by Event Action

attributes.description:
This visualization displays the overall volume of audit events over time, establishing a rhythm of normal activity for your environment.

**Why and when this visualization works:**
It's your first stop for identifying unusual activity.

By understanding your system's baseline, any deviation becomes immediately apparent.

Use this to spot broad changes, such as a sudden spike in activity that could signal an ongoing incident, or a drop to zero, which might indicate a critical logging failure.

**How an analyst can use it:**
- Look for significant spikes or dips in the event count.
- A surge in 'failure' outcomes is a strong indicator of malicious activity like a brute-force attack.
- Hover over data points to see the exact event count at a specific time.
- Use the time range selector to zoom in on suspicious periods identified in this chart, then use the detailed table below to investigate the specific events.

query.query: event.category:(authentication or authorization or configuration or database or driver or file or host or iam or intrusion_detection or network or package or process or registry or session or threat) or event.dataset:(*.audit* or "osquery.result" or *.zeek.* or *.suricata.* or "sysmon.event" or "windows.security" or "windows.system" or "k8s.audit" or "crowdstrike.event" or "carbonblack.event") or event.module:(auditbeat or winlogbeat or filebeat or sysmon or osquery or zeek or suricata or gcp or azure or aws or o365 or okta)

attributes.title: [CCI-001910] Overall Audit Event Trend (converted)

attributes.description:
This table provides a detailed, sortable breakdown of all failed audit events, helping you pinpoint the exact source of potentially inappropriate activity.

**Why and when this visualization works:**
When the trend chart shows a spike in failures, this is the place to investigate the 'who, what, and where'.

It provides the granular evidence needed for any follow-up investigation, showing you exactly which users, hosts, or processes are responsible for the suspicious activity.

**How an analyst can use it:**
- Sort by `user.name` or `source.ip` to identify accounts or systems with a high number of failures.
- Sort by `process.executable` to see if a specific piece of software is failing authentication or access checks repeatedly.
- Examine the `event.action` to understand what is being attempted (e.g., `user_login`, `file_access`).
- Use this information to report on specific threats or identify misconfigured systems.

query.query: ((event.category:(authentication or authorization or configuration or database or driver or file or host or iam or intrusion_detection or network or package or process or registry or session or threat) or event.dataset:(*.audit* or "osquery.result" or *.zeek.* or *.suricata.* or "sysmon.event" or "windows.security" or "windows.system" or "k8s.audit" or "crowdstrike.event" or "carbonblack.event")) and event.outcome:(failure or failed)) and not (winlog.event_id:4768 and winlog.event_data.Status:"0x18") and not (process.name:("chrome.exe" or "firefox.exe" or "msedge.exe" or "MicrosoftEdge.exe") and event.category:file and event.type:access and event.outcome:failure)

attributes.title: [CCI-001910] Top Sources of Failed Activity

attributes.description:
**Why and When:** This table provides the context for anomalies where a rare action occurred on a specific host (supporting CCI-000171 and CCI-001910). It's the primary tool for understanding why the ML job flagged the behavior as anomalous.

**How to Use:** After identifying an anomaly in the trend chart, filter this table to that time. The `partition_field_value` column shows the host where the anomaly occurred, and the `by_field_value` shows the specific `event.action` that was rare for that host. Check the associated `influencers.user.name` and `influencers.process.name` to understand the full context and determine if the action was legitimate.
attributes.title: [AU-12, CCIs 000171, 001910] ML - Detailed Rare Action by Host Anomalies

attributes.description:
**Why and When:** When the trend chart shows a spike in failed activities, use this table to conduct a detailed investigation. It provides the essential context needed to understand the nature of the unusual activity and determine if it poses a threat, as required by CCI-001910.

**How to Use:** Filter to the time of the anomaly. The `influencers` fields will immediately highlight the key entities involved. For example, a high number of failures from a single `influencers.source.ip` could indicate an external attacker, while failures associated with a specific `influencers.user.name` might point to a compromised account or insider activity.
attributes.title: [AU-12, CCI-001910] ML - Detailed Failed Activity Spike Anomalies

attributes.description:
This section is designed to help you centrally review and analyze audit records for compliance with CCI-001910.

The visualizations below will help you identify inappropriate or unusual activity across your systems.

**How an analyst can use it:**
To perform a component-level compliance assessment, use the global filters at the top of the dashboard.

For instance, apply a filter like `host.name: <your_component_name>` to isolate the activity of a specific server, or `user.name: <username>` to review a particular user's actions.

Observe the trends and patterns across these visualizations.

Look for:
- **Unexpected Spikes or Dips:** The trend chart should show a consistent rhythm of activity.
- **High-Volume Failures:** The detailed table will pinpoint users or processes with a high rate of failed actions, which could indicate brute-force attempts or misconfigurations.
- **Anomalous Actions:** The event action chart shows what users are doing.

 A sudden shift, like an increase in deletion events, is a significant indicator of unusual activity.

attributes.title: [CCI-001910]

attributes.description: This section provides visualizations to help you assess compliance with CCI-003878, focusing on monitoring data source diversity. This visualization displays the health status of data source diversity based on anomaly scores from the associated ML job. To perform a component-level compliance assessment, utilize the global filters at the top of the Kibana dashboard. Apply filters like 'host.name: <your_component_name>' or 'event.provider: <provider_name>' to focus on specific systems or data sources. Observe the trends and individual anomaly events. A high anomaly score indicates a significant deviation from the established baseline, which could signify unexpected changes in the types or volume of data sources being monitored, potentially impacting the comprehensiveness of your security monitoring.
attributes.title: [CA-7, CCI-003878] Data Source Diversity Monitoring ML

attributes.description: This visualization displays the health status of CCI-000133 compliance based on anomaly scores from the associated ML job. High scores may indicate an anomalous number of events missing user identity information.
attributes.title: [CCI-000133] Content of Audit Records ML

attributes.description:
This visualization displays a time-series trend comparing the count of events that have user identity information with those that do not.

**Why and When:** This is your first stop for assessing CCI-000133. It provides a high-level, continuous view of compliance over time. Use it to quickly determine if missing user identity is a new problem (a recent spike in red) or a chronic issue (a persistent red line). It's most effective for understanding the overall health and trends of user attribution in your logs.

**How to Use:** The green area represents events where `user.name` is present, which is the desired state. The red area represents events where `user.name` is missing. For any given time period, the ratio of red to green shows your compliance level. A compliant system should have a minimal, if any, red area. If you see a significant red area, use the time selector to zoom in on that period and then use the detailed table visualization to investigate the specific non-compliant events.

query.query: event.category:(authentication OR process OR file OR iam OR network OR registry) OR event.type:(start OR change OR creation OR deletion OR access OR logon OR policy)

attributes.title: [CCI-000133] User Identity Presence Trend (converted)

attributes.description:
This visualization shows a percentage breakdown of which hosts are generating logs that are missing user identity information.

**Why and When:** Use this chart after you've identified that there's a problem with missing user data. It helps you prioritize your investigation by immediately showing which systems are the biggest offenders. If one host is responsible for 95% of the non-compliant logs, you know exactly where to start your analysis.

**How to Use:** Each slice of the donut represents a host, and the size of the slice corresponds to the percentage of non-compliant logs it has generated. Hover over a slice to see the host's name and the exact count of events. Click on a slice to filter the entire dashboard to focus on just that host, allowing you to see its specific trends and detailed events in the other visualizations.

query.query: ((event.category:(authentication OR process OR file OR iam) OR event.action:(*login* OR *session* OR *sudo* OR *runas* OR *su*)) AND (NOT user.name : * AND NOT user.id : *)) AND NOT (process.name:(kernel OR systemd OR svchost.exe OR services.exe OR lsass.exe OR wininit.exe OR crond OR anacron OR atd OR udevd OR dbus-daemon) OR user.domain:("NT AUTHORITY" OR "LOCAL SERVICE" OR "NETWORK SERVICE"))

attributes.title: [CCI-000133] Hosts Generating Events Without User Identity

attributes.description:
This table provides a detailed list of all audit events that are missing user identity information (`user.name`).

**Why and When:** Use this visualization when the trend chart shows a problem. This is your primary tool for deep-dive investigation. It provides the ground-truth data needed to identify the source of non-compliant logs and begin remediation.

**How to Use:** The table is pre-filtered to show only events where `user.name` is not present. Review the `host.name`, `process.name`, and `event.action` columns to understand the context of these events. For example, you might find that a specific script running as a service (`process.name`) on a particular server (`host.name`) is responsible for the non-compliant logs. You can use the search bar at the top to further filter the results (e.g., for a specific host). This information is critical for providing actionable feedback to system administrators.

query.query: ((event.category:(authentication OR process OR file OR iam) OR event.action:(*login* OR *session* OR *sudo* OR *runas* OR *su*)) AND (NOT user.name : * AND NOT user.id : *)) AND NOT (process.name:(kernel OR systemd OR svchost.exe OR services.exe OR lsass.exe OR wininit.exe OR crond OR anacron OR atd OR udevd OR dbus-daemon) OR user.domain:("NT AUTHORITY" OR "LOCAL SERVICE" OR "NETWORK SERVICE"))

attributes.title: [CCI-000133] Detailed Events Missing User Identity Table

attributes.description:
**Why and When:** This table is your primary tool for investigating CCI-000133 anomalies. It breaks down the anomalies by host, process, and event action, showing you precisely which activities are generating logs without user context.

**How to Use:** Identify entries with high anomaly scores. The `host.name` and `process.name` fields tell you where the issue is occurring and what process is responsible. This allows you to investigate the logging configuration for that specific process on that host to ensure user information is correctly captured and forwarded.
attributes.title: [AU-3, CCI-000133] ML - Hosts with Missing User Identities (converted)

attributes.description:
This section provides visualizations to help you assess compliance with CCI-000133. The primary goal is to verify that audit records contain information establishing the identity of any individual or subject associated with an event.

**Analyst Guidance for Component-Level Assessment:**

1.  **Set the Scope:** Use the main Kibana dashboard filters to narrow your focus to a specific component you are assessing. For example, set `host.name: "your-server-01"` or `service.name: "your-application"`.

2.  **Review the Trend:** Examine the 'User Identity Presence Trend' visualization. In a compliant system, the green line (Identity Present) should account for the vast majority of events. The red line (Identity Missing) should be at or near zero. Any significant volume of 'Identity Missing' events indicates a potential compliance gap.

3.  **Investigate Missing Identity:** If you see events with missing identity, turn to the 'Hosts Generating Events Without User Identity' donut chart. This will immediately show you which hosts are the source of the non-compliant logs. 

4.  **Analyze Raw Events:** Use the 'Detailed Events Missing User Identity' table to investigate the specific non-compliant events. Look at the `process.name` and `event.action` to understand what is happening on the system that is generating logs without user context. This is your primary tool for root cause analysis. The goal is to determine *why* these events are not being attributed to a user and remediate the configuration on that component.

attributes.title: [AU-3 CCI-000133] [AU.L2-3.3]

attributes.description: This visualization displays the health status for CCI-000832 based on the anomaly scores from associated ML jobs. The color indicates the maximum anomaly score detected across jobs that monitor for unusual signal volume, severity spikes, triage backlogs, and noisy hosts, which are all indicators of incident tracking and documentation effectiveness. Green indicates normal activity (score < 20), yellow indicates a minor anomaly (score 20-99), and red indicates a critical anomaly (score >= 100) that requires immediate investigation.

query.query: job_id:ir-5-cci-000832-signals-trend OR job_id:ir-5-cci-000832-severity-trend OR job_id:ir-5-cci-000832-triage-backlog-trend OR job_id:"ir-5-cci-000832-noisy-host-trend"

attributes.title: [CCI-000832] Incident Monitoring ML Score

attributes.description:
**Why and when this visualization works:**
This trend chart is essential for understanding the volume of incoming security signals over time, representing all trackable events. It helps visualize your environment's security posture and the workload for your analysts before documentation occurs. Spikes indicate a potential attack or misconfiguration that needs investigation.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Monitor the trend for anomalies. A large, sudden spike in signals should be investigated immediately. Use the time range selector to zoom in on periods of interest and correlate with the detailed log to see which detection rules are firing. This chart shows the start of the 'tracking' process.
attributes.title: [CCI-000832] Incoming Security Signals Trend (converted)

attributes.description:
This visualization displays the maximum anomaly score over time for the job monitoring the overall volume of security signals.

**Why and When It's Used:**
This chart is crucial for detecting broad, system-wide events. A sudden spike in the anomaly score suggests a significant deviation from the normal volume of security signals, which could indicate a large-scale attack, a widespread system misconfiguration, or a major outage. It provides an early warning that something unusual is affecting many parts of your environment at once.

**How an Analyst Can Use It:**
Monitor for any sharp increases or sustained high anomaly scores. A score of 75 or higher is critical and demands immediate investigation. Use the time selector to isolate the period of the anomaly, and then cross-reference with the 'Detailed Anomalies' table to identify the primary influencers (e.g., `signal.rule.name` or `host.name`) driving the high score.
attributes.title: [IR-5, CCI-000832] ML - Overall Security Signal Anomaly Trend

attributes.description:
This table provides a detailed, event-level view of all anomalies detected by the CCI-000832 ML jobs. It serves as the primary tool for deep-dive investigations.

**Why and When It's Used:**
When a trend chart shows a high anomaly score, this table is the next step. It provides the ground truth for the anomaly, showing the exact time, the job that triggered, the severity score, and the specific entities (influencers) involved. Use this to get the context needed for a full investigation.

**How an Analyst Can Use It:**
Filter this table by a specific `job_id` and time range to investigate a spike from one of the trend charts. Sort by `anomaly_score` in descending order to prioritize the most severe anomalies. The influencer columns (`host.name`, `user.name`, `signal.rule.name`) will tell you exactly who or what was involved in the anomalous activity.
attributes.title: [IR-5, CCI-000832] ML - Detailed Anomalies

attributes.description:
**Why and when this visualization works:**
This metric provides an immediate, at-a-glance count of all security signals that have been escalated to a case, representing formally documented incidents. Use it as a quick health check to gauge the overall volume of documented incident response activity.

**How an analyst can use it:**
This number directly reflects your documented incidents. A zero count when signals are present elsewhere is a red flag. A high count indicates a busy response team. Correlate this number with the total signal count to understand your signal-to-case ratio.
attributes.title: [CCI-000832] Total Documented Incidents (Cases)
attributes.visState: {"title":"[CCI-000832] Total Documented Incidents (Cases)","type":"metric","aggs":[{"id":"1","enabled":true,"type":"count","params":{"customLabel":"Total Incidents Documented","emptyAsNull":false},"schema":"metric"}],"params":{"addTooltip":true,"fontSize":"60","addLegend":false,"type":"metric","metric":{"percentageMode":false,"useRanges":false,"colorSchema":"Green to Red","metricColorMode":"None","colorsRange":[{"from":0,"to":10000}],"labels":{"show":true},"invertColors":false,"style":{"bgFill":"#000","bgColor":false,"labelColor":false,"subText":"","fontSize":60}}}}

attributes.description:
**Why and when this visualization works:**
This chart provides a quick overview of the documentation status of your security signals. It shows how many have been escalated to a case versus those that are still just open signals. This is key to ensuring that signals are being triaged and that the important ones are formally documented.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Review the distribution. A very large number of signals that are not in a case could indicate a triage backlog. A healthy distribution shows a manageable number of open signals and a steady flow into cases. You can click on either segment of the donut to filter the detailed table below.
attributes.title: [CCI-000832] Signal Documentation Status
attributes.visState: {"title":"[CCI-000832] Signal Documentation Status","type":"pie","aggs":[{"id":"1","enabled":true,"type":"count","params":{"emptyAsNull":false},"schema":"metric"},{"id":"2","enabled":true,"type":"filters","params":{"filters":[{"input":{"language":"kuery","query":"cases.status: *"},"label":"Documented in Case"},{"input":{"language":"kuery","query":"NOT cases.status: *"},"label":"Signal Not in Case"}]},"schema":"segment"}],"params":{"addTooltip":true,"addLegend":true,"legendPosition":"right","isDonut":true,"type":"pie","legendDisplay":"hide","nestedLegend":false,"truncateLegend":true,"maxLegendLines":1,"distinctColors":false,"emptySizeRatio":0.3,"palette":{"type":"palette","name":"default"},"labels":{"show":true,"last_level":false,"values":true,"valuesFormat":"percent","percentDecimals":2,"truncate":100,"position":"default"}}}

attributes.description:
**Why and when this visualization works:**
This table provides the definitive, granular proof of incident tracking and documentation. It lists individual security signals, allowing an analyst to see which rule was triggered and whether the signal was escalated to a case. This is the core visualization for verifying that the full process is being followed.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Filter for a specific component (e.g., 'host.name'). Review this table to see all security signals from that component. The 'Documentation Status' column is critical; use it to confirm that high-priority signals are being documented. If a signal is documented, you can see its case details. If not, this is an opportunity to investigate why.
attributes.title: [CCI-000832] Detailed Signal and Case Log
