attributes.title: [CCI-000832]

attributes.description: This section provides visualizations to help you assess compliance with CCI-002087, focusing on monitoring system change events. This visualization displays the health status of system change event monitoring based on anomaly scores from the associated ML job. To perform a component-level compliance assessment, utilize the global filters at the top of the Kibana dashboard. Apply filters like 'host.name: <your_component_name>' or 'user.name: <user_name>' to focus on a specific system or user. Observe the trends and individual anomaly events. A high anomaly score indicates a significant deviation from the established baseline, which could signify unauthorized system changes, misconfigurations, or unusual activity that warrants immediate investigation.
attributes.title: [CA-7, CCI-002087] System Change Events Monitoring ML

attributes.description:
**Why and When:** This trend chart is your first stop for understanding the rhythm of changes in your environment.
It helps establish a baseline of normal activity to quickly identify deviations.
Use it to spot anomalies at a high level. A sudden, unexpected spike in changes could indicate a major software deployment, a configuration update, or potentially unauthorized activity that warrants immediate investigation.

**How:** Hover over the chart to see the exact number of changes at any given time.
Use the time selector to zoom in on periods of interest.
If you see a spike, note the time and use the 'Detailed System Change Events' table to investigate the specific events that occurred during that period.

query.query: (event.category:package AND event.type:(installation OR deletion OR change)) OR (event.category:service AND event.type:(start OR stop OR change)) OR (event.category:configuration AND event.type:change)

attributes.title: [CA-7 / CCI-002087] System Change Events Over Time

attributes.description:
**Why this visualization works:** This visualization displays the maximum anomaly score over time for the job monitoring system change events. It helps analysts quickly spot unusual spikes or trends in system changes that might bypass rule-based alerts, such as unexpected installations, deletions, or configuration modifications. 

**How an analyst can use it:** Look for any sudden increases in the anomaly score. A high score suggests a significant deviation from the normal pattern of system changes. Use the time selector to pinpoint these periods and investigate the specific `host.name`, `user.name`, `event.category`, and `event.action` associated with the anomaly to determine if the change was authorized and intentional.
attributes.title: [CA-7, CCI-002087] ML - System Change Events Anomaly Trend (converted)

attributes.description:
**Why and When:** This is your primary tool for investigating the specifics of a change to determine its security impact.
When you need to understand the 'who, what, and when' of a change, this table provides the necessary context.
Use it after identifying a suspicious time frame from the trend chart or when you need to audit all changes on a critical system.

**How:** Use the column headers to sort the data.
For example, sort by `host.name` to group all changes for a specific server.
Use the search bar within the visualization to filter for specific users, processes, or file paths.
This detailed view is where you will correlate system events with change management tickets to verify that all modifications were authorized.

query.query: (event.category:package AND event.type:(installation OR deletion OR change)) OR (event.category:service AND event.type:(start OR stop OR change)) OR (event.category:configuration AND event.type:change)

attributes.title: [CA-7 / CCI-002087] Detailed System Change Events

attributes.title: CA-7 CCI-002087

attributes.description: This visualization displays the health status for CCI-004326 based on anomaly scores from associated ML jobs. These jobs detect hosts with unusually high vulnerability counts, spikes in new critical/high vulnerabilities, and anomalous remediation trends. A high anomaly score (yellow or red) indicates a significant deviation from the established baseline and warrants immediate investigation.

0f2a47a0-4f42-4501-861e-f42aa015c65e.label: [CCI-004326] Plan of Action and Milestones Process

attributes.title: [CCI-004326] Plan of Action and Milestones Process ML

attributes.description:
This visualization provides a high-level health status for CCI-004326 compliance by displaying the maximum anomaly score from all related vulnerability management ML jobs. A score of 0 indicates normal operation, while higher scores signal potential issues requiring investigation.

**Why this visualization works:**
It offers an immediate, at-a-glance summary of the vulnerability management posture. Instead of sifting through raw data, an analyst can instantly see if there are significant deviations from normal behavior across the monitored components.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Use the color coding as a guide: Green (0-24) is normal, Yellow (25-74) is a warning, and Red (75+) indicates a critical anomaly that requires immediate attention. To perform a component-level compliance assessment, use the dashboard's global filters (e.g., 'host.name: <your_component_name>') to narrow the scope. This metric will dynamically update to reflect the health of the specific component you are assessing. Use this as a starting point to dive into the more detailed trend and table visualizations below.
attributes.title: [CCI-004326] ML - Vulnerability Management Health Status

attributes.description:
This metric shows the total count of 'Critical' severity vulnerabilities that are currently in an 'open' state.

**Why this visualization works:**
It provides an immediate, at-a-glance indicator of the highest-risk vulnerabilities in the environment. A non-zero number here requires immediate attention.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Monitor this number daily. Any increase should trigger an investigation. When filtering for a specific component, this metric tells you instantly if that component has an unresolved critical vulnerability.
attributes.title: [CCI-004326] Open Critical Vulnerabilities

attributes.description:
This metric shows the total count of 'High' severity vulnerabilities that are currently in an 'open' state.

**Why this visualization works:**
It provides a quick overview of significant risks that should be prioritized for remediation right after critical ones.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Track this number to ensure that high-severity vulnerabilities are being addressed in a timely manner. A stagnant or increasing number indicates a backlog in the remediation process, which is a compliance concern.
attributes.title: [CCI-004326] Open High Vulnerabilities

attributes.description:
This chart shows the trend of open vs. fixed vulnerabilities over time.

**Why this visualization works:**
It provides a clear visual representation of the effectiveness of the vulnerability management program. It answers the question: 'Are we fixing things faster than they are being discovered?'.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Look for a downward trend in the 'open' line and an upward trend in the 'fixed' line. A crossing pattern where 'fixed' surpasses 'open' is a sign of a healthy program. If the 'open' line is continuously increasing, it indicates that the remediation process cannot keep up with new discoveries, which is a significant compliance risk.
attributes.title: [CCI-004326] Vulnerability Remediation Trend

attributes.description:
This is a searchable data table that provides granular details for all vulnerability findings.

**Why this visualization works:**
It serves as the definitive source for all vulnerability data, allowing for deep-dive investigations and providing the evidence needed for compliance audits.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Use the search bar to find specific vulnerabilities (e.g., by CVE ID) or to filter for a specific host. Sort by `@timestamp` to see the newest or oldest vulnerabilities. An analyst must use this table to confirm that vulnerabilities are being tracked and that their status is updated as they are remediated. This is the primary tool for verifying the details of the Plan of Action and Milestones process.
attributes.title: [CCI-004326] Detailed Vulnerability Findings

attributes.description:
This table lists the top 10 hosts with the highest number of open vulnerabilities.

**Why this visualization works:**
It immediately identifies the systems that pose the most risk to the organization, allowing for focused and prioritized remediation efforts.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Use this list to create action plans for system administrators. If a critical system appears on this list, it should be a top priority. An analyst can also use this to identify systemic issues; for example, if all the top hosts are running the same operating system, there may be a problem with the patching process for that OS.
attributes.title: [CCI-004326] Top 10 Vulnerable Hosts

attributes.description:
This section provides visualizations to help you assess compliance with CCI-004326, which focuses on the organization's process for mitigating known vulnerabilities. These visualizations rely on data from vulnerability scanners (e.g., Nessus, Qualys, Trivy) being ingested into Elasticsearch.

**How to Perform a Component-Level Compliance Assessment:**
To assess a specific component, use the global filters at the top of the Kibana dashboard. For example, apply a filter like `host.name: "your_component_name"` to scope all the visualizations to that single system.

1.  **Review the 'Open Vulnerabilities' Metrics:** Check the counts of critical, high, medium, and low vulnerabilities for the component. A large number of open critical or high vulnerabilities indicates a significant risk and potential non-compliance.
2.  **Analyze the 'Vulnerability Remediation Trend':** This chart shows whether your team is closing vulnerabilities faster than new ones are being discovered. For a compliant component, you should see the number of 'fixed' vulnerabilities trending upwards and 'open' vulnerabilities trending downwards over time.
3.  **Examine the 'Top Vulnerable Hosts' chart:** While filtering for a single component, this chart will confirm its vulnerability count relative to others. When viewed without filters, it helps prioritize which systems need immediate attention.
4.  **Investigate the 'Detailed Vulnerability Findings' Table:** This table provides the ground truth. For your specific component, review the open vulnerabilities. Pay attention to the `@timestamp` to determine the age of each finding. Long-outstanding critical vulnerabilities are a major compliance concern.
attributes.title: [CCI-004326] Plan of Action and Milestones Process

**How to Perform a Component-Level Compliance Assessment:**
To assess a specific component, use the global filters at the top of the Kibana dashboard. For example, apply a filter like `host.name: "your_component_name"` to scope all the visualizations to that single system.

4.  **Investigate the 'Detailed Vulnerability Findings' Table:** This table provides the ground truth. For your specific component, review the open vulnerabilities. Pay attention to the `@timestamp` to determine the age of each finding. attributes.title: [CCI-004326] PM-4

attributes.description: This visualization displays the health status of CCI-000134 compliance based on anomaly scores from the associated ML job. High scores may indicate an anomalous number of events missing the 'event.outcome' field.
attributes.title: [CCI-000134] Content of Audit Records ML

attributes.description:
**Why and when this visualization works:**
This pie chart provides a high-level, at-a-glance overview of the entire dataset. It is the best starting point to quickly assess whether the 'event.outcome' field is being populated across all monitored components. A healthy chart will be dominated by 'success' and 'failure' slices.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Look for the presence of a 'missing' slice in the pie chart. If a significant portion of events are missing an outcome, it signifies a critical gap in logging and a failure to meet this CCI. Use this as a starting point to filter down to the components or log sources that are failing to report event outcomes.

query.query: (event.category:(authentication OR process OR file OR network OR iam OR configuration OR policy OR database OR host OR web OR intrusion_detection OR malware OR session OR threat) OR event.type:(access OR change OR creation OR deletion OR error OR start OR end OR info OR installation OR modification OR network OR policy OR process OR protocol OR session OR user OR admin) OR event.dataset:(*.audit OR *.security OR *.events OR *.log OR auditd OR sysmon OR system OR security OR powershell OR osquery OR cloudtrail OR azure.* OR gcp.* OR o365 OR okta OR crowdstrike OR carbonblack OR sentinelone OR suricata OR zeek OR nginx OR apache OR mssql OR mysql OR postgresql OR auditbeat OR winlogbeat OR filebeat))

attributes.title: [CCI-000134] Event Outcome Distribution (converted)

attributes.description:
**Why and when this visualization works:**
This stacked bar chart offers a more granular view by breaking down event outcomes for each specific event category (e.g., authentication, file, network). It's crucial for identifying if logging gaps are isolated to certain types of activities. For instance, you might find that authentication logs are compliant, but file modification events are not.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Examine each bar to ensure it contains both 'success' and 'failure' segments[cite: 10]. If a bar for a critical category (like 'authentication' or 'iam') shows a large 'missing' segment or is missing entirely, it indicates a compliance failure for that specific type of logging. Use this to pinpoint which log shippers or configurations need to be fixed.

query.query: (event.category:(authentication OR process OR file OR network OR iam OR configuration OR policy OR database OR host OR web OR intrusion_detection OR malware OR session OR threat) OR event.type:(access OR change OR creation OR deletion OR error OR start OR end OR info OR installation OR modification OR network OR policy OR process OR protocol OR session OR user OR admin) OR event.dataset:(*.audit OR *.security OR *.events OR *.log OR auditd OR sysmon OR system OR security OR powershell OR osquery OR cloudtrail OR azure.* OR gcp.* OR o365 OR okta OR crowdstrike OR carbonblack OR sentinelone OR suricata OR zeek OR nginx OR apache OR mssql OR mysql OR postgresql OR auditbeat OR winlogbeat OR filebeat))

attributes.title: [CCI-000134] Event Outcomes by Category

attributes.description:
**Why and when this visualization works:**
This table provides the most granular evidence for compliance. It is essential for deep-dive analysis, incident response, and providing concrete proof to auditors. When the charts indicate a problem, this table allows you to see the raw log details to understand the root cause.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Use this table to sample events or investigate specific issues found in the other visualizations. Filter by a host or user to see their activity. Sort by the 'event.outcome' column to quickly group successes and failures. The key is to confirm that for any given event, the 'event.outcome' field is populated. If you filter for a specific log source and find this column is consistently empty, you have identified a non-compliant component.

query.query: (event.category:(authentication OR process OR file OR network OR iam OR configuration OR policy OR database OR host OR web OR intrusion_detection OR malware OR session OR threat) OR event.type:(access OR change OR creation OR deletion OR error OR start OR end OR info OR installation OR modification OR network OR policy OR process OR protocol OR session OR user OR admin) OR event.dataset:(*.audit OR *.security OR *.events OR *.log OR auditd OR sysmon OR system OR security OR powershell OR osquery OR cloudtrail OR azure.* OR gcp.* OR o365 OR okta OR crowdstrike OR carbonblack OR sentinelone OR suricata OR zeek OR nginx OR apache OR mssql OR mysql OR postgresql OR auditbeat OR winlogbeat OR filebeat))

attributes.title: [CCI-000134] Detailed Event Records

attributes.description:
**Why and When:** This table provides actionable details for anomalies related to CCI-000134. It identifies the specific hosts, event categories, and actions that are most frequently associated with missing `event.outcome` fields, enabling efficient troubleshooting.

**How to Use:** Focus on entries with the highest anomaly scores. The combination of `host.name`, `event.category`, and `event.action` provides precise context about where logging is failing. For a given host, you can investigate the logging configuration for the specific application or service generating the anomalous events to ensure `event.outcome` is properly captured.
attributes.title: [AU-3, CCI-000134] ML - Hosts with Missing Event Outcomes (converted)

attributes.description:
To perform a component-level compliance assessment for CCI-000134, utilize the global filters at the top of the Kibana dashboard. Apply filters such as 'host.name: <your_component_name>' or 'data_stream.dataset: <your_log_source>' to isolate and evaluate a specific system or data source.

The primary goal is to verify that audit logs consistently contain the 'event.outcome' field, populated with either 'success' or 'failure'.

1.  **Examine the 'Event Outcome Distribution'**: This chart should primarily show 'success' and 'failure'. A significant 'missing' slice indicates that those log sources are not compliant with this CCI[cite: 1].
2.  **Analyze the 'Event Outcomes by Category'**: This visualization breaks down outcomes by the type of activity. Check critical categories like 'authentication', 'process', and 'file' to ensure they all report outcomes. [cite_start]If a category is missing this data, it points to a specific logging deficiency[cite: 2].
3.  [cite_start]**Inspect the 'Detailed Event Records'**: Use this table to investigate specific anomalies or to sample individual events[cite: 2]. [cite_start]Confirm that for each row, the 'event.outcome' column is populated, providing the necessary evidence for compliance[cite: 3].
attributes.title: [CCI-000134] Content of Audit Records

attributes.title: AU-3 CCI-000134 AU.L2-3.3

attributes.description: This visualization displays the health status for CCI-001048 by monitoring the highest anomaly scores from its associated Machine Learning job, which detects unusual numbers of high-severity events. A high anomaly score indicates a significant deviation from the baseline, suggesting an increased likelihood of harm.
attributes.title: [CCI-001048] Determine Likelihood and Magnitude of Harm ML

attributes.description:
**Why and when this visualization works:**
This table is the cornerstone for deep-dive analysis. After identifying a host or time of interest from the other visualizations, this table provides the granular details needed to understand the 'who, what, and where' of each potential risk event. It is essential for confirming the nature of a threat and making a final determination on the magnitude of harm.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Once you have a target for investigation (e.g., a specific host or a time range), filter the dashboard accordingly. Then, use this table to examine the specific events. Sort by 'event.severity' or '@timestamp' to focus on the most critical or recent events. The context from fields like 'user.name', 'event.action', and 'rule.name' is crucial for your final risk assessment.

query.query: event.kind:(alert OR signal) OR (event.category:(authentication OR process OR file OR network OR database OR intrusion_detection) AND event.outcome:failure)

attributes.title: [CCI-001048] Detailed Risk Assessment Events

attributes.description:
**Why and when this visualization works:**
This heatmap provides a high-level, color-coded overview of your riskiest assets. It correlates event severity (a proxy for magnitude) with event frequency (a proxy for likelihood). It's best used as a starting point to quickly identify which systems are under the most stress or are experiencing the most critical security events, helping you prioritize where to begin your risk assessment.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Look for the 'hottest' cells (darkest colors), as these represent hosts with a high count of severe events. Hover over a cell to see the exact count. Use this to identify a list of priority systems, then use the detailed table and global filters ('host.name') to investigate the specific events on those systems.

query.query: event.kind:(alert OR signal) OR (event.category:(authentication OR process OR file OR network OR database OR intrusion_detection) AND event.outcome:failure)

attributes.title: [CCI-001048] Host Risk Matrix (Severity vs. Frequency)

attributes.description:
**Why and when this visualization works:**
This trend chart specifically tracks the volume of high-severity events over time. It is essential for understanding if the likelihood of a significant incident is increasing. Use this chart to spot anomalies in your security posture, such as a sudden spike in critical alerts which could indicate an active attack, or a gradual increase which might point to a new, persistent threat.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Monitor this chart for any significant deviation from the baseline. If a spike occurs, use the time selector to zoom in on that specific period. The data from this chart, when filtered for a specific component, provides strong evidence for assessing the likelihood of harm over time.

query.query: (event.kind:(alert OR signal) OR (event.category:(authentication OR process OR file OR network OR database OR intrusion_detection) AND event.outcome:failure)) AND event.severity:(high OR critical)

attributes.title: [CCI-001048] High-Severity Event Trend

attributes.description:
Why this visualization works: It offers granular detail on each anomaly detected by the ML job, including the specific entities (host, user, process) that influenced the anomaly score. This is crucial for moving from detection to investigation, providing the necessary context to understand the 'who, what, and where' of each anomalous event.

How an analyst can use it: Sort by 'anomaly_score' to prioritize the most significant anomalies for investigation. Examine the influencer fields ('influencers.host.name', 'influencers.user.name', etc.) to understand the context of each event. Use this table to pivot to other data sources for a deeper dive into why a particular host or user is generating an unusual number of high-severity events.
attributes.title: [CCI-001048] ML - Detailed High Severity Event Anomalies

attributes.description:
This section provides visualizations to help you conduct a risk assessment for CCI-001048. The goal is to provide the underlying data to help determine the likelihood and magnitude of harm from unauthorized access, use, disclosure, disruption, modification, or destruction of a system or its information.

**How to perform a component-level compliance assessment:**
Utilize the global filters at the top of the Kibana dashboard to focus on a specific system or component (e.g., 'host.name: <your_component_name>' or 'asset.category: <your_asset_category>'). The following visualizations will help you gather evidence to make an informed judgment:
1. **Host Risk Matrix:** Use this to quickly identify which components are experiencing the most frequent and severe events. This helps prioritize where the greatest risk may lie.
2. **High-Severity Event Trend:** Monitor this chart for spikes or upward trends, which indicate an increasing likelihood of a significant incident for the filtered component(s).
3. **Detailed Risk Assessment Events:** This table provides the raw data. Once a high-risk host or time frame is identified, use this table to investigate the specific events, users, and actions involved to understand the potential magnitude of harm.

By combining these views, you can build a data-driven narrative about the risks facing each component, which is the core requirement of this control.
attributes.title: [CCI-001048] Determine Likelihood and Magnitude of Harm

attributes.title: [RA-3 CCI-001048]

attributes.description: This section provides visualizations to help you assess compliance with CCI-002088, focusing on monitoring security assessment events. This visualization displays the health status of security assessment event monitoring based on anomaly scores from the associated ML job. To perform a component-level compliance assessment, utilize the global filters at the top of the Kibana dashboard. Apply filters like 'host.name: <your_component_name>' or 'vulnerability.scanner.vendor: <vendor_name>' to focus on a specific system or scanner. Observe the trends and individual anomaly events. A high anomaly score indicates a significant deviation from the established baseline, which could signify unusual or unauthorized security assessment activity, misconfigurations, or a lack of expected assessment events.
attributes.title: [CA-7, CCI-002088] Security Assessment Events Monitoring ML

attributes.description:
**Why and When:** This table offers granular, verifiable proof that security assessments are being conducted.
This level of detail is essential for any audit, allowing you to pinpoint the exact time, target, and type of assessment.
Use it to investigate anomalies from the trend chart or to gather evidence for a specific component.

**How:** This is your primary source for detailed compliance verification.
Sort by '@timestamp' to confirm the recency of scans for a component.
Filter by `host.name` to isolate the evidence for the system you are assessing.
The `vulnerability.scanner.vendor` field can be used to confirm that approved scanning tools are being used.

query.query: (event.category:vulnerability OR event.module:(nessus OR qualys OR tenable OR lynis OR openscap OR trivy) OR vulnerability.scanner.vendor:* OR file.name:(lynis-report-* OR nessus_report* OR qualys_scan* OR openscap-report* OR trivy-report*))

attributes.title: [CA-7 / CCI-002088] Detailed Security Assessment Events

attributes.description:
This lists all of the unique host names that have had scans done on them.
This can be cross-referenced with other visualizations showing filtered lists of hosts.
In this situation the analyst should assess if the number is more or less than what the total hosts number count.
Some hosts will never be scanned and so it is expected this list to be less than the other

Other visualizations on this dashboard can be used to assess which hosts have not been scanned

query.query: (event.category:vulnerability OR event.module:(nessus OR qualys OR tenable OR lynis OR openscap OR trivy) OR vulnerability.scanner.vendor:* OR file.name:(lynis-report-* OR nessus_report* OR qualys_scan* OR openscap-report* OR trivy-report*))

attributes.title: [CA-7 / CCI-002088] Total Scanned Hosts

attributes.description:
This lists all of the unique host names found in our logs.
This can be cross-referenced with other visualizations showing filtered lists of hosts
attributes.title: [CA-7 / CCI-002088] Total Hosts

attributes.description:
**Why and When:** This chart provides a clear view of assessment coverage, helping you quickly verify if critical components are being scanned as part of the continuous monitoring program.
It's a useful tool to spot imbalances and identify any systems that might be over- or under-represented in scan activity.

**How:** Review the chart to ensure that your critical hosts are present.
If a component you are assessing is missing from this list, it may not be configured for scanning correctly.
Use the global filters to narrow the focus to a specific set of hosts to verify their individual scan status.

query.query: (event.category:vulnerability OR event.module:(nessus OR qualys OR tenable OR lynis OR openscap OR trivy) OR vulnerability.scanner.vendor:* OR file.name:(lynis-report-* OR nessus_report* OR qualys_scan* OR openscap-report* OR trivy-report*))

attributes.title: [CA-7 / CCI-002088] Assessment Coverage by Host

attributes.title: CA-7 CCI-002088

attributes.description: This visualization displays the health status of system inventory development for CCI-004328 based on anomaly scores from associated ML jobs. These jobs detect undocumented assets, rare operating systems, and unmonitored assets. A green status indicates normal inventory activity, yellow indicates a minor anomaly, and red signifies a critical issue, such as a spike in new, unknown hosts, that requires immediate investigation.
attributes.title: [CCI-004328] Develop System Inventory ML

attributes.description:
**Why this visualization works:**
It gives an immediate, high-level count of all unique systems currently sending data to Elastic. This number serves as a quick baseline for inventory validation.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Compare this number directly with the total number of assets listed in your official inventory or CMDB. A significant discrepancy is an immediate indicator that your inventory is out of date. If this number is higher than your CMDB, you have undocumented systems. If it's lower, you have unmonitored systems.
attributes.title: [CCI-004328] Total Discovered Systems

attributes.description:
This chart displays the anomaly score over time for the ML job that detects when a host unexpectedly stops sending data.

**Why this visualization works:**
It helps validate your inventory by identifying assets that have gone silent (CCI-004329). A host that stops reporting could be offline due to a failure, or its monitoring agent could have been disabled, either accidentally or maliciously.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Look for spikes in the anomaly score. Each spike represents a host (or group of hosts) that the ML model expected to see but didn't. Use the detailed table to find out which specific host stopped reporting.
attributes.title: [PM-5, CCI-004328, CCI-004329] ML - Hosts Stopped Reporting Anomaly Trend

attributes.description:
This chart shows the anomaly score over time from the ML job that identifies rare or new operating systems on the network.

**Why this visualization works:**
It automatically flags systems that don't conform to your organization's baseline OS standards, which is a key part of inventory management (CCI-004328). A rare OS could be an unauthorized device, a misconfigured system, or even a sign of a compromised host.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Monitor for any spikes in the anomaly score. A score greater than zero indicates a rare OS was detected. Investigate these spikes using the detailed anomaly table to identify the specific host and the rare OS.
attributes.title: [PM-5, CCI-004328] ML - Rare OS Anomaly Score Trend

attributes.description:
**Why this visualization works:**
This chart provides a clear breakdown of your discovered systems by their operating system. It helps you quickly understand the composition of your environment and spot anomalies, such as the appearance of an unexpected or unauthorized OS.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Regularly review this chart to ensure the OS distribution aligns with your organization's policies and expectations. If a new OS appears that is not standard for your environment, it could indicate a rogue device or a misconfigured system that requires immediate investigation. Use dashboard filters to see if specific network segments or cloud environments show different OS patterns.
attributes.title: [CCI-004328] System Activity by Operating System

attributes.description:
This section provides visualizations to help you develop and validate an inventory of organizational systems, a key requirement of CCI-004328. These visualizations leverage log data to discover systems reporting to Elastic, which can then be compared against your authoritative inventory (like a CMDB).

**How an analyst can use it:**

1.  **Component-Level Assessment:** To assess a specific part of your architecture, use the global filters at the top of the dashboard. For example, filter by `cloud.provider: 'aws'` to see only AWS systems, or `host.geo.name: 'us-east-1'` to focus on a specific region.
2.  **Inventory Validation:** The core task is to compare the systems discovered here with your official inventory. Use the 'Discovered Systems Details' table for this. Any system appearing in this dashboard that is *not* in your CMDB is an **undocumented asset** and requires investigation. Conversely, any system in your CMDB that is *not* in this dashboard is an **unmonitored asset** and needs to have logging configured.
3.  **Monitor Trends:** Observe the 'Total Discovered Systems' and 'System Activity by Operating System' visualizations over time. A sudden, unexpected increase in systems could indicate unauthorized devices on the network. A new, unauthorized OS appearing could also be a sign of a policy violation or threat.
attributes.title: [CCI-004328] Develop System Inventory
attributes.visState: {"aggs":[],"params":{"fontSize":12,"markdown":"# [CCI-004328] Develop System Inventory\n\n### Discover and validate systems on the network.","openLinksInNewTab":false},"title":"[CCI-004328] Develop System Inventory","type":"markdown"}

attributes.description:
**Why this visualization works:**
This table is the most critical tool for detailed inventory validation. It provides a comprehensive list of every system reporting to Elastic, along with key identifying information and the timestamp of the last log received.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Use this table as your primary reference for validating your official inventory. Export the data and perform a diff against your CMDB. 
- **Sort by 'Last Seen'** to identify systems that have recently stopped reporting, which could indicate a system has been decommissioned or is having agent issues.
- **Filter by `host.name`** or other attributes to investigate specific assets.
- Any system in this list but not in your CMDB is an **'undocumented asset'** and must be investigated.
- Any system in your CMDB but not on this list is an **'unmonitored asset'**.
attributes.title: [CCI-004328] Discovered Systems Details

attributes.description:
This table lists the specific anomalies for hosts that have stopped reporting to the network.

**Why this visualization works:**
It pinpoints the exact asset ('over_field_value') that has gone missing from your monitoring coverage. This is essential for maintaining an accurate and validated inventory (CCI-004329).

**How an analyst can use it:**
After seeing a spike on the trend chart, come to this table. The 'over_field_value' column will show the name of the host that stopped reporting. Your task is to investigate why this host is silent. Is it decommissioned, experiencing an outage, or has its security agent been tampered with?

Conversely, any system in your CMDB that is *not* in this dashboard is an **unmonitored asset** and needs to have logging configured.
3.  **Monitor Trends:** Observe the 'Total Discovered Systems' and 'System Activity by Operating System' visualizations over time. attributes.title: [CCI-004328]

attributes.description: This visualization displays the health status for CCI-001052 by monitoring the highest anomaly scores from its associated Machine Learning job, which detects anomalous rates of significant system changes. A high anomaly score indicates a significant deviation from the baseline, suggesting a risk assessment update is needed.
attributes.title: [CCI-001052] Risk Assessment Updates ML

attributes.description:
**Why this visualization works:** It provides the raw, granular data behind the trends. When a spike is observed in the trend chart, this table is used to see the specific events, including the host, user, process, and file involved. This level of detail is essential for the root cause analysis that informs a risk assessment. 

**How an analyst can use it:** After identifying a period of interest, use this table to investigate the individual change events. Sort by 'host.name' or 'user.name' to identify concentrated areas of activity. Examine the 'event.action', 'event.category', and 'file.path' fields to understand precisely what change occurred.

query.query: (vulnerability.id:*) OR (event.category:file AND event.type:(change OR creation OR deletion) AND file.path:(*.conf OR *.ini OR *.xml OR *.yml OR *.yaml OR /etc/* OR /boot/* OR "C:\\Windows\\System32\\config\\*")) OR (event.category:package AND event.type:(installation OR deletion)) OR (event.category:iam AND event.type:(user_creation OR user_deletion OR group_creation OR group_deletion OR policy_update OR role_change)) OR (event.category:service AND event.type:(start OR stop OR modification)) OR (signal.rule.severity:(high OR critical))

attributes.title: [CCI-001052] Detailed Systems Change Events

attributes.description:
**Why this visualization works:** 
It offers a clear, at-a-glance breakdown of the *types* of significant changes occurring. This helps an analyst quickly prioritize their investigation by showing whether the majority of changes are related to vulnerabilities, file modifications, user accounts, or package installs. 

**How an analyst can use it:** 
Observe the distribution of change categories. A large slice for 'vulnerability' or 'iam' might be more critical than 'package', depending on context. Click on a slice of the donut to filter the dashboard and see the detailed events for only that category.

query.query: (vulnerability.id:*) OR (event.category:file AND event.type:(change OR creation OR deletion) AND file.path:(*.conf OR *.ini OR *.xml OR *.yml OR *.yaml OR /etc/* OR /boot/* OR "C:\\Windows\\System32\\config\\*")) OR (event.category:package AND event.type:(installation OR deletion)) OR (event.category:iam AND event.type:(user_creation OR user_deletion OR group_creation OR group_deletion OR policy_update OR role_change)) OR (event.category:service AND event.type:(start OR stop OR modification)) OR (signal.rule.severity:(high OR critical))

attributes.title: [CCI-001052] System Change Breakdown

attributes.description:
**Why this visualization works:** 
It provides a time-series view of significant system changes, broken down by category. This allows for a quick visual assessment of when and what type of changes are occurring, helping to spot unusual spikes that might warrant a risk assessment review. 

**How an analyst can use it:** 
Look for sudden increases in the overall trend or in a specific category of change. Hover over data points to see the exact count at a given time. Use the time range selector to narrow down suspicious periods where multiple significant changes occurred.

query.query: (vulnerability.id:*) OR (event.category:file AND event.type:(change OR creation OR deletion) AND file.path:(*.conf OR *.ini OR *.xml OR *.yml OR *.yaml OR /etc/* OR /boot/* OR "C:\\Windows\\System32\\config\\*")) OR (event.category:package AND event.type:(installation OR deletion)) OR (event.category:iam AND event.type:(user_creation OR user_deletion OR group_creation OR group_deletion OR policy_update OR role_change)) OR (event.category:service AND event.type:(start OR stop OR modification)) OR (signal.rule.severity:(high OR critical))

attributes.title: [CCI-001052] Significant System Change Trend

attributes.description:
Why this visualization works: It provides the actionable details behind an anomaly score. When the trend chart shows a spike, this table provides the 'who, what, and where,' listing the specific users, processes, and file paths that the ML job identified as anomalous. This is critical for efficient incident response and compliance verification.

How an analyst can use it: When investigating an anomaly, use this table to get immediate context. Sort by 'anomaly_score' to see the most impactful events. Look at the 'influencers.file.path' to see which critical files were changed, 'influencers.user.name' to see who made the change, and 'influencers.process.name' to see how the change was made. This information is key to determining if a change was authorized.
attributes.title: [CCI-001052] ML - Detailed System Change Anomalies

attributes.description: This section provides visualizations to help identify significant system changes that should trigger a risk assessment update, addressing CCI-001052. It tracks new vulnerabilities, critical configuration modifications, software changes, and high-severity security alerts. To perform a component-level compliance assessment, utilize the global filters at the top of the Kibana dashboard. Apply filters like 'host.name: <your_component_name>' to focus on a specific system. Observe the trends and individual events in the visualizations for any spikes or anomalies. A sudden increase in any of these areas indicates a change in the system's security state, which is a key trigger for initiating a risk assessment review as per organizational policy.

attributes.title: [RA-3 CCI-001052]

attributes.description: This visualization displays the health status for CCI-001050 by monitoring the highest anomaly scores from its associated Machine Learning job, which detects unusual volumes of new security signals. A high anomaly score indicates a significant deviation from the baseline, suggesting that a risk review is warranted.
attributes.title: [CCI-001050] Review Risk Assessment Results ML

attributes.description:
**Why and when this is useful:**
This metric provides an immediate, at-a-glance summary of the current number of open security signals. It's the first place to look for a high-level snapshot of your organization's current risk posture.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Use this number as a baseline. A sudden increase in this count warrants immediate investigation. Compare this number over different review periods (e.g., daily or weekly) to understand the effectiveness of your risk mitigation efforts. A decreasing count is a positive sign, while a steadily increasing count indicates a worsening risk posture.
attributes.title: [CCI-001050] Overall Risk Score (Open Signals)

attributes.description:
**Why and when this is useful:**
This visualization is crucial for prioritizing your risk review efforts. It immediately shows which hosts or components are generating the most security alerts, indicating they may be misconfigured, more vulnerable, or actively targeted. Use this to focus your limited resources on the areas of highest risk.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Review the hosts in the largest segments of the pie chart first. Click on a segment to filter the entire dashboard by that specific host, allowing you to see its risk trend and detailed events. An unexpected host appearing in this chart is a strong signal that it requires investigation.
attributes.title: [CCI-001050] Top Risky Hosts by Open Signals

attributes.description:
**Why and when this is useful:**
This table provides the most granular view of the data used in the risk assessment. When you need to move beyond high-level trends and scores, this table gives you the specific details of each security signal. It is essential for root cause analysis and for understanding the exact nature of the risks your organization faces.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Use this table to investigate the alerts behind the numbers. Sort by '@timestamp' to see the latest risks. Filter by 'signal.rule.name' to focus on specific types of threats (e.g., 'Potential Ransomware Activity'). Examine the 'host.name', 'user.name', 'source.ip', and 'destination.ip' fields to understand the full context of an event. This is your primary tool for deep-dive investigation during your risk review.
attributes.title: [CCI-001050] Detailed Risk Assessment Events

attributes.description:
Why this visualization works: It provides a direct, contextualized list of the most significant anomalies related to new security signals. Instead of just seeing a spike on a chart, an analyst can immediately see the hosts, users, and source/destination IPs that are contributing most to the anomaly, accelerating the investigation process.

How an analyst can use it: Filter and sort the table by 'anomaly_score' to focus on the highest-risk events. Examine the influencer columns to identify patterns. For example, are many anomalous signals originating from a single 'source.ip' or targeting a specific 'host.name'? This information is critical for pinpointing the source of a new threat or a widespread detection issue.
attributes.title: [CCI-001050, CCI-004618] ML - Detailed New Security Signal Anomalies

attributes.description:
**Why and when this is useful:**
This trend chart helps you visualize your risk posture over time. It's essential for understanding whether your security efforts are reducing risk and for identifying when significant risk-related events occurred. It answers the question, 'Is our risk posture getting better or worse?'

**How an analyst can use it:**
Look for spikes in the chart, as they indicate a surge in security alerts and a potential security incident or widespread misconfiguration. Hover over the data points to see the exact count of signals at a specific time. Use the time selector in Kibana to zoom in on periods of high activity to correlate them with other events. The breakdown by severity helps you understand the nature of the risks being detected.
attributes.title: [CCI-001050] Risk Trend Over Time (converted)

attributes.description:
This section provides visualizations to help you assess compliance with CCI-001050, focusing on the periodic review of risk assessment results. These visualizations leverage real-time security signal data to provide a dynamic view of your organization's risk posture.

**How to perform a component-level compliance assessment:**

1.  Utilize the global filters at the top of the Kibana dashboard to isolate specific components or assets.
2.  Apply filters like **'host.name: <your_component_name>'** or **'cloud.instance.id: <your_instance_id>'** to focus on a single system.
3.  Observe the 'Overall Risk Score' to get an immediate snapshot of open, high-priority risks for that component.
4.  Analyze the 'Risk Trend Over Time' to determine if the component's risk posture is improving or degrading.
5.  Use the 'Top Risky Hosts' visualization to identify which systems across your environment require the most immediate attention.
6.  Drill down into the 'Detailed Risk Assessment Events' table to investigate the specific alerts contributing to the risk score, understanding the 'who, what, and where' of each potential risk.
attributes.title: [CCI-001050] Review Risk Assessment Results

attributes.title: [CCI-001050] RA-3

attributes.description: This section provides visualizations to help you assess compliance with CCI-002090, focusing on the timely triaging of security alerts. This visualization displays the health status of alert triaging based on anomaly scores from the associated ML job. To perform a component-level compliance assessment, utilize the global filters at the top of the Kibana dashboard. Apply filters like 'host.name: <your_component_name>' or 'kibana.alert.rule.name: <rule_name>' to focus on alerts from specific systems or rules. Observe the trends and individual anomaly events. A high anomaly score indicates a significant deviation from the established baseline, which could signify an increase in untriaged alerts, potential backlog issues, or a failure in the alert handling process, all of which warrant immediate investigation.
attributes.title: [CA-7, CCI-002090] Untriaged Alerts Monitoring ML

attributes.description:
**Why and When:** This visualization provides an immediate, high-level Key Performance Indicator (KPI) of the current alert triage backlog.
It is a direct measure of the organization's immediate capacity to respond to threats.
A consistently high or rising number indicates a potential compliance gap with CCI-002090.

**How:** Use this number as a daily health check to gauge the security response pipeline.
If the number is high, it suggests the response team may be overwhelmed or that the process for escalating alerts into cases is inefficient.
attributes.title: [CA-7 / CCI-002090] Total Untriaged Security Alerts

attributes.description:
**Why and When:** This chart provides a simple, percentage-based view of the overall effectiveness of the alert response process.
This is an excellent visualization for managers and compliance auditors to quickly assess procedural health without needing to dive into the technical details.

**How:** Monitor the 'Untriaged' slice of the pie.
If this slice is consistently large or growing, it's a clear visual indicator that the response workflow is not keeping pace with the volume of incoming alerts.
It helps justify the need for more resources, better alert tuning, or process improvements.
attributes.title: [CA-7 / CCI-002090] Alert Response Status (Triaged vs. Untriaged)

attributes.description:
**Why and When:** This visualization provides an immediate, high-level Key Performance Indicator (KPI) of the current alert triage backlog.
It is a direct measure of the organization's immediate capacity to respond to threats.
A consistently high or rising number indicates a potential compliance gap with CCI-002090.

**How:** Use this number as a daily health check to gauge the security response pipeline.
If the number is high, it suggests the response team may be overwhelmed or that the process for escalating alerts into cases is inefficient.
attributes.title: [CA-7 / CCI-002090] Total Triaged Security Alerts

attributes.description:
**Why this visualization works:** This visualization displays the maximum anomaly score over time for the job monitoring untriaged security alerts. It is crucial for identifying when the volume of unaddressed alerts deviates from normal, which could indicate a growing backlog, insufficient staffing, or a failure in the alert triaging process. 

**How an analyst can use it:** Look for spikes or sustained high levels in the anomaly score. This suggests an unusual number of alerts that haven't been triaged. Use the time selector to zoom into these periods and cross-reference with your alert management system to understand the types of alerts, the systems involved, and why they are not being triaged in a timely manner. This helps in assessing the effectiveness of your alert handling procedures.
attributes.title: [CA-7, CCI-002090] ML - Untriaged Alerts Anomaly Trend

attributes.description:
**Why and When:** This table offers the granular detail needed for direct action.
Analysts can see exactly which detection rules are firing, on which hosts, and when, allowing them to prioritize their response efforts effectively.
It serves as the primary work queue for alert triage.

**How:** Sort by '@timestamp' to address the newest alerts first, or sort by 'kibana.alert.rule.name' to identify noisy or frequently firing rules that may need tuning.
Clicking on the details helps an analyst begin the investigation needed to determine if an alert warrants escalation to a formal case.
attributes.title: [CA-7 / CCI-002090] Detailed Untriaged Security Alerts

attributes.title: CA-7 CCI-002090

attributes.description: This visualization displays the health status for maintaining and updating the system inventory as required by CCI-004329. The status is based on anomaly scores from ML jobs that detect new hosts appearing on the network and existing hosts that stop reporting. A green status indicates a stable and monitored inventory, yellow suggests a minor change, and red indicates a significant event, like multiple systems going offline, which could impact inventory accuracy.
attributes.title: [CCI-004329] Update System Inventory ML

attributes.description: Why and when: This trend chart helps you visualize the discovery of new hosts over time. It's useful for tracking the growth of your environment and identifying periods of significant change. A sudden, unexpected spike in new hosts could indicate unauthorized devices connecting to the network and should be investigated. How: Look for spikes in the chart. Use the time range selector to zoom in on a specific period. Correlate spikes with known deployment schedules or system provisioning activities to verify their legitimacy.
attributes.title: [CCI-004329] New Hosts Over Time (converted)

attributes.description:
This visualization displays the anomaly score over time for the ML job that detects an unusual increase in the number of unique hosts.

**Why this visualization works:**
It provides a continuous, automated check on your asset inventory baseline. A sudden spike in new hosts, flagged as an anomaly, could indicate unauthorized devices connecting to the network or a misconfiguration in asset provisioning. This is a leading indicator for maintaining inventory accuracy (CCI-004328).

**How an analyst can use it:**
Look for spikes in the anomaly score. A high score suggests a significant deviation from the norm. Use the time selector to isolate these periods and investigate the corresponding detailed anomaly table to identify the specific hosts and their characteristics that contributed to the anomaly.
attributes.title: [PM-5, CCI-004328, CCI-004329] ML- New Hosts Anomaly Score Trend

attributes.description:
This chart displays the anomaly score over time for the ML job that detects when a host unexpectedly stops sending data.

**Why this visualization works:**
It helps validate your inventory by identifying assets that have gone silent (CCI-004329). A host that stops reporting could be offline due to a failure, or its monitoring agent could have been disabled, either accidentally or maliciously.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Look for spikes in the anomaly score. Each spike represents a host (or group of hosts) that the ML model expected to see but didn't. Use the detailed table to find out which specific host stopped reporting.
attributes.title: [PM-5, CCI-004328, CCI-004329] ML - Hosts Stopped Reporting Anomaly Trend

attributes.description:
This visualization displays the anomaly score over time for the ML job that detects an unusual increase in the number of unique hosts.

**Why this visualization works:**
It provides a continuous, automated check on your asset inventory baseline. A sudden spike in new hosts, flagged as an anomaly, could indicate unauthorized devices connecting to the network or a misconfiguration in asset provisioning. This is a leading indicator for maintaining inventory accuracy (CCI-004328).

**How an analyst can use it:**
Look for spikes in the anomaly score. A high score suggests a significant deviation from the norm. Use the time selector to isolate these periods and investigate the corresponding detailed anomaly table to identify the specific hosts and their characteristics that contributed to the anomaly.
attributes.title: [PM-5, CCI-004328, CCI-004329] ML - New Hosts Anomaly Score Trend

attributes.description:
This table lists the specific anomalies detected when an unusual number of new hosts appeared on the network.

**Why this visualization works:**
It provides the granular details needed for an investigation, directly linking the anomaly to influencer fields like the OS family, cloud provider, or geographic location. This helps you quickly understand the context of the new hosts.

**How an analyst can use it:**
When the trend chart shows a spike, use this table to see the details. Sort by 'anomaly_score' to prioritize the most significant events. Examine the influencer columns to identify patterns—for example, are all new hosts from a specific cloud provider or running an unexpected OS? This information is critical for validating your asset inventory (CCI-004329).
attributes.title: [PM-5, CCI-004328, CCI-004329] ML - Detailed New Host Anomalies

attributes.description: To perform a component-level compliance assessment, compare the list of reporting hosts in these visualizations against your organization's official system inventory. Use the time range selector to define your inventory update frequency (e.g., last 30 days). Identify any hosts present in the visualizations but missing from your official inventory, and conversely, any systems in your inventory that are not reporting logs. Use filters like `host.os.family` or `cloud.provider` to segment the inventory by different criteria to aid in your review.

attributes.title: [CCI-004329]

attributes.description: This section provides visualizations to help you assess compliance with CCI-000162, focusing on protecting audit information from unauthorized access. You'll find a series of visualizations designed to highlight all access attempts (both successful and failed) on audit logs and tools. To perform a component-level compliance assessment, utilize the global filters at the top of the Kibana dashboard. Apply filters like 'host.name: <your_component_name>' to focus on a specific system. Observe the trends and individual events in the visualizations for any anomalies or failed protection mechanisms. Pay close attention to 'user.name' and 'event.outcome' to ensure only authorized users are accessing audit data and that unauthorized attempts are being properly blocked. This visualization now displays the health status of audit information protection for CCI-000162 based on the anomaly scores from associated ML jobs.
attributes.title: [CCI-000162] Protect Audit Information from Unauthorized Access ML

attributes.description:
This visualization presents a breakdown of access, modification, and deletion attempts by their outcome (success or failure) and the type of action performed. This provides a critical summary for the overall **AU-9** control, and is relevant to **CCI-000162, CCI-000163, CCI-000164, CCI-001494, CCI-001495, CCI-003831, and CCI-003832**. 

**Why this visualization works:** It offers a quick visual summary of the effectiveness of audit information protection mechanisms. A high number of successful unauthorized attempts is a clear indication of a compliance failure, while a high number of failed attempts might indicate active threats or misconfigurations that need to be addressed, directly supporting the requirements of AU-9 to protect audit information. 

**How an analyst can use it:** Prioritize investigation into successful unauthorized attempts. Analyze the distribution of failed vs. successful attempts to understand if protection mechanisms are effectively thwarting unauthorized actions. Use global filters to drill down into specific components or timeframes. This visualization provides an immediate compliance status check for AU-9, helping you quickly identify areas where audit information or tools are vulnerable to unauthorized access, modification, or deletion. Remember to use global filters to focus on a specific component (e.g., 'host.name: <your_component_name>').
attributes.title: [AU-9] Unfiltered ALL Audit Activity Outcome and Action Breakdown

attributes.description:
This visualization displays the total count of all access attempts (both successful and failed) on audit information or audit tools. This metric is foundational for monitoring compliance with **CCI-000162** (protecting audit information from unauthorized access), **CCI-000163** (monitoring audit log integrity), and **CCI-000164** (monitoring audit log and tool integrity). 

**Why this visualization works:** It provides a quick, high-level understanding of the overall activity directed at protecting audit data. A sudden or sustained increase in this number, even if most attempts are failed, warrants further investigation as it can indicate increased threat activity or misconfigurations. 

**How an analyst can use it:** Use this metric in conjunction with the trend visualization to understand if a high number of attempts is a recent spike or a persistent issue. This visualization acts as a quick health check for audit information protection. A continuously high count, even if not spiking, suggests a need for deeper investigation into your audit security. Remember that high numbers aren't always bad, but they should be explainable by authorized activity. For **CCI-000163** and **CCI-000164**, a high total count might indicate frequent integrity checks or, conversely, frequent attempts to compromise integrity.

kibanaSavedObjectMeta.searchSourceJSON:
{"query":{"query":"(
  (
    file.path : "/var/log/audit/*"
    OR file.path : "/var/log/secure"
    OR file.path : "/var/log/messages"
    OR file.path : "C:\\Windows\\System32\\winevt\\Logs\\*"
    OR file.path : "C:\\Windows\\System32\\Config\\*"
  )
  OR (event.module : (auditbeat OR winlogbeat OR filebeat OR sysmon OR osquery) AND file.extension : (evtx OR log))
)
AND (
  event.action : (file_deleted OR file_modified OR file_renamed OR file_moved OR file_created OR registry_value_deleted OR registry_value_set OR registry_key_deleted OR registry_key_created OR configuration_changed OR service_configuration_changed)
  OR event.category : (registry OR file)
)
AND NOT (
  user.name : ("NT AUTHORITY\\SYSTEM" OR "LocalSystem" OR NetworkService OR "NT AUTHORITY\\LOCAL SERVICE" OR administrator)
)
AND NOT process.name : (filebeat OR auditbeat OR metricbeat OR winlogbeat OR nxlog.exe OR rsyslogd OR systemd-journald)
","language":"kuery"},"filter":[],"indexRefName":"kibanaSavedObjectMeta.searchSourceJSON.index"}

attributes.description:
This table provides a detailed breakdown of individual events related to access attempts on audit information or audit tools. This is essential for monitoring compliance with **CCI-000162** (protecting audit information from unauthorized access), **CCI-000163** (monitoring audit log integrity), and **CCI-000164** (monitoring audit log and tool integrity). 

**Why this visualization works:** It offers granular detail on each event, including the timestamp, host, user, process, file involved, and the specific action attempted. This is crucial for in-depth investigation and identifying 'who', 'what', and 'where' regarding interactions with audit data, directly supporting the granular analysis required for compliance. 

**How an analyst can use it:** Sort by '@timestamp' to see the most recent events, or by 'user.name' to identify common sources of activity. Examine 'file.path' and 'process.name' to pinpoint specific audit resources being targeted. The 'event.outcome' field is critical for distinguishing between successful and failed attempts. For compliance, you should be able to explain any successful access by unauthorized users, or a high volume of failed attempts which might indicate active threats. For **CCI-000163** and **CCI-000164**, specifically look for any modification or deletion actions that were unauthorized. Remember to apply global filters for specific components (e.g., 'host.name: <your_component_name>') to get a targeted view.

attributes.description:
This section provides Machine Learning (ML) powered visualizations to help assess compliance with CCIs 000162, 000163, and 000164, which focus on protecting audit information and tools from unauthorized access. These visualizations analyze anomaly scores from ML jobs that detect unusual patterns in both successful and failed access attempts.

To perform a component-level compliance assessment, utilize the global filters at the top of the Kibana dashboard. Apply filters such as 'influencers.host.name: <your_component_name>' or 'influencers.user.name: <user_name>' to focus on a specific system or user. Observe the trends and individual anomaly events. A high anomaly score (e.g., 75 or above) indicates a significant deviation from the established baseline and warrants immediate investigation, as it could signify a sophisticated attempt to bypass security controls that rule-based alerts might miss.
attributes.title: [AU-9-CCIs-000162,163,164] ML - Audit Information Access Anomalies
attributes.visState: {"title":"[AU-9-CCIs-000162,163,164] ML - Audit Information Access Anomalies","type":"markdown","aggs":[],"params":{"fontSize":12,"markdown":"# [AU-9] CCIs 000162/000163/000164: Access to Audit Information (ML)\n\n### Monitor for anomalous access attempts on audit information and tools.","openLinksInNewTab":false}}

attributes.description:
This table provides a detailed breakdown of the most significant anomalies related to audit information access.

**Why this visualization works:**
It offers granular, actionable intelligence. Instead of just knowing an anomaly happened, you can see the 'who, what, and where'—the users, hosts, processes, and files that influenced the anomaly score. This is critical for rapid investigation and response.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Filter for high anomaly scores (e.g., anomaly_score >= 75) to focus on the most critical events. Sort by `influencers.user.name` or `influencers.host.name` to identify systems or accounts exhibiting the most unusual behavior. This data helps you pinpoint the source of a potential compromise or misconfiguration.

(converted)"},"panelIndex":"2bd63fe3-eb9d-4401-a94b-c041f1f196f6","gridData":{"i":"2bd63fe3-eb9d-4401-a94b-c041f1f196f6","y":72,"x":24,"w":24,"h":15}},{"type":"visualization","embeddableConfig":{"description":"This visualization displays the total count of all access attempts (both successful and failed) on audit information or audit tools. This metric is foundational for monitoring compliance with **CCI-000162** (protecting audit information from unauthorized access), **CCI-000163** (monitoring audit log integrity), and **CCI-000164** (monitoring audit log and tool integrity). 

**Why this visualization works:** It provides a quick, high-level understanding of the overall activity directed at protecting audit data. A sudden or sustained increase in this number, even if most attempts are failed, warrants further investigation as it can indicate increased threat activity or misconfigurations. 

**How an analyst can use it:** Use this metric in conjunction with the trend visualization to understand if a high number of attempts is a recent spike or a persistent issue. This visualization acts as a quick health check for audit information protection. A continuously high count, even if not spiking, suggests a need for deeper investigation into your audit security. Remember that high numbers aren't always bad, but they should be explainable by authorized activity. For **CCI-000163** and **CCI-000164**, a high total count might indicate frequent integrity checks or, conversely, frequent attempts to compromise integrity.","enhancements":{"dynamicActions":{"events":[]}},"title":"[AU-9, CCI-000162, CCI-000163, CCI-000164] Total Audit Information Access Attempts"},"panelIndex":"80bd7e9b-65ad-42c6-a74b-b3bcf8e3ccdc","gridData":{"i":"80bd7e9b-65ad-42c6-a74b-b3bcf8e3ccdc","y":57,"x":0,"w":24,"h":15}},{"type":"lens","embeddableConfig":{"enhancements":{"dynamicActions":{"events":[]}},"syncColors":false,"syncCursor":true,"syncTooltips":false,"filters":[],"query":{"language":"kuery","query":"(file.path:"/var/log/audit/*" OR file.path:"C:\Windows\System32\winevt\Logs\*" OR file.extension:(log OR evtx OR json OR xml OR conf OR cfg OR ini) OR process.name:(auditd OR wevtutil OR auditpol OR journalctl OR powershell.exe OR cmd.exe OR bash OR wmic OR sc.exe OR reg.exe OR net.exe OR taskkill.exe OR schtasks.exe OR at.exe OR psexec.exe OR mimikatz.exe OR certutil.exe OR bitsadmin.exe) OR event.module:(auditbeat OR winlogbeat OR filebeat OR sysmon OR osquery OR zeek OR suricata) OR cloud.service.name:(CloudTrail OR Azure Activity Logs OR GCP Audit Logs OR Kubernetes)) AND (event.category:(file OR process OR registry OR network OR iam) OR event.type:(access OR modification OR deletion OR creation OR configuration OR execution OR authentication OR authorization OR policy OR service_control)) AND NOT (user.name:(system OR administrator OR "NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM" OR LocalSystem OR NetworkService) AND event.outcome:success)"},"attributes":{"title":"[AU-9, CCI-000162, CCI-000163, CCI-000164] Audit Information Access Attempts Trend (converted)","description":"This visualization displays a time-series trend of all access attempts (successful and failed) on audit information or audit tools. This is crucial for monitoring compliance with **CCI-000162** (protecting audit information from unauthorized access), **CCI-000163** (monitoring audit log integrity), and **CCI-000164** (monitoring audit log and tool integrity). 

**Why this visualization works:** It provides a quick visual of spikes or consistent activity over time, allowing analysts to spot unusual patterns or potential attacks. Seeing both successful and failed attempts helps paint a complete picture of interaction with audit data, offering insights into both successful protection and attempted breaches. This trend helps you assess the overall security posture and effectiveness of your protection mechanisms for audit information over time. 

**How an analyst can use it:** Look for sudden increases in attempts, especially unexpected successful ones by unauthorized users, or a high volume of failed attempts. Hover over data points to see the exact count at a given time. Use the 'event.outcome' breakdown to understand if attempts are being successfully thwarted or if there are successful unauthorized accesses. For **CCI-000163** and **CCI-000164**, pay close attention to any successful attempts that might indicate a compromise of log or tool integrity. Remember to use the global filters at the top of the Kibana dashboard to focus on a specific component (e.g., 'host.name: visualization displays a time-series trend of *failed* attempts to access, modify, or delete audit information or audit tools. This is directly relevant to **CCI-000162** (protecting from unauthorized access), **CCI-000163** (monitoring log integrity), and **CCI-000164** (monitoring log and tool integrity), as failed attempts indicate that protection mechanisms are in place and working, but also highlight potential threats or misconfigurations. 

**Why this visualization works:** It specifically highlights events that indicate potential unauthorized access or attempted breaches of your audit protection mechanisms. Spikes or consistent failed attempts directly point to areas needing attention, providing a clear signal of ongoing challenges to your audit security. 

**How an analyst can use it:** Look for any activity in this visualization, as a perfectly compliant system should ideally have very few, if any, failed unauthorized access attempts. Spikes in this chart demand immediate investigation. Use the time range selector to narrow down suspicious periods. This visualization is a key indicator for assessing whether the protection of audit information from unauthorized access, modification, or deletion is effective and if any active threats are present against your audit infrastructure. Remember to use the global filters to focus on a specific component (e.g., 'host.name: This is essential for monitoring compliance with **CCI-000162** (protecting audit information from unauthorized access), **CCI-000163** (monitoring audit log integrity), and **CCI-000164** (monitoring audit log and tool integrity). 

**Why this visualization works:** It offers granular detail on each event, including the timestamp, host, user, process, file involved, and the specific action attempted. This is crucial for in-depth investigation and identifying 'who', 'what', and 'where' regarding interactions with audit data, directly supporting the granular analysis required for compliance. 

**How an analyst can use it:** Sort by '@timestamp' to see the most recent events, or by 'user.name' to identify common sources of activity. Examine 'file.path' and 'process.name' to pinpoint specific audit resources being targeted. The 'event.outcome' field is critical for distinguishing between successful and failed attempts. For compliance, you should be able to explain any successful access by unauthorized users, or a high volume of failed attempts which might indicate active threats. For **CCI-000163** and **CCI-000164**, specifically look for any modification or deletion actions that were unauthorized. To perform a component-level compliance assessment, utilize the global filters at the top of the Kibana dashboard. Apply filters such as 'influencers.host.name: <your_component_name>' or 'influencers.user.name: <user_name>' to focus on a specific system or user. Observe the trends and individual anomaly events. A high anomaly score (e.g., 75 or above) indicates a significant deviation from the established baseline and warrants immediate investigation, as it could signify a sophisticated attempt to bypass security controls that rule-based alerts might miss.","enhancements":{"dynamicActions":{"events":[]}},"title":"[AU-9-CCIs-000162,163,164] ML - Audit Information Access Anomalies"},"panelIndex":"288f2947-17aa-4d45-a68c-6eab803e3fea","gridData":{"i":"288f2947-17aa-4d45-a68c-6eab803e3fea","y":0,"x":0,"w":48,"h":9}},{"type":"lens","embeddableConfig":{"enhancements":{"dynamicActions":{"events":[]}},"description":"This section provides visualizations to help you assess compliance with CCI-000162, focusing on protecting audit information from unauthorized access.     file.path : "/var/log/audit/*"
    OR file.path : "/var/log/secure"
    OR file.path : "/var/log/messages"
    OR file.path : "C:\\Windows\\System32\\winevt\\Logs\\*"
    OR file.path : "C:\\Windows\\System32\\Config\\*"
  )
  OR (event.module : (auditbeat OR winlogbeat OR filebeat OR sysmon OR osquery) AND file.extension : (evtx OR log))
)
AND (
  event.action : (file_deleted OR file_modified OR file_renamed OR file_moved OR file_created OR registry_value_deleted OR registry_value_set OR registry_key_deleted OR registry_key_created OR configuration_changed OR service_configuration_changed)
  OR event.category : (registry OR file)
)
AND NOT (
  user.name : ("NT AUTHORITY\\SYSTEM" OR "LocalSystem" OR NetworkService OR "NT AUTHORITY\\LOCAL SERVICE" OR administrator)
)
AND NOT process.name : (filebeat OR auditbeat OR metricbeat OR winlogbeat OR nxlog.exe OR rsyslogd OR systemd-journald)
