attributes.title: [CCI-000162][CCI-000163][CCI-000164] CCI RMF Dashboard

attributes.description: This visualization displays the health status of central audit management for CCI-001459 based on the anomaly scores from the associated ML job. A high score indicates a host's audit configuration has drifted from the population norm, signaling a potential compliance issue.
attributes.title: [CCI-001459] Audit Generation ML

attributes.description:
This chart provides a high-level overview of how your audit agents are managed across the environment.

**Why and when this visualization works:**
It helps you quickly determine if a central management tool, like Elastic Fleet, is the primary method for agent deployment and configuration. [cite: 33] [cite_start]A system relying on central management will have a more uniform and compliant posture. [cite: 33]

**How an analyst can use it:**
1.  Observe the breakdown of agent types. [cite_start]A high percentage of `elastic-agent` strongly suggests central management via Fleet. [cite: 34]
2.  [cite_start]A significant presence of legacy agents (`auditbeat`, `winlogbeat`, etc.) may indicate that configurations are managed manually on a host-by-host basis. [cite: 35] [cite_start]While not necessarily non-compliant, manual management increases the risk of configuration drift and should be scrutinized more closely using the other visualizations. [cite: 36]

query.query: agent.type:* and agent.type:(elastic-agent or *beat or osquery)

attributes.title: [CCI-001459] Audit Agent Management Type

attributes.description:
This chart visualizes the specific audit modules enabled on each host, making it easy to spot inconsistencies.

**Why and when this visualization works:**
This is the most direct way to visually verify 'central management'. [cite: 26] If systems are managed by a central policy, their configurations should be identical. [cite_start]This chart makes any deviation immediately obvious. [cite: 26] [cite_start]Use this after confirming the basic inventory to check for configuration drift. [cite: 27]

**How an analyst can use it:**
1. [cite_start]Look for uniformity. [cite: 27] [cite_start]The colored bars representing different `event.module` values should appear in the same pattern for each host. [cite: 28]
2.  [cite_start]Identify any host that has a different pattern of colors. [cite: 29] [cite_start]This indicates its configuration does not match the baseline and has 'drifted'. [cite: 30]
3.  [cite_start]Hover over any bar segment to see the specific module and the event count. [cite: 31]
4.  [cite_start]A host with a different configuration must be investigated to determine if the change was unauthorized, as it violates the principle of central management. [cite: 32]

query.query: (event.module : (auditd or winlogbeat or sysmon or system or security) or agent.type : (auditbeat or winlogbeat or osquery or elastic-agent or filebeat) or event.dataset : (*.audit_log or *.security or \"aws.cloudtrail\" or \"azure.activitylogs\" or \"gcp.audit\" or \"k8s.audit\") or process.name : (auditd or rsyslogd or \"syslog-ng\" or nxlogd))

attributes.title: [CCI-001459] Audit Configuration Consistency

attributes.description:
**Why and When:** Use this table to investigate configuration drift anomalies flagged in the trend chart. It helps you pinpoint exactly which host has deviated from the centrally managed baseline, which is a key requirement of CCI-001459.

**How to Use:** Filter by time to focus on a specific anomaly. The `over_field_value` shows the anomalous host, and the `partition_field_value` shows the audit module that is unusual for that host. This allows you to quickly identify systems that need to be brought back into compliance with the standard configuration.
attributes.title: [AU-12, CCI-001459] ML - Detailed Config Drift Anomalies

attributes.description:
This table provides a complete inventory of all deployed audit agents and the hosts they are running on.

**Why and when this visualization works:**
This is your starting point for the compliance check. It gives you a baseline of what audit capabilities are deployed across your infrastructure. Use it to quickly identify if critical systems are missing an audit agent entirely. [cite: 15]

**How an analyst can use it:**
1.  [cite_start]Scan the `host.name` column to ensure all in-scope systems are present. [cite: 15]
2.  [cite_start]Review the `agent.type` and `event.module` columns to verify that the correct type of auditing is active on each host (e.g., `auditbeat` for Linux, `winlogbeat` for Windows). [cite: 15, 16]
3.  Filter for a specific host to drill down into its particular configuration. [cite_start]Any host that is expected to be generating audit logs but is missing from this table is a potential compliance finding. [cite: 16]

query.query: (event.module : (auditd or winlogbeat or sysmon or system or security) or agent.type : (auditbeat or winlogbeat or osquery or elastic-agent or filebeat) or event.dataset : (*.audit_log or *.security or "aws.cloudtrail" or "azure.activitylogs" or "gcp.audit" or "k8s.audit") or process.name : (auditd or rsyslogd or "syslog-ng" or nxlogd))

attributes.title: [CCI-001459] Audit Source Inventory

attributes.description:
To perform a component-level compliance assessment for CCI-001459, you must verify that the content of audit records is centrally managed. [cite: 17]

**How to Assess Compliance:**

1.  [cite_start]**Use Global Filters:** At the top of the dashboard, apply filters to narrow your focus. [cite: 18] [cite_start]To assess a specific component, use a filter like `host.name: <your_component_name>`. [cite: 19]

2.  [cite_start]**Review the Inventory:** Start with the 'Audit Source Inventory' table to confirm that every required system component is running an approved audit agent (e.g., Auditbeat, Winlogbeat, or Elastic Agent). [cite: 20]

3.  [cite_start]**Check for Consistency:** Use the 'Audit Configuration Consistency' chart to spot deviations. [cite: 21] [cite_start]If all systems are centrally managed, their configurations (represented by the colored bars) should be identical. [cite: 22] [cite_start]Any host that looks different from the baseline has likely drifted from the central policy and requires investigation. [cite: 23]

4.  [cite_start]**Verify Management Type:** The 'Audit Agent Management Type' chart shows if agents are managed by a tool like Elastic Fleet ('elastic-agent'). [cite: 24] [cite_start]A large number of legacy agents ('auditbeat', 'winlogbeat') may indicate manual, decentralized management, which could be a compliance risk. [cite: 25]

attributes.title: [AU-12 CCI-001459]

attributes.description: This visualization displays the health status of plan protection for CCI-003063 based on anomaly scores from the associated ML job. It helps assess compliance with PL-2e by highlighting unusual patterns in unauthorized access attempts on security and system plan repositories. A high anomaly score warrants immediate investigation.
attributes.title: [PL-2e, CCI-003063] Plan Protection ML

attributes.description:
This visualization displays a time-series trend of unauthorized attempts to access security and system plans. This includes both successful and failed attempts, providing insight into potential threats and the effectiveness of access controls.

**Why this visualization works:** It provides a quick visual of spikes or consistent unauthorized access attempts over time. Identifying these trends helps in proactively addressing vulnerabilities, whether they are from external threats or internal misconfigurations.

**How an analyst can use it:** Look for sudden increases in access attempts, which could signify a targeted effort to obtain sensitive plans. Hover over data points to see the exact count at a given time and use the time range selector to narrow down suspicious periods. This trend is a key indicator of how well your plan repositories are protected.

query.query: ((file.path:(*\\plans\\* OR *\\policies\\* OR *\\procedures\\* OR *\\baselines\\* OR *\\diagrams\\*) OR file.name:(*plan* OR *policy* OR *procedure* OR *guidance* OR *baseline*)) AND file.extension:(docx OR pdf OR xlsx OR md OR txt OR vsd OR zip)) AND event.category:file AND event.type:access AND NOT user.name:(system OR administrator OR "NT AUTHORITY\\SYSTEM" OR *_admin OR authorized_plan_editor)

attributes.title: [CCI-003063] Unauthorized Plan Access Attempts Trend (converted)

attributes.description:
This table provides a detailed breakdown of individual events related to unauthorized access attempts on security and system plans. It offers the granular data needed for in-depth investigation of any suspicious activity.

**Why this visualization works:** It presents the critical details for each access event: who, what, where, and when. This allows an analyst to move from high-level trend analysis to specific event investigation, which is essential for forensic analysis and compliance verification.

**How an analyst can use it:** Sort by '@timestamp' to review the most recent events. Sort by 'user.name' to identify accounts with repeated unauthorized access attempts. Examine 'file.path' to see which specific plans are being targeted. The 'event.outcome' column is crucial for differentiating between failed attempts (blocked) and successful unauthorized access (a breach).

attributes.description:
This visualization displays the breakdown of unauthorized plan access attempts by user account. It helps to quickly identify which users are most frequently associated with these security events.

**Why this visualization works:** It immediately highlights potential problem accounts, whether they are compromised user accounts, misconfigured service accounts, or insiders attempting to gain unauthorized access. Visualizing the distribution makes outliers obvious.

**How an analyst can use it:** Look for any unexpected user names in this chart. An authorized user should not appear here. If they do, it suggests a flaw in the exclusion logic of the query or a real security event. A high count from a single user is a strong signal for targeted investigation of that user's activity.

attributes.description:
This table provides a detailed breakdown of individual anomalies detected by the ML job for unauthorized plan access. It lists the specific events that were flagged as unusual.

**Why and when this visualization works:**
This view is essential for investigating the 'who, what, and where' behind an anomaly. After identifying a spike in the trend chart, use this table to see the granular details, such as the user, host, and file path that influenced the anomaly score.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Filter this table to the time range of a high-score anomaly. Sort by 'anomaly_score' to prioritize the most significant events. Examine the 'summary' and influencer fields ('influencers.user.name', 'influencers.host.name', 'influencers.file.path') to understand why the activity was flagged. This information is critical for determining if the anomaly represents a misconfiguration, a policy violation, or a potential threat.

attributes.description: This section provides visualizations to help you assess compliance with CCI-003063 (PL-2e), focusing on the protection of system and security plans from unauthorized disclosure. You'll find a series of visualizations designed to highlight unauthorized access attempts on the repositories where these plans are stored. To perform a component-level compliance assessment, utilize the global filters at the top of the Kibana dashboard. Apply filters like 'host.name: <your_component_name>' to focus on a specific system or file repository. Observe the trends and individual events in the visualizations for any anomalies or unauthorized access. A high number of failed attempts could indicate active threats or misconfigurations, while any successful unauthorized access requires immediate investigation.

attributes.title: [PL-2 CCI-003063

attributes.description: This visualization displays the health status of Information Privacy Measures of Performance monitoring for CCI-004336. The status is determined by the maximum anomaly score from an associated Machine Learning job that detects anomalous access patterns to sensitive data. A high score indicates a significant deviation from the baseline, suggesting potential privacy risks or unauthorized access that requires investigation.

05f3d081-41c1-463b-8a78-e6b1bc6b29c6.label: [CCI-004336] Monitor Information Privacy Measures of Performance

attributes.title: [CCI-004336] Monitor Information Privacy Measures of Performance ML

attributes.description:
This visualization displays the maximum anomaly score over time for the job monitoring access to sensitive data.

**Why this visualization works:** It excels at finding insider threats or compromised accounts by focusing on behavior. A rule might permit an HR employee to access sensitive files, but the ML job will flag when that employee starts accessing thousands of files an hour, or accesses files they've never touched before, both of which are highly anomalous behaviors.

**How an analyst can use it:** Any spike in this chart, especially a high-severity one, is a priority for investigation. Use the time selector to isolate the event and then examine the 'Top Anomaly Influencers' table to see exactly which user is responsible, what data they were accessing, and from which host.
attributes.title: [PM-6, CCI-004336] ML - Sensitive Data Access Anomaly Score Trend

attributes.description:
This table provides a detailed breakdown of the entities influencing the anomaly scores for sensitive data access.

**Why this visualization works:** It provides the critical details needed to validate access to sensitive information. For any given anomaly, this table will show the user, the host they connected from, the specific file they accessed, and whether that access succeeded or failed. This is precisely the information needed to verify if the access was authorized.

**How an analyst can use it:** When an anomaly appears on the trend chart, review this table to understand its context. A high anomaly score for a user who rarely accesses sensitive data, combined with a 'success' outcome on a sensitive file path, requires immediate follow-up with the user or their manager to confirm the legitimacy of the access.
attributes.title: [PM-6, CCI-004336] ML - Top Sensitive Data Access Anomaly Influencers

attributes.description:
This visualization displays a time-series trend of access events related to files and data sources that are tagged as sensitive or contain Personally Identifiable Information (PII).

**Why and when this visualization works:**
This chart is your first stop for understanding the rhythm of sensitive data access in your environment. It's most effective for establishing a baseline of normal activity and quickly spotting deviations. A sudden spike in access, especially failed attempts, can be an early indicator of a data breach attempt, insider threat, or a misconfigured application scanning files it shouldn't.

**How an analyst can use it:**
- **Monitor the baseline:** Look for significant deviations from the established normal pattern.
- **Investigate spikes:** If you see a spike, use the time range selector to zoom in on that period. Cross-reference the time of the spike with the 'Detailed Privacy-Related Events' table to see the specific users, hosts, and files involved.
- **Analyze outcomes:** The breakdown by 'success' and 'failure' helps you understand if your access controls are working. A high number of failures might indicate that controls are effective but are being heavily tested.

query.query: (tags: "PII" OR data.classification: confidential OR file.path: (*sensitive* OR *confidential* OR *pii*)) AND event.category: file AND event.type: access

attributes.title: [CCI-004336] Sensitive Data Access Trend (converted)

attributes.description:
This visualization provides a quick, high-level count of all alerts generated by your Data Loss Prevention (DLP) solution. 

**Why and when this visualization works:**
This metric serves as a direct measure of performance for your DLP policy enforcement. It is most useful for getting an immediate sense of how often data handling policies are being violated. A consistently high or rising number indicates a potential need for user training, policy tuning, or investigation into systemic issues.

**How an analyst can use it:**
- **Track Overall Violations:** Use this number as a key performance indicator (KPI) for your privacy program. Report on it regularly to management.
- **Correlate with Trends:** If this number seems high, compare it with the 'Sensitive Data Access Trend' to see if there's a broader pattern of unusual data handling.
- **Trigger Deeper Dives:** A high number here should prompt you to investigate the 'Detailed Privacy-Related Events' table, filtering for `event.module: dlp`, to understand the nature of the alerts.
attributes.title: [CCI-004336] Total DLP Alerts

attributes.description:
This chart shows the top users interacting with data that has been identified as sensitive or PII. 

**Why and when this visualization works:**
This is a powerful tool for auditing access controls. It helps you instantly see if access patterns align with your organization's policies and user roles. It's best used to answer the question, 'Are the people accessing our most sensitive data the ones who are supposed to be?' Any unexpected names on this list, especially in the top positions, are a significant finding that requires immediate investigation.

**How an analyst can use it:**
- **Regularly Audit Top Users:** Review this chart periodically to ensure the list of users aligns with authorized personnel for each data category.
- **Investigate Anomalies:** If an unfamiliar user or a service account appears, it could indicate a compromised account or misconfigured permissions. Use the filter to select that user and pivot to the detailed event table to see exactly what they are doing.
- **Filter by Component:** Use global filters (e.g., for a specific application's host) to see who is accessing sensitive data on that particular component.

query.query: (tags: "PII" OR data.classification: confidential OR file.path: (*sensitive* OR *confidential* OR *pii*)) AND event.category: file AND event.type: access

attributes.title: [CCI-004336] Top Users Accessing Sensitive Data

attributes.description:
This table provides the granular, event-level details for any activity that meets the privacy monitoring criteria. This is the ultimate source of truth for any investigation.

**Why and when this visualization works:**
This view is essential for forensic analysis. When a high-level chart or metric indicates a potential issue, this table allows you to see the raw data: the who, what, where, and when of each specific event. It is the primary tool for confirming a privacy incident and gathering evidence.

**How an analyst can use it:**
- **Drill Down:** After identifying a spike in a trend or an anomalous user, filter this table by the relevant time, user name, or host name to see the specific actions they performed.
- **Search for Keywords:** Use the search bar to look for specific file names, user IDs, or hosts to conduct targeted investigations.
- **Export for Reporting:** The data in this table can be exported to provide detailed evidence for incident reports or compliance audits. The columns provide a comprehensive view of the event, from the user and process involved to the specific file path or network destination.

query.query: (tags: "PII" OR data.classification: confidential OR file.path: (*sensitive* OR *confidential* OR *pii*) OR event.module: dlp)

attributes.title: [CCI-004336] Detailed Privacy-Related Events

attributes.description:
This section provides visualizations to help you assess compliance with CCI-004336 by monitoring information privacy measures of performance. You'll find visualizations designed to track access to sensitive data, monitor Data Loss Prevention (DLP) alerts, and identify user activity patterns involving personally identifiable information (PII).

**How to Perform a Component-Level Compliance Assessment:**
To assess a specific component, use the global filters at the top of the dashboard. For example, apply filters like 'host.name: <your_component_name>' or 'data_stream.dataset: <your_component_dataset>' to narrow the focus.

Observe the trends and metrics to answer key performance questions:
1.  **Is access to sensitive data increasing or spiking unexpectedly?** Use the 'Sensitive Data Access Trend' to monitor this.
2.  **Are the right people accessing sensitive data?** The 'Top Users Accessing Sensitive Data' chart helps verify that access aligns with job roles.
3.  **Are our automated privacy policies (like DLP) effective?** The 'Total DLP Alerts' metric provides a direct count of policy violations.
4.  **What are the specific details of a potential privacy incident?** The 'Detailed Privacy-Related Events' table allows for in-depth investigation.

These visualizations rely on your data being properly tagged (e.g., using a 'PII' tag) or located in known sensitive paths. You must customize the queries in these visualizations to match your organization's specific definition of sensitive data.

attributes.title: [PM-6 CCI-004336]

attributes.description: This section provides visualizations to help you assess compliance with CCI-000167, which focuses on retaining audit records for a defined period. This visualization displays the health status of audit record retention for CCI-000167 based on the anomaly scores from associated ML jobs.
attributes.title: [CCI-000167] Audit Record Retention ML

attributes.description: Tag for RMF Control AU-11: Audit Record Retention
attributes.name: au-11

attributes.description:
This visualization displays the maximum anomaly score over time for the job monitoring audit record ingestion rates. 

**Why this visualization works:** The ML job `au-11-cci-000167-ingestion-rate-job` is designed to learn the normal patterns of log ingestion. A spike in the anomaly score indicates an unusual change in the volume or source of ingested audit data. This could mean a log shipper has failed, a new data source has appeared, or an attacker is attempting to flood the system with data to obscure malicious activity. Rule-based alerts might miss these subtle deviations, but the ML job will flag them for investigation.

**How an analyst can use it:** Look for any spikes in the anomaly score trend. A high score (e.g., 75 or above) warrants immediate investigation. Use the time range selector to narrow down the period of the anomaly. The `influencers` table below will help you identify the specific `host.name`, `event.dataset`, and `agent.type` associated with the anomalous behavior. This helps you quickly pinpoint the source of the ingestion issue to ensure compliance with CCI-000167.
attributes.title: [AU-11, CCI-000167] ML - Audit Ingestion Rate Anomaly Score Trend

attributes.description:
This visualization displays the maximum anomaly score over time for the job monitoring audit record retention. 

**Why this visualization works:** The ML job `au-11-cci-000167-oldest-record-job` establishes a baseline for the number of older audit records over time. An anomaly in this trend, particularly a sudden drop in the `low_count` of older records, is a direct indicator of a potential retention issue or premature deletion of audit logs. This is a critical check for CCI-000167, which requires that audit records are retained for a specific period. The ML model is particularly useful for detecting subtle, non-rule-based deletions or data purging events.

**How an analyst can use it:** Monitor for any spikes in the anomaly score. A high score suggests that an unusual number of older audit records have been removed or are no longer being ingested, which is a compliance failure. Use the time selector to investigate the specific period of the anomaly. Use the `influencers` table below to identify which `host.name` or `event.dataset` is responsible for the anomaly. This will help you determine if an authorized retention policy was executed or if there was an unauthorized deletion of records.
attributes.title: [AU-11, CCI-000167] ML - Audit Retention Anomaly Score Trend (converted)

attributes.description:
This table provides a retention breakdown for each log source, showing the oldest and newest record for each one.

**Why this visualization works:**
It offers granular proof of retention across your entire technology stack. [cite: 10] [cite_start]A single 'Oldest Record' metric might be skewed by one long-lived data source, while this table ensures that all sources are meeting compliance mandates individually.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Sort the table by the 'Oldest Record' column. [cite: 11] [cite_start]Look for data sources with an oldest record date that is much more recent than others. [cite: 12] [cite_start]This is a red flag indicating that the retention policy for that specific data source may not be working correctly or that the source stopped sending logs for a period. [cite: 13] This is the most effective way to find component-level retention failures.

query.query: event.kind: (*event* or *alert* or *signal* or *metric* or *state*) or event.category: (*authentication* or *configuration* or *database* or *driver* or *file* or *host* or *iam* or *intrusion_detection* or *malware* or *network* or *package* or *process* or *registry* or *session* or *threat* or *web*) or event.module: * or event.dataset: * or winlog.event_id: * or host.os.type: (*linux* or *macos* or *unix* or *windows*) or cloud.provider: *

attributes.title: [CCI-000167] Audit Record Retention by Data Source

attributes.description:
This metric displays the timestamp of the oldest audit record found within the selected time range and data set.

**Why this visualization works:**
It provides an immediate, definitive starting point for your data retention period. [cite: 4] [cite_start]This date is the most critical piece of evidence for verifying long-term retention.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Compare this date to the current date to calculate the total retention duration. [cite: 5] [cite_start]For example, if today is January 1, 2025, and the oldest record is from January 1, 2024, you have at least 365 days of retention. [cite: 6] [cite_start]This should be checked against your organization's specific retention policy. [cite: 7] If the oldest record is more recent than your policy dictates, it signals a potential compliance gap.

attributes.description:
This metric displays the timestamp of the most recent audit record found within the selected time range and data set.

**Why this visualization works:**
It confirms that log ingestion is currently active and provides a definitive end-point for the retention period calculation.

**How an analyst can use it:**
This date should be very close to the current time. [cite: 8] [cite_start]If the newest record is several hours or days old, it may indicate an issue with log collection agents or the ingestion pipeline for the selected component. [cite: 9] It confirms the system is 'live' and that data is being collected up to the present.

attributes.title: AU-11 CCI-000167

attributes.description: This visualization displays the health status of CCI-001487 compliance based on anomaly scores from the associated ML job. High scores may indicate events missing organization-defined required fields.
attributes.title: [CCI-001487] Content of Audit Records ML

attributes.description:
This table provides a detailed, event-by-event list of all audit records that are missing required, organization-defined fields.

**Why and When This is Used:**
This is the most critical tool for investigation. When the metric or trend visualizations indicate a problem, this table provides the ground truth. It allows for a granular review of each non-compliant event to understand exactly what happened and what information is missing.

**How an Analyst Can Use It:**
Review the entries in this table to identify patterns. Is a particular host, user, or event type consistently failing to log the required data? Sort by columns like `host.name` or `event.action` to pinpoint the source of the compliance gap. The data here is essential for creating trouble tickets and directing remediation efforts.

query.query: ((event.category:authentication OR event.type:access) AND (NOT source.ip:* OR NOT user.name:* OR NOT host.name:*)) OR ((event.category:process AND event.type:(start OR creation)) AND (NOT process.executable:* OR NOT process.command_line:* OR NOT user.name:*)) OR ((event.category:file AND event.type:(creation OR modification OR deletion)) AND (NOT file.path:* OR NOT file.name:* OR NOT user.name:*)) OR ((event.category:network AND event.type:connection) AND (NOT source.ip:* OR NOT destination.ip:* OR NOT destination.port:*)) OR (event.category:registry AND (NOT registry.path:* OR NOT user.name:*)) OR ((event.module:*aws AND event.dataset:*cloudtrail) AND (NOT aws.cloudtrail.event_name:* OR NOT aws.cloudtrail.user_identity.arn:*)) OR ((event.category:iam AND event.type:(user_creation OR group_creation OR policy)) AND (NOT user.name:* OR NOT user.target.name:*))

attributes.title: [CCI-001487] Detailed Events Missing Required Fields Table

attributes.description:
**Why and When:** This table is essential for diagnosing the root cause of CCI-001487 anomalies. It breaks down the anomalies by host and event category, showing you exactly where your logging pipeline is failing to capture required data fields.

**How to Use:** Look for entries with high anomaly scores. The `host.name` and `event.category` fields pinpoint the source of the incomplete logs. This allows you to review the data shippers and parsing configurations for that data type and host to ensure all required fields are being populated correctly according to your organization's policy.
attributes.title: [AU-3, CCI-001487] ML - Hosts Missing Required Fields Chart

attributes.description:
This visualization displays a time-series trend of audit records that are missing required fields, broken down by the host.

**Why and When This is Used:**
This chart is essential for understanding the scope and timeline of data content issues. A sudden spike might indicate a recent misconfiguration on a specific host, while a persistent low-level count could signal a systemic, long-term problem].

**How an Analyst Can Use It:**
Look for any spikes or consistent counts above zero. Hover over the chart to see which hosts (`host.name`) are responsible for the non-compliant records. Use the time selector to isolate periods of interest and then move to the detailed table to investigate the specific events from that time and host.

query.query: ((event.category:authentication OR event.type:access) AND (NOT source.ip:* OR NOT user.name:* OR NOT host.name:*)) OR ((event.category:process AND event.type:(start OR creation)) AND (NOT process.executable:* OR NOT process.command_line:* OR NOT user.name:*)) OR ((event.category:file AND event.type:(creation OR modification OR deletion)) AND (NOT file.path:* OR NOT file.name:* OR NOT user.name:*)) OR ((event.category:network AND event.type:connection) AND (NOT source.ip:* OR NOT destination.ip:* OR NOT destination.port:*)) OR (event.category:registry AND (NOT registry.path:* OR NOT user.name:*)) OR ((event.module:*aws AND event.dataset:*cloudtrail) AND (NOT aws.cloudtrail.event_name:* OR NOT aws.cloudtrail.user_identity.arn:*)) OR ((event.category:iam AND event.type:(user_creation OR group_creation OR policy)) AND (NOT user.name:* OR NOT user.target.name:*))

attributes.title: [CCI-001487] Trend of Records Missing Required Fields by Host (converted)

attributes.description:
This visualization shows the total number of audit records that are missing one or more of your organization's required fields. 

**Why and When This is Used:**
This metric serves as an immediate health check. It provides a simple, at-a-glance confirmation of whether there are any records with incomplete data. A compliant system should show a '0'.

**How an Analyst Can Use It:**
If this number is greater than zero, it confirms a compliance deviation. Use this as a starting point to dive into the trend and detailed table visualizations to understand the scope and specifics of the missing data. Remember to customize the query for this visualization to match your organization's specific field requirements.

query.query: (event.category:authentication AND NOT source.ip:*) OR (event.category:process AND NOT process.command_line:*) OR (event.category:file AND NOT file.path:*)

attributes.title: [CCI-001487] Total Records Missing Required Fields Number

attributes.description:
This section is designed to help you verify compliance with CCI-001487, which requires that audit records contain specific, organization-defined information. [cite_start]The visualizations below are templates that you must customize to match your organization's auditing policy[cite: 11].

**How to Perform a Component-Level Compliance Assessment:**

1. [cite_start]**Identify Required Fields:** First, consult your organization's policy to determine the mandatory fields for different event types (e.g., for authentication events, are 'source.ip' and 'user.agent.original' required?)[cite: 12].

2. **Customize Visualizations:** Edit each visualization in this section. [cite_start]In the search bar for each visualization, modify the KQL query to look for events that are *missing* your required fields[cite: 13]. For example, to check if authentication or process creation events are missing 'source.ip' or 'process.command_line' respectively, your query would look like this:

    `(event.category:authentication AND NOT source.ip:*) OR (event.category:process AND event.action:start AND NOT process.command_line:*)`

3. [cite_start]**Filter by Component:** Use the main dashboard filters to narrow the scope to a specific component you are assessing (e.g., `host.name: <your_component_name>`)[cite: 15].

4. [cite_start]**Analyze the Results:**
    * **Total Missing Fields:** This metric should be zero[cite: 16]. [cite_start]Any count greater than zero indicates a non-compliant record [cite: 17][cite_start].
    * **Missing Fields Trend:** This chart helps you see if the issue is ongoing or a recent problem [cite: 17][cite_start].
    * **Detailed Events Table:** This table lists the specific records that are missing fields, allowing for direct investigation and remediation[cite: 17].
attributes.title: [CCI-001487] Content of Audit Records

3. **Filter by Component:** Use the main dashboard filters to narrow the scope to a specific component you are assessing (e.g., `host.name: <your_component_name>`).

4. **Analyze the Results:**
    * **Total Missing Fields:** This metric should be zero. Any count greater than zero indicates a non-compliant record.
    * **Missing Fields Trend:** This chart helps you see if the issue is ongoing or a recent problem.
    * **Detailed Events Table:** This table lists the specific records that are missing fields, allowing for direct investigation and remediation.","enhancements":{"dynamicActions":{"events":[]}},"title":"[CCI-001487] [AU.L2-3.3] Content of Audit Records"},"panelIndex":"d8d14935-d54e-462c-86d1-e93647b45ded","gridData":{"i":"d8d14935-d54e-462c-86d1-e93647b45ded","y":0,"x":0,"w":48,"h":8}},{"type":"lens","embeddableConfig":{"enhancements":{"dynamicActions":{"events":[]}},"syncColors":false,"syncCursor":true,"syncTooltips":false,"filters":[],"query":{"language":"kuery","query":"(event.category:authentication AND NOT source.ip:*) OR (event.category:process AND NOT process.command_line:*) OR (event.category:file AND NOT file.path:*)"},"savedObjectId":"4ca63876-700d-48cb-a29b-5af6b878d69b"},"panelIndex":"7d0aa1f5-f788-4b43-8cb8-565b62e38561","gridData":{"i":"7d0aa1f5-f788-4b43-8cb8-565b62e38561","y":19,"x":24,"w":24,"h":15}},{"type":"lens","embeddableConfig":{"enhancements":{"dynamicActions":{"events":[]}},"syncColors":false,"syncCursor":true,"syncTooltips":false,"filters":[],"query":{"language":"kuery","query":"job_id: "au-3-cci-001487-missing-required-fields-trend""},"attributes":{"title":"[AU-3, CCI-001487] ML - Missing Required Fields Anomaly Trend (converted)","description":"**Why and When:** This chart supports CCI-001487 by detecting anomalous increases in events that are missing organization-defined required fields. attributes.title: [AU-3 CCI-001487] [AU.L2-3.3]

attributes.description: This visualization displays the health status for CCI-003064 based on anomaly scores from the associated ML job. It helps assess compliance with PL-2 by highlighting unusual patterns in unauthorized modification attempts on critical plan documents. A high anomaly score warrants immediate investigation.

79463777-ee43-4b04-94c2-5d15361b7022.label: [PL-2, CCI-003064] Protect Plans from Unauthorized Modification

attributes.title: [PL-2, CCI-003064] Protect Plans from Unauthorized Modification ML

attributes.description:
This visualization displays a time-series trend of attempts to modify critical plan documents.

**Why this visualization works:**
It provides a quick visual of spikes or consistent attempts to modify plans over time, which can indicate an ongoing attack, a misconfiguration, or a policy violation. Identifying these trends allows for proactive intervention and helps assess the effectiveness of your integrity controls.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Look for sudden increases in modification events or a consistently high baseline. Hover over data points to see the exact count at a given time. Use the time range selector to narrow down suspicious periods. This helps you understand if your plan protection mechanisms are being consistently challenged. Pay close attention to events where 'user.name' is not an authorized administrator or service account.

query.query: (file.path:("*\plans\*" OR "*\documents\*" OR "*.plan" OR "*.conf" OR "*.xml" OR "*.yml" OR "*.ini") OR file.extension:("plan" OR "conf" OR "xml" OR "yml" OR "ini")) AND event.type:("change" OR "modification" OR "deletion" OR "renaming" OR "permission_change") AND NOT (user.name:(*system* OR *administrator* OR "NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM" OR *authorized_admin*) AND event.outcome:success)

attributes.title: [PL-2, CCI-003064] Unauthorized Plan Modification Trend (converted)

attributes.description:
This visualization displays the total count of instances where unauthorized modification attempts were made on plan documents.

**Why this visualization works:**
It provides a quick, high-level understanding of the overall unauthorized activity directed at critical plans. A non-zero count here immediately flags a potential compliance issue or a security incident that requires investigation.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Use this metric in conjunction with the trend visualization to understand if a high number of unauthorized attempts is a recent spike or a persistent issue. This visualization acts as a quick health check for the modification controls on your plan documents. A continuously high count suggests a need for deeper investigation into why unauthorized users or processes are attempting to modify these files.

query.query: (file.path:("*\plans\*" OR "*\documents\*" OR "*.plan" OR "*.conf" OR "*.xml" OR "*.yml" OR "*.ini") OR file.extension:("plan" OR "conf" OR "xml" OR "yml" OR "ini")) AND event.type:("change" OR "modification" OR "deletion" OR "renaming" OR "permission_change") AND NOT (user.name:(*system* OR *administrator* OR "NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM" OR *authorized_admin*) AND event.outcome:success)

attributes.title: [PL-2, CCI-003064] Total Unauthorized Plan Modifications

attributes.description:
This table provides a detailed breakdown of individual events related to unauthorized modifications of critical plan documents.

**Why this visualization works:**
It offers granular detail on each event, including the user, process, and file involved, and the specific action attempted (e.g., modification, deletion, permission change). This level of detail is crucial for in-depth investigation and pinpointing exact policy violations and security incidents.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Sort by 'user.name' or 'process.name' to identify common sources of unauthorized activity. Examine 'file.path' to pinpoint specific plan documents being targeted. The 'event.action' field will show precisely what action was attempted. This table is critical for understanding the 'who, what, and where' of potential plan integrity failures and is essential for any resulting investigation.

query.query: (file.path:("*\plans\*" OR "*\documents\*" OR "*.plan" OR "*.conf" OR "*.xml" OR "*.yml" OR "*.ini") OR file.extension:("plan" OR "conf" OR "xml" OR "yml" OR "ini")) AND event.type:("change" OR "modification" OR "deletion" OR "renaming" OR "permission_change") AND NOT (user.name:(*system* OR *administrator* OR "NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM" OR *authorized_admin*) AND event.outcome:success)

attributes.title: [PL-2, CCI-003064] Detailed Unauthorized Plan Modification Events

attributes.description:
This table provides a detailed breakdown of individual anomalies detected by the ML job for unauthorized plan modifications. It lists the specific events that were flagged as unusual.

**Why and when this visualization works:**
This view is essential for investigating the specifics of a modification anomaly. When the trend chart shows a spike, this table provides the necessary context to determine the event's legitimacy. Understanding which user, process, and host were involved is key to verifying compliance.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Filter the time range to focus on a specific anomaly. Sort by 'anomaly_score' to see the most critical events first. Review the influencer fields ('influencers.user.name', 'influencers.process.name', 'influencers.file.path') to determine the source of the modification. Cross-reference this information with change management records to confirm if the activity was authorized. Unexplained anomalies must be investigated as potential security incidents.
attributes.title: [PL-2, CCI-003064] ML - Detailed Plan Modification Anomalies

attributes.description: This section provides visualizations to help you assess compliance with CCI-003064, focusing on protecting critical plans from unauthorized modification. You'll find visualizations designed to highlight any attempts to alter plan documents, configurations, or permissions. To perform a component-level compliance assessment, utilize the global filters at the top of the Kibana dashboard. Apply filters like 'host.name: <your_component_name>' to focus on a specific system. Observe the trends and individual events in the visualizations for any anomalies or unauthorized changes. Any modification event not performed by an explicitly authorized user or process should be investigated thoroughly, as this control is critical for maintaining the integrity of operational and strategic plans.

attributes.title: [CCI-003064]

attributes.description: This visualization displays the health status of Information Privacy Measures of Performance reporting for CCI-004337. The status is determined by the maximum anomaly score from an associated Machine Learning job that detects unusual increases in privacy-related incidents. A high score indicates a significant deviation from the baseline, suggesting potential privacy events that require investigation and reporting.

c4f0d639-813f-4433-9d1a-53de59dd14bc.label: [CCI-004337] Report Information Privacy Measures of Performance

attributes.title: [CCI-004337] Report Information Privacy Measures of Performance ML (converted)

attributes.description:
This table provides a detailed breakdown of the entities influencing the anomaly scores for privacy-related incidents.

**Why this visualization works:** It provides immediate, actionable context for privacy-related anomalies. Instead of just an alert, it shows the specific users accessing sensitive file paths on particular hosts, which is critical for a fast and effective response to a potential data breach or policy violation.

**How an analyst can use it:** When an anomaly is detected, this table should be the first place to look. Identify the user, host, and especially the file path involved. A high anomaly score linked to an unfamiliar user accessing a file path known to contain PII is a critical event that requires immediate escalation and investigation to determine if a reportable privacy incident has occurred.
attributes.title: [PM-6, CCI-004337] ML - Top Privacy Incident Anomaly Influencers

attributes.description:
**Why and when this visualization works:**
This table provides the detailed, granular evidence required for any credible performance report. It moves beyond metrics to show the specific events that constitute the 'measures of performance.' This is where you can prove compliance by showing exactly what was detected.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Include this table in an appendix or as a drill-down option in your report. It allows stakeholders to see the 'who, what, and when' of each privacy incident. You can sort by any column to identify repeat issues, such as a specific user who frequently triggers DLP rules or a particular file path that is a common source of data exposure. This detail is essential for root cause analysis and for validating the effectiveness of your privacy controls.

query.query: (event.category:(file OR database OR network) AND (file.path:(*pii* OR *phi* OR *ssn* OR *financial*) OR rule.name:*privacy* OR event.code:*privacy_violation*)) OR (data_stream.dataset:*dlp*)

attributes.title: [CCI-004337] Detailed Privacy Incidents

attributes.description:
**Why and when this visualization works:**
This trend chart is essential for adding context to performance reports. Instead of just a single number, it shows the pattern of privacy incidents over time, helping to distinguish between a single anomalous spike and a persistent, ongoing problem. This is vital for demonstrating continuous monitoring and the effectiveness of responses.

**How an analyst can use it:**
In your report, use this chart to illustrate the narrative. Look for spikes and correlate them with known events (e.g., new deployments, policy changes). A downward trend demonstrates improving performance, while an upward trend signals a need for investigation and potential policy reinforcement. Use the time range selector to focus on periods of interest for more detailed reporting.

query.query: (event.category:(file OR database OR network) AND (file.path:(*pii* OR *phi* OR *ssn* OR *financial*) OR rule.name:*privacy* OR event.code:*privacy_violation*)) OR (data_stream.dataset:*dlp*)

attributes.title: [CCI-004337] Privacy Incident Trend (converted)

attributes.description:
**Why and when this visualization works:**
This metric provides a crucial at-a-glance summary for any performance report. It shows the total volume of detected privacy-related incidents over the selected time frame, acting as the primary Key Performance Indicator (KPI) for privacy compliance.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Use this number as the headline figure in your reports. A high or unexpectedly increasing number should trigger a deeper dive using the trend and detailed table visualizations to understand the context and formulate a response. A low or zero count is a strong indicator of effective privacy controls.

query.query: (event.category:(file OR database OR network) AND (file.path:(*pii* OR *phi* OR *ssn* OR *financial*) OR rule.name:*privacy* OR event.code:*privacy_violation*)) OR (data_stream.dataset:*dlp*)

attributes.title: [CCI-004337] Total Privacy Incidents

attributes.description:
This section provides visualizations designed to be the foundation for reporting on information privacy measures of performance, directly addressing CCI-004337. The visualizations below summarize key privacy-related events and trends.

**How an analyst can use it:**
1.  Assemble these visualizations onto a new or existing Kibana dashboard.
2.  To perform a component-level compliance assessment, utilize the global filters at the top of the dashboard. Apply filters like 'host.name: <your_component_name>' or 'data_stream.dataset: <your_data_source>' to focus on a specific system or data source.
3.  Use Kibana's native 'Reporting' feature found in the dashboard's toolbar to generate on-demand or scheduled reports in PDF or CSV format.
4.  These reports can then be distributed to stakeholders as required, fulfilling the obligation to 'Report on the results of information privacy measures of performance.' The visualizations provide both high-level metrics and detailed evidence suitable for comprehensive performance reviews.
attributes.title: [CCI-004337] Report Information Privacy Measures of Performance

**Why this visualization works:** It helps to detect subtle or emerging threats to sensitive data that rule-based systems might miss. By establishing a baseline of normal privacy-event activity, it can flag unusual spikes that could indicate a data leak, policy violation, or reconnaissance activity targeting sensitive information.

**How an analyst can use it:** Look for any spikes in the anomaly score. A high score is a strong indicator that investigation is needed. attributes.title: [CCI-004337] PM-6

attributes.description: This section provides visualizations to help you assess compliance with CCI-000168, focusing on the protection of audit records retained in long-term storage. This visualization displays the health status of audit records in long-term storage for CCI-000168 based on the anomaly scores from associated ML jobs.
attributes.title: [CCI-000168] Audit Record Retention ML

attributes.description:
This visualization displays the maximum anomaly score over time for the job monitoring unauthorized activity in long-term storage. 

**Why this visualization works:** The ML job `au-11-cci-000168-unauth-storage-activity-job` learns the normal, authorized behavior of privileged users interacting with long-term audit storage. A spike in this score indicates an unusual event, such as an unauthorized user attempting to access, modify, or delete archived audit logs. This is a critical measure for CCI-000168, which requires that audit records in long-term storage are protected from unauthorized activity. The ML model provides a powerful layer of defense by detecting subtle behavioral changes that might be missed by simple rule-based alerts, like an authorized user performing a deletion at an unusual time or from an unexpected host.

**How an analyst can use it:** Monitor for any spikes in the anomaly score. A high score (e.g., 75 or above) warrants immediate investigation. Use the time range selector to narrow down the period of the anomaly. The `influencers` table below will help you identify the specific `user.name`, `host.name`, `file.path`, and `event.action` associated with the anomalous behavior. This will help you quickly determine if the activity was malicious or a benign, but unusual, administrative task.
attributes.title: [AU-11, CCI-000168] ML - Unauth Storage Anomaly Score Trend

attributes.title: AU-11 CCI-000168

attributes.description: This section provides visualizations to help you assess compliance with CCI-001493, focusing on ensuring that only authorized personnel can manage audit functionality. You'll find visualizations that highlight attempts to use audit management tools by unauthorized roles or personnel. To perform a component-level compliance assessment, utilize the global filters at the top of the Kibana dashboard. Apply filters like 'host.name: <your_component_name>' to focus on a specific system. Observe the trends and individual events in the visualizations for any anomalies or policy violations. Instances where users outside the defined 'auditor-group' (or your organization-defined authorized group) are executing audit management tools indicate a potential compliance failure. Pay close attention to the 'user.name' and 'user.group.name' fields to verify proper authorization. This visualization now displays the health status for CCI-001493 based on the anomaly scores from associated ML jobs.
attributes.title: [CCI-001493] Protection of Audit Information: Authorized Management ML

attributes.description:
This visualization displays the total count of instances where audit management tools were used by unauthorized personnel or roles, providing a critical high-level metric for **CCI-001493** (authorizing access to management of audit functionality). 

**Why this visualization works:** It provides a quick, high-level understanding of the overall unauthorized activity directed at audit functionality. A non-zero count here immediately flags a potential compliance issue, signaling a need for deeper investigation into your access controls. 

**How an analyst can use it:** Use this metric in conjunction with the trend visualization to understand if a high number of unauthorized attempts is a recent spike or a persistent issue. This visualization acts as a quick health check for the authorization controls on audit management. A continuously high count, even if not spiking, suggests a need for deeper investigation into why unauthorized users are attempting to manage audit functions. Remember to use global filters to focus on specific components (e.g., 'host.name: <your_component_name>').

query.query: (process.name:(auditctl OR wevtutil.exe OR auditpol OR journalctl OR powershell.exe OR cmd.exe OR bash OR wmic OR sc.exe OR reg.exe OR net.exe OR taskkill.exe OR schtasks.exe OR at.exe OR psexec.exe OR mimikatz.exe OR certutil.exe OR bitsadmin.exe)) AND (event.category:(process OR file OR registry OR network OR iam) OR event.type:(creation OR modification OR deletion OR execution OR policy_update OR service_control OR authentication OR authorization OR role_change OR user_provisioning OR user_deprovisioning)) AND NOT (user.group.name:"auditor-group" OR user.name:('authorized_user_1' OR 'admin_service_account' OR system OR root OR  administrator OR "NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM" OR LocalSystem OR NetworkService)) AND NOT (process.name:auditd AND event.outcome:success)

attributes.title: [AU-9, CCI-001493] Total Unauthorized Audit Tool Usage (converted)

attributes.description:
This visualization displays the distribution of access, modification, and deletion attempts on audit information and tools by different users. This provides a comprehensive view for the overall **AU-9** control, and specifically supports **CCI-001493** (authorized management). 

**Why this visualization works:** It quickly highlights which users are most frequently interacting with or attempting to interact with audit data and tools, allowing analysts to identify both authorized and potentially unauthorized high-activity users. This is crucial for verifying that only a defined subset of privileged users is managing audit functionality as per AU-9(4). 

**How an analyst can use it:** Look for unexpected users appearing with high counts, especially for modification or deletion attempts. Use global filters to narrow down by host or specific audit tools. Investigate any users who are not part of the 'organization-defined subset of privileged users' for audit management. This helps verify that access to audit management functionality is appropriately restricted across all relevant CCIs under AU-9. Remember to use global filters to focus on a specific component (e.g., 'host.name: <your_component_name>').

query.query: (process.name:(auditctl OR wevtutil.exe OR auditpol OR journalctl OR powershell.exe OR cmd.exe OR bash OR wmic OR sc.exe OR reg.exe OR net.exe OR taskkill.exe OR schtasks.exe OR at.exe OR psexec.exe OR mimikatz.exe OR certutil.exe OR bitsadmin.exe)) AND (event.category:(process OR file OR registry OR network OR iam) OR event.type:(creation OR modification OR deletion OR execution OR policy_update OR service_control OR authentication OR authorization OR role_change OR user_provisioning OR user_deprovisioning)) AND NOT (user.group.name:"auditor-group" OR user.name:('authorized_user_1' OR 'admin_service_account' OR system OR root OR  administrator OR "NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM" OR LocalSystem OR NetworkService)) AND NOT (process.name:auditd AND event.outcome:success)

attributes.title: [CCI-001493] Audit Activity by User (All CCIs)

attributes.description:
This table provides a detailed breakdown of individual events where audit management tools were used by unauthorized personnel or roles, providing granular evidence for **CCI-001493** (authorizing access to management of audit functionality). 

**Why this visualization works:** It offers granular detail on each event, including the timestamp, host, user, their group, the process involved, and the specific arguments used. This level of detail is crucial for in-depth investigation and pinpointing exact policy violations, allowing you to understand the 'who, what, and where' of potential compliance failures related to audit management access. 

**How an analyst can use it:** Sort by 'user.name' or 'user.group.name' to identify common sources of unauthorized activity. Examine 'process.name' and 'process.args' to understand which specific audit tools were used and how. The 'host.name' field helps to identify the component involved. This table is essential for understanding the context of each unauthorized event and verifying if your access controls are working as intended. Remember to apply global filters for specific components (e.g., 'host.name: <your_component_name>') to refine your view.

query.query: (process.name:(auditctl OR wevtutil.exe OR auditpol OR journalctl OR powershell.exe OR cmd.exe OR bash OR wmic OR sc.exe OR reg.exe OR net.exe OR taskkill.exe OR schtasks.exe OR at.exe OR psexec.exe OR mimikatz.exe OR certutil.exe OR bitsadmin.exe)) AND (event.category:(process OR file OR registry OR network OR iam) OR event.type:(creation OR modification OR deletion OR execution OR policy_update OR service_control OR authentication OR authorization OR role_change OR user_provisioning OR user_deprovisioning)) AND NOT (user.group.name:"auditor-group" OR user.name:('authorized_user_1' OR 'admin_service_account' OR system OR root OR  administrator OR "NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM" OR LocalSystem OR NetworkService)) AND NOT (process.name:auditd AND event.outcome:success)

attributes.title: [AU-9, CCI-001493] Detailed Unauthorized Audit Tool Usage Events

attributes.description:
This visualization displays the maximum anomaly score over time for the job monitoring the usage of audit management tools.

**Why this visualization works:**
It helps detect unusual behavior that might otherwise go unnoticed. For instance, an administrator using an audit tool at an odd hour, or from an unusual location, could be flagged by the ML job, whereas a simple rule-based alert would miss this context.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Look for spikes in the anomaly score. A high score indicates a significant deviation from normal, authorized behavior. Use the time selector to isolate these periods and cross-reference with the detailed table to see which user and tool are associated with the anomaly.
attributes.title: [AU-9-CCI-001493] ML - Audit Tool Usage Anomaly Score Trend

attributes.description:
This section provides Machine Learning (ML) powered visualizations to help assess compliance with CCI-001493, which focuses on authorizing access for the management of audit functionality. This visualization analyzes anomaly scores from an ML job that detects unusual usage of audit management tools.

To perform a component-level compliance assessment, utilize the global filters at the top of the Kibana dashboard. Apply filters such as 'influencers.host.name: <your_component_name>' to focus on a specific system. The ML job learns the normal patterns of tool usage by authorized users. Therefore, an anomaly could represent a compromised authorized account or an insider threat. Investigate any high-score anomalies, paying close attention to the user, the tool used, and the time of the event.
attributes.title: [AU-9-CCI-001493] ML - Unauthorized Audit Tool Usage Anomalies

attributes.title: [CCI-001493] CCI RMF Dashboard

attributes.description: This section provides visualizations to help you assess compliance with CCI-003832, focusing on validating that designated personnel or roles are properly alerted upon detection of unauthorized access, modification, or deletion of audit information. The visualizations here highlight the *events* that should trigger such alerts. To perform a component-level compliance assessment, utilize the global filters at the top of the Kibana dashboard. Apply filters like 'host.name: <your_component_name>' to focus on a specific system. Observe the trends and individual events in the visualizations for any unauthorized activity. Your primary task as an analyst is to verify that for each detected unauthorized event, an alert was indeed generated and received by the specific 'Alert Group defined per role' for that component. This often involves cross-referencing with your organization's alert management system or incident response logs. The goal is to ensure that the *definition* of who gets alerted translates into *actual* alerts for relevant security incidents, ensuring timely response and compliance. This visualization now displays the health status for CCI-003832 based on the anomaly scores from associated ML jobs.
attributes.title: [CCI-003832] Alert Personnel on Unauthorized Audit Activity ML

attributes.description:
This table provides a detailed breakdown of individual events related to unauthorized access, modification, or deletion of audit information. These are the specific events that should trigger alerts to your defined personnel, directly supporting **CCI-003831** and **CCI-003832**. 

**Why this visualization works:** It offers granular detail on each event, including the timestamp, host, user, affected file, and the specific action attempted. This level of detail is crucial for in-depth investigation and allows you to pinpoint exact policy violations that demand an alert. It enables a direct cross-reference with your alert system to confirm that each specific incident resulted in the expected notification to the 'Alert Group defined per role'. 

**How an analyst can use it:** Sort by '@timestamp' to see the most recent events, or by 'user.name' to identify repeat offenders. Examine 'file.path' to pinpoint specific audit resources being targeted. The 'event.action' field will show what unauthorized action was attempted (e.g., access, change, delete). For each event displayed here (especially those indicating a critical unauthorized action), you must **verify that a corresponding alert was generated and successfully delivered** to the appropriate personnel or roles as defined by your organization's policy for **CCI-003831** and **CCI-003832**. This table is essential for detailed compliance verification at the individual event level. Remember to apply global filters for specific components (e.g., 'host.name: <your_component_name>') to refine your view.

kibanaSavedObjectMeta.searchSourceJSON: {"query":{"language":"kuery","query":"(file.path:\"/var/log/audit/*\" OR file.path:\"C:\\Windows\\System32\\winevt\\Logs\\*\" OR file.extension:(log OR evtx OR json OR xml OR conf OR cfg OR ini) OR process.name:(auditd OR wevtutil OR auditpol OR journalctl OR powershell.exe OR cmd.exe OR bash OR wmic OR sc.exe OR reg.exe OR net.exe OR taskkill.exe OR schtasks.exe OR at.exe OR psexec.exe OR mimikatz.exe OR certutil.exe OR bitsadmin.exe) OR event.module:(auditbeat OR winlogbeat OR filebeat OR sysmon OR osquery OR zeek OR suricata) OR cloud.service.name:(CloudTrail OR Azure Activity Logs OR GCP Audit Logs OR Kubernetes)) AND (event.category:(file OR process OR registry OR network OR iam) OR event.type:(access OR modification OR deletion OR creation OR configuration OR execution OR authentication OR authorization OR policy OR service_control)) AND NOT (user.name:(*authorized_user* OR *system* OR *administrator* OR \"NT AUTHORITY\\SYSTEM\" OR LocalSystem OR NetworkService) AND event.outcome:success) AND NOT (process.name:auditd AND event.outcome:success)"},"filter":[],"indexRefName":"kibanaSavedObjectMeta.searchSourceJSON.index"}

attributes.description:
This visualization displays the total count of unauthorized access, modification, or deletion attempts on audit information. This metric is crucial for both **CCI-003831** (alerting personnel) and **CCI-003832** (verifying alerts). 

**Why this visualization works:** It provides a quick, high-level understanding of the overall unauthorized activity that *should* be triggering alerts to the defined personnel. A non-zero count here immediately indicates a need for the analyst to investigate whether an alert was appropriately generated and delivered to the correct 'Alert Group defined per role'. 

**How an analyst can use it:** Use this metric in conjunction with the trend visualization to understand if a high number of unauthorized attempts is a recent spike or a persistent issue. This visualization acts as a quick health check for the effectiveness of your alerting mechanisms. A continuously high count, even if not spiking, suggests a need for deeper investigation into why unauthorized users are attempting to interact with audit data, and more importantly, to verify if your defined alerting procedures are being followed and are effective in notifying the appropriate personnel for these events. Remember to use global filters to focus on specific components (e.g., 'host.name: <your_component_name>').

attributes.description:
This visualization presents a breakdown of access, modification, and deletion attempts by their outcome (success or failure) and the type of action performed. This provides a critical summary for the overall **AU-9** control, and is relevant to **CCI-000162, CCI-000163, CCI-000164, CCI-001494, CCI-001495, CCI-003831, and CCI-003832**. 

**Why this visualization works:** It offers a quick visual summary of the effectiveness of audit information protection mechanisms. A high number of successful unauthorized attempts is a clear indication of a compliance failure, while a high number of failed attempts might indicate active threats or misconfigurations that need to be addressed, directly supporting the requirements of AU-9 to protect audit information. 

**How an analyst can use it:** Prioritize investigation into successful unauthorized attempts. Analyze the distribution of failed vs. successful attempts to understand if protection mechanisms are effectively thwarting unauthorized actions. Use global filters to drill down into specific components or timeframes. This visualization provides an immediate compliance status check for AU-9, helping you quickly identify areas where audit information or tools are vulnerable to unauthorized access, modification, or deletion. Remember to use global filters to focus on a specific component (e.g., 'host.name: <your_component_name>').

kibanaSavedObjectMeta.searchSourceJSON:
{"query":{"query":"(
  (
    file.path : "/var/log/audit/*"
    OR file.path : "/var/log/secure"
    OR file.path : "/var/log/messages"
    OR file.path : "C:\\Windows\\System32\\winevt\\Logs\\*"
    OR file.path : "C:\\Windows\\System32\\Config\\*"
  )
  OR (event.module : (auditbeat OR winlogbeat OR filebeat OR sysmon OR osquery) AND file.extension : (evtx OR log))
)
AND (
  event.action : (file_deleted OR file_modified OR file_renamed OR file_moved OR file_created OR registry_value_deleted OR registry_value_set OR registry_key_deleted OR registry_key_created OR configuration_changed OR service_configuration_changed)
  OR event.category : (registry OR file)
)
AND NOT (
  user.name : ("NT AUTHORITY\\SYSTEM" OR "LocalSystem" OR NetworkService OR "NT AUTHORITY\\LOCAL SERVICE" OR administrator)
)
AND NOT process.name : (filebeat OR auditbeat OR metricbeat OR winlogbeat OR nxlog.exe OR rsyslogd OR systemd-journald)
","language":"kuery"},"filter":[],"indexRefName":"kibanaSavedObjectMeta.searchSourceJSON.index"}

This visualization provides an immediate compliance status check for AU-9, helping you quickly identify areas where audit information or tools are vulnerable to unauthorized access, modification, or deletion. Remember to use global filters to focus on a specific component (e.g., 'host.name: <your_component_name>').","enhancements":{"dynamicActions":{"events":[]}},"title":"[AU-9] Audit Activity Outcome and Action Breakdown (All CCIs)"},"panelIndex":"fe5934af-fc5e-4607-94c9-88ba13cb3e83","gridData":{"i":"fe5934af-fc5e-4607-94c9-88ba13cb3e83","y":9,"x":0,"w":24,"h":30}}]
attributes.title: [CCI-003832 AU-9]

attributes.description: This visualization displays the health status for CCI-004618 by monitoring the highest anomaly scores from its associated Machine Learning job, which detects unusual volumes of new security signals. A high anomaly score indicates a significant deviation from the baseline, suggesting newly identified threats.
attributes.title: [CCI-004618] Identify Threats ML

attributes.description:
This visualization provides a high-level, at-a-glance count of all security signals that are currently open and require investigation.

**Why this visualization works:**
It offers an immediate summary of the current threat backlog. This number serves as a key performance indicator for the security team's operational tempo and the overall health of the monitored components.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Use this metric as a starting point for daily assessments. A sudden, unexplained increase in this number should trigger an immediate investigation. Use it in conjunction with the trend and detailed visualizations to understand the context behind the total count.
attributes.title: [CCI-004618] Total Active Threats

attributes.description:
This visualization displays a time-series trend of open security signals, broken down by threat severity.

**Why this visualization works:**
It provides a dynamic view of threat activity over time, allowing for the identification of spikes or patterns. Visualizing when threats occur helps correlate them with other events and understand if you are facing a prolonged attack campaign or isolated incidents.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Look for sudden increases in threat counts, which could indicate the start of an attack. A consistent, high baseline of threats may suggest a widespread misconfiguration or a persistent adversary. Use the time range selector to zoom in on suspicious periods and correlate the findings with other data sources.
attributes.title: [CCI-004618] Threat Identification Trend by Severity (converted)

attributes.description:
This table provides a detailed, granular view of every open security signal, showing the raw data needed for deep-dive forensic analysis.

**Why this visualization works:**
It provides the ground-truth data behind all other visualizations. For any identified threat or anomaly, this table contains the specific details required to investigate, validate, and respond. It is the cornerstone of the threat identification process.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Use this table as the final step in an investigation. After identifying a trend or a high-level threat, find the corresponding raw events here. Sort by columns like `host.name` or `rule.name` to group related activity. Use the data in this table to pivot to other tools or to initiate incident response procedures.
attributes.title: [CCI-004618] Detailed Threat Events

attributes.description:
This section helps you assess compliance with CCI-004618 by identifying threats to your systems. The visualizations below leverage Elastic SIEM's detection signals to provide a comprehensive view of the threat landscape.

**How to Perform a Component-Level Compliance Assessment:**
1.  Utilize the global filters at the top of the Kibana dashboard to focus on a specific system or component (e.g., apply a filter like `host.name: <your_component_name>`).
2.  Review the **Total Active Threats** metric for an immediate count of open security signals for that component.
3.  Analyze the **Threat Identification Trend** to understand the volume and timeline of threats. Look for spikes or sustained activity that may indicate a targeted attack or persistent issue.
4.  Examine the **Top Threats** visualizations to understand the nature of the threats. Are they focused on initial access, execution, or data exfiltration? This context is crucial for risk assessment.
5.  Use the **Detailed Threat Events** table to drill down into specific alerts for forensic investigation. This table provides the 'who, what, and where' for each identified threat.

By combining these views, you can build a detailed threat profile for each component, which is a foundational activity for any risk assessment as required by RA-3.

attributes.title: [RA-3 CCI-004618]

attributes.description: This visualization displays the health status of audit report generation for CCI-000169 based on the anomaly scores from the associated ML job. A high score indicates a significant deviation from the normal rate of report generation.
attributes.title: [CCI-000169] Audit Generation ML

attributes.description:
This table provides a detailed list of all audit reports that have been generated. It is the primary tool for verifying that the required reports are being created for each component.

**Why and when this visualization is best used:**
This table is most effective when you need to see the specifics of each generated report. Use it to confirm the existence of reports, check their creation times, and identify the responsible user and process. It's the go-to visualization for a detailed, evidence-based compliance check.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Filter the table by 'host.name' to focus on a specific system. You can also sort by '@timestamp' to see the most recent reports. The 'user.name' and 'process.name' fields will help you identify who or what is generating the reports. The 'file.path' will show you where the report is stored. By examining this data, you can confirm that each component is meeting its audit reporting requirements.

query.query: (process.name: (*report* OR *audit*) AND file.extension: (pdf OR csv OR txt OR xlsx)) OR (event.action: (report_generated OR audit_review_completed))

attributes.title: [CCI-000169] Generated Audit Reports

attributes.description:
This heatmap shows which systems (hosts) are generating key types of audit events. It provides a quick, visual confirmation of audit coverage across your architecture, directly supporting CCI-000169 (Audit Record Generation).

**Why and When:** Use this visualization to get a high-level overview of your audit posture. It's most valuable for quickly identifying blind spots. For instance, if a critical server is not reporting any 'authentication' or 'file' events, it signifies a major gap in audit generation that needs immediate attention.

**How to Use:** Each cell represents the volume of a specific event action generated by a particular host. Darker cells indicate higher event counts. Look for rows (hosts) that are entirely light-colored or have unexpectedly light cells for critical actions. This suggests that the host is either offline, misconfigured, or not generating the required audit trails. Use the global filters to narrow down to a specific group of assets to check their collective coverage.

query.query: event.category:(authentication OR file OR process OR network)

attributes.title: [AU-12] Audit Event Generation Matrix by Host

attributes.description:
This bar chart shows the number of audit reports generated by each user. It helps to quickly identify which users are responsible for report generation and to spot any potential anomalies.

**Why and when this visualization is best used:**
Use this chart to get a quick overview of report generation activity across different users. It is particularly useful for identifying users who are generating an unusually high or low number of reports, which could indicate a misconfiguration, a policy violation, or a security issue.

**How an analyst can use it:**
The height of each bar represents the number of reports generated by that user. Hover over a bar to see the exact count. If a user who is not authorized to generate reports appears in the chart, or if an authorized user has a count of zero, it warrants further investigation. Use this chart as a starting point to identify potential issues, then use the detailed table to dig deeper.

query.query: (process.name: (*report* OR *audit*) AND file.extension: (pdf OR csv OR txt OR xlsx)) OR (event.action: (report_generated OR audit_review_completed))

attributes.title: [CCI-000169] Audit Reports Generated by User

attributes.description: This section provides visualizations to help you assess compliance with CCI-000169, which requires the organization to provide a report of audit review, analysis, and reporting to designated personnel. These visualizations will help you track the generation of these reports and ensure they are delivered to the correct individuals. To perform a component-level compliance assessment, use the global filters at the top of the Kibana dashboard to filter by 'host.name' or other relevant fields. This will allow you to verify that each component is generating the required audit reports.
