attributes.title: [CCI-000169] AU-12

attributes.description: This section provides visualizations to help you assess compliance with CCI-001494, focusing on protecting audit tools from unauthorized modification. You'll find visualizations designed to highlight any attempts to alter audit tool configurations, binaries, or permissions. To perform a component-level compliance assessment, utilize the global filters at the top of the Kibana dashboard. Apply filters like 'host.name: <your_component_name>' to focus on a specific system. Observe the trends and individual events in the visualizations for any anomalies or unauthorized changes. Any modification event not performed by an explicitly authorized user or process should be investigated thoroughly. This visualization now displays the health status for CCI-001494 based on the anomaly scores from associated ML jobs.
attributes.title: [CCI-001494] Protect Audit Tools from Unauthorized Modification ML

attributes.description:
This table provides a detailed breakdown of individual events related to unauthorized modifications of audit tools, offering granular insights for **CCI-001494** (protecting audit tools from unauthorized modification). 

**Why this visualization works:** It offers granular detail on each event, including the timestamp, host, user, process, and file involved, and the specific action attempted. This level of detail is crucial for in-depth investigation and pinpointing exact policy violations, allowing you to understand the 'who, what, and where' of potential audit tool integrity failures. 

**How an analyst can use it:** Sort by 'user.name' or 'process.name' to identify common sources of unauthorized activity. Examine 'file.path' and 'file.name' to pinpoint specific audit resources being targeted. The 'event.action' field will show what action was attempted (e.g., file_change, file_modify). This table is critical for understanding the context of each unauthorized modification and verifying if your integrity controls are working as intended. Remember to apply global filters for specific components (e.g., 'host.name: <your_component_name>') to refine your view.

query.query: (file.path:"/var/log/audit/*" OR file.path:"C:\Windows\System32\winevt\Logs\*" OR file.extension:(log OR evtx OR json OR xml OR conf OR cfg OR ini) OR process.name:(auditd OR wevtutil OR auditpol OR journalctl OR powershell.exe OR cmd.exe OR bash OR wmic OR sc.exe OR reg.exe OR net.exe OR taskkill.exe OR schtasks.exe OR at.exe OR psexec.exe OR mimikatz.exe OR certutil.exe OR bitsadmin.exe)) AND (event.category:(file OR process OR registry) OR event.type:(modification OR change OR file_change OR file_modify OR file_deletion OR file_rename OR process_modification OR registry_key_modification OR registry_value_change OR permission_change OR ownership_change)) AND NOT (user.name:(*system* OR *administrator* OR 'authorized_admin_group' OR "NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM" OR LocalSystem OR NetworkService) AND event.outcome:success) AND NOT (process.name:auditd AND event.action:file_modify AND event.outcome:success)

attributes.title: [AU-9, CCI-001494] Detailed Unauthorized Audit Tool Modification Events

attributes.description:
This table provides a detailed breakdown of the most significant anomalies related to the modification or deletion of audit information and tools.

**Why this visualization works:**
It provides the critical details needed for a forensic investigation. By highlighting the influencers (user, file, process, action), it allows an analyst to quickly understand the context of an anomalous change and determine if it was a legitimate administrative action or a malicious act.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Filter for high anomaly scores (e.g., anomaly_score >= 75). Focus on the `influencers.file.path` to see what was changed and `influencers.event.action` to see how (modification or deletion). Cross-reference with `influencers.user.name` to verify authorization. This is your primary tool for investigating potential tampering events.
attributes.title: [AU-9-CCIs-001494,1495] ML - Detailed Audit Mod/Del Anomalies

attributes.description:
This visualization displays the total count of instances where unauthorized modification attempts were made on audit tools, providing a high-level overview for **CCI-001494** (protecting audit tools from unauthorized modification). 

**Why this visualization works:** It provides a quick, high-level understanding of the overall unauthorized activity directed at protecting audit tools. A non-zero count here immediately flags a potential compliance issue, signaling a need for deeper investigation into your integrity controls. 

**How an analyst can use it:** Use this metric in conjunction with the trend visualization to understand if a high number of unauthorized attempts is a recent spike or a persistent issue. This visualization acts as a quick health check for the modification controls on audit tools. A continuously high count, even if not spiking, suggests a need for deeper investigation into why unauthorized users are attempting to modify audit functions. Remember to use global filters to focus on specific components (e.g., 'host.name: <your_component_name>').

query.query: (file.path:"/var/log/audit/*" OR file.path:"C:\Windows\System32\winevt\Logs\*" OR file.extension:(log OR evtx OR json OR xml OR conf OR cfg OR ini) OR process.name:(auditd OR wevtutil OR auditpol OR journalctl OR powershell.exe OR cmd.exe OR bash OR wmic OR sc.exe OR reg.exe OR net.exe OR taskkill.exe OR schtasks.exe OR at.exe OR psexec.exe OR mimikatz.exe OR certutil.exe OR bitsadmin.exe)) AND (event.category:(file OR process OR registry) OR event.type:(modification OR change OR file_change OR file_modify OR file_deletion OR file_rename OR process_modification OR registry_key_modification OR registry_value_change OR permission_change OR ownership_change)) AND NOT (user.name:(*system* OR *administrator* OR 'authorized_admin_group' OR "NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM" OR LocalSystem OR NetworkService) AND event.outcome:success) AND NOT (process.name:auditd AND event.action:file_modify AND event.outcome:success)

attributes.title: [CCI-001494] CCI RMF Dashboard

attributes.description: This section provides visualizations to help you assess compliance with CCI-003874, focusing on monitoring system software and service changes. This visualization displays the health status of software and service change monitoring based on anomaly scores from the associated ML job. To perform a component-level compliance assessment, utilize the global filters at the top of the Kibana dashboard. Apply filters like 'host.name: <your_component_name>' or 'package.name: <package_name>' to focus on specific systems or software. Observe the trends and individual anomaly events. A high anomaly score indicates a significant deviation from the established baseline, which could signify unauthorized software installations, service disruptions, or configuration changes, all of which warrant immediate investigation.
attributes.title: [CA-7, CCI-003874] Software & Service Changes Monitoring ML

attributes.description:
**Why and When:** This table lists the services running on your systems and their current state.
It is a key part of configuration management, helping to ensure that only authorized services are active.
Use this during audits to verify that system configurations align with security policies and authorized baselines.

**How:** Filter by `host.name` to focus on a single component.
Look for services that are unexpected or not part of your standard build (e.g., `service.state: running` for a service that should be disabled).
Investigate services with unusual names or those that shouldn't be running on a particular asset to determine any potential security impact.
attributes.title: [CA-7 / CCI-003874] System Service Inventory

attributes.description:
**Why and When:** This table provides a comprehensive, point-in-time inventory of all detected software packages and their versions.
It is the foundation for establishing an authorized software baseline.
Use it to verify that only approved software is present and to identify outdated or unauthorized packages during a compliance review.

**How:** Filter by `host.name` to audit a specific system.
Sort by `package.name` to group similar software. Compare the list against your organization's authorized software list.
A single instance of an unauthorized package is a clear finding that needs to be investigated to understand its security impact.
attributes.title: [CA-7 / CCI-003874] System Software Inventory

attributes.description:
**Why this visualization works:** This visualization displays the maximum anomaly score over time for the job monitoring software and service changes. It is particularly useful for detecting deviations from normal patterns of software installations, updates, removals, and service state changes, which could indicate unauthorized activity, misconfigurations, or system instability impacting compliance. 

**How an analyst can use it:** Look for spikes in the anomaly score. A high score indicates an unusual number of changes. Use the time selector to pinpoint these periods and investigate the specific `host.name`, `package.name`, `service.name`, `event.action`, and `event.type` associated with the anomaly. This helps determine if changes were authorized and if compliance is being maintained.
attributes.title: [CA-7, CCI-003874] ML - Software & Service Changes Anomaly Trend

attributes.title: CA-7 CCI-003874

attributes.description: This visualization displays the health status for CCI-004619 by monitoring the highest anomaly scores from its associated Machine Learning job, which detects unusual numbers of new vulnerabilities. A high anomaly score indicates a significant deviation from the baseline, highlighting newly identified system vulnerabilities.
attributes.title: [CCI-004619] Identify Vulnerabilities ML

attributes.description:
**Why and When:** This table is your primary tool for deep-dive analysis and remediation. It provides the granular, actionable details needed to understand and address each specific vulnerability. Use this when you need to assign remediation tasks or investigate a particular finding.

**How an analyst can use it:** This table lists individual vulnerabilities with critical details. You can sort by any column, such as `vulnerability.severity`, to prioritize the most critical issues first. Use the search bar at the top of the table to find specific vulnerabilities by name (e.g., 'Log4Shell') or to filter for a particular host.
attributes.title: [CCI-004619] Detailed Vulnerability Listings

attributes.description:
Why and When: This chart helps you identify which systems are the most vulnerable and therefore at the highest risk. Use this visualization to direct your patching and remediation teams to the assets that need the most immediate attention.

How an analyst can use it: The bar chart displays hosts with the highest counts of vulnerabilities. The tallest bars represent the systems that should be prioritized for remediation. You can click on any bar to filter the entire dashboard, allowing you to see the specific vulnerabilities for that single host.
attributes.title: [CCI-004619] Top 10 Vulnerable Hosts

attributes.description:
Why this visualization works: This table provides the essential context behind a vulnerability anomaly. It directly links a high anomaly score to the specific hosts affected and the names of the vulnerabilities involved, enabling a rapid assessment of the risk and the start of a remediation workflow.

How an analyst can use it: When an anomaly is detected, use this table to get immediate answers. Sort by 'anomaly_score' to see the most critical events. Identify which hosts ('influencers.host.name') are reporting an unusual number of new vulnerabilities and which vulnerabilities ('influencers.vulnerability.name') are most prevalent. This information is key to prioritizing patching and other mitigation efforts.
attributes.title: [CCI-004619] ML - Detailed New Vulnerability Anomalies

attributes.description:
This section provides visualizations to help you assess compliance with CCI-004619, which focuses on identifying vulnerabilities within the system. While Elastic does not perform vulnerability scanning, it excels at consolidating and analyzing data from dedicated scanners like Nessus or Qualys.

**How to Perform a Component-Level Compliance Assessment:**

1.  **Filter by Component:** Use the global filters at the top of the dashboard to focus on a specific system or component. For example, apply a filter like `host.name: "your_component_name"` or `host.ip: "192.168.1.10"`.
2.  **Analyze Vulnerability Posture:** With the component filter applied, observe the visualizations. Check the 'Vulnerability Breakdown by Severity' to understand the risk level. Identify critical or high-severity vulnerabilities that require immediate attention.
3.  **Prioritize Remediation:** Use the 'Detailed Vulnerability Listings' table to view specific vulnerabilities for the filtered component. Sort by severity to prioritize patching and remediation efforts.
4.  **Track Trends:** Observe the 'Vulnerability Discovery Trend' to see if new vulnerabilities are being introduced to the component over time, which could indicate issues with patching cycles or configuration management.

attributes.title: [CCI-004619]
CCI-000171 Rare Event Action By Host

attributes.description: This visualization displays the health status of [AU-3 CCI-000130] [AU.L2-3.3]  compliance based on anomaly scores from the associated ML job. High scores may indicate an abnormally low count of specific event actions, suggesting a failure to log required events.
attributes.title: [CCI-000130] Audit Events ML

attributes.description:
This chart displays the distribution of audit events by the reporting module (e.g., auditbeat, winlogbeat, osquery). It helps confirm that all your deployed logging agents are active and sending data.

**Why this visualization works:**
It provides a simple, clear view of your log sources. If a critical system is supposed to be reporting via a specific beat and that module is missing from this chart, you have a clear indicator of a compliance gap.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Check the chart to ensure all expected 'event.module' values are present. If a module is missing, it could mean the agent is down or misconfigured on one or more systems. Click on a slice of the pie to filter the dashboard and see only the events from that specific module.

query.query: event.module: (auditd or auditbeat or sysmon or system or security or powershell or osquery or "windows" or "linux" or "aws" or "gcp" or "azure") OR event.dataset: (*.log or *.audit or *.security or *.system or *.application or "cloudtrail" or "guardduty") OR event.category: (authentication or configuration or database or driver or file or host or iam or intrusion_detection or malware or network or package or process or registry or session or threat or web) OR event.type: (access or change or connection or creation or deletion or end or error or group or info or installation or modification or process_started or protocol or start or user)

attributes.title: [AU-3 CCI-000130] Events by Module

attributes.description:
This chart shows the total count of different event actions over time. It helps you quickly verify if expected actions are being logged and to spot any unusual spikes or dips in activity.

**Why this visualization works:**
It provides a high-level overview of system activity. If your policy requires logging all file modifications, but you see no 'file_modify' events, this chart makes that gap immediately obvious.

**How an analyst can use it:**
Review the chart to ensure that all event actions defined in your organization's audit plan are present. Look for any actions that have a count of zero, as this may indicate a logging failure. Use the time selector to investigate specific periods of interest. Click on a specific action in the legend to filter the dashboard for only that event type.

query.query: event.module: (auditd or auditbeat or sysmon or system or security or powershell or osquery or "windows" or "linux" or "aws" or "gcp" or "azure") OR event.dataset: (*.log or *.audit or *.security or *.system or *.application or "cloudtrail" or "guardduty") OR event.category: (authentication or configuration or database or driver or file or host or iam or intrusion_detection or malware or network or package or process or registry or session or threat or web) OR event.type: (access or change or connection or creation or deletion or end or error or group or info or installation or modification or process_started or protocol or start or user)

attributes.title: [AU-3 CCI-000130] Event Actions Breakdown (converted)

attributes.description:
This table provides a detailed, searchable log of all audit events. It is the primary tool for verifying that specific, required audit event content is being captured.

**Why this visualization works:**
It offers a granular, unfiltered view of the raw data, which is essential for compliance verification. You can inspect individual log entries to confirm they contain the necessary details (e.g., user, source IP, action taken).

**How an analyst can use it:**
Use the search bar at the top of the table to filter for specific event types, users, hosts, or keywords relevant to your organization's audit requirements. For example, search for 'event.action:login' to verify login events are being recorded. Sort by different columns to analyze patterns. This table is your ground truth for CCI-000130 compliance.

query.query: event.module: (auditd or auditbeat or sysmon or system or security or powershell or osquery or "windows" or "linux" or "aws" or "gcp" or "azure") OR event.dataset: (*.log or *.audit or *.security or *.system or *.application or "cloudtrail" or "guardduty") OR event.category: (authentication or configuration or database or driver or file or host or iam or intrusion_detection or malware or network or package or process or registry or session or threat or web) OR event.type: (access or change or connection or creation or deletion or end or error or group or info or installation or modification or process_started or protocol or start or user)

attributes.title: [AU-3 CCI-000130] Audit Event Log (converted)

attributes.description:
**Why and When:** This table identifies the specific `event.action` types that are exhibiting anomalously low counts, as required by CCI-000130. It provides the necessary detail to investigate a potential logging failure identified in the trend chart.

**How to Use:** When the trend chart shows an anomaly, consult this table. It will show the `event.action` and `host.name` with a high anomaly score. This tells you, for example, that 'user-login' events from 'server-01' have dropped unexpectedly. This allows you to focus your investigation on the authentication logging mechanisms for that specific server.
attributes.title: [AU-3, CCI-000130] ML - Event Actions with Low Volume

It helps confirm that all your deployed logging agents are active and sending data.

**Why this visualization works:**
It provides a simple, clear view of your log sources. If a critical system is supposed to be reporting via a specific beat and that module is missing from this chart, you have a clear indicator of a compliance gap.

**How an analyst can use it:**

attributes.title: CMMC/RMF Main Dashboard

attributes.description:
This visualization displays a time-series trend of overall security events. [cite_start]This serves as a primary measure of performance, showing the volume of security-relevant activities over time[cite: 3].

**Why this visualization works:** It provides a high-level overview of the operational tempo and helps identify periods of unusual activity that may require reporting. [cite_start]A sudden spike or a sustained increase in events can be a key performance indicator (KPI) for security reports [cite: 4][cite_start].

**How an analyst can use it:** Monitor the trend for significant deviations from the baseline[cite: 5]. [cite_start]Use the time range selector to drill down into specific periods of interest[cite: 5]. [cite_start]This chart is a foundational element for any security performance report, providing context for more detailed metrics[cite: 6].

query.query: ((file.path:("/var/log/audit/*" OR "C:\Windows\System32\winevt\Logs\*") OR file.extension:("log" OR "evtx" OR "json" OR "xml" OR "conf" OR "cfg" OR "ini") OR process.name:("auditd" OR "wevtutil" OR "auditpol" OR "journalctl" OR "powershell.exe" OR "cmd.exe" OR "bash" OR "wmic" OR "sc.exe" OR "reg.exe" OR "net.exe" OR "taskkill.exe" OR "schtasks.exe" OR "at.exe" OR "psexec.exe") OR event.module:("auditbeat" OR "winlogbeat" OR "filebeat" OR "sysmon" OR "osquery" OR "zeek" OR "suricata") OR cloud.service.name:("CloudTrail" OR "Azure Activity Logs" OR "GCP Audit Logs" OR "Kubernetes")) AND (event.category:("file" OR "process" OR "registry" OR "network" OR "iam" OR "configuration" OR "authentication" OR "malware" OR "intrusion_detection") OR event.type:("access" OR "modification" OR "deletion" OR "creation" OR "change" OR "start" OR "end" OR "info" OR "error" OR "connection" OR "protocol" OR "user" OR "group" OR "policy" OR "service_control" OR "execution" OR "rename" OR "permission_change" OR "ownership_change"))) AND NOT ((user.name:("system" OR "administrator" OR "NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM" OR "LocalSystem" OR "NetworkService" OR "*$" OR "org_admin_user") OR user.group.name:("Domain Admins" OR "org_admin_group")) AND event.outcome:success)

attributes.title: [CCI-000211] Overall Security Event Trend (converted)

attributes.description:
This visualization displays the maximum anomaly score over time for the job monitoring reportable incidents.

**Why this visualization works:** It provides a timeline of when potentially reportable incidents are occurring at an anomalous rate. This is a crucial leading indicator, helping analysts proactively identify and investigate clusters of activity that may need to be formally reported, ensuring compliance with CCI-000211.

**How an analyst can use it:** Monitor this chart for spikes in the anomaly score. Any significant increase warrants a deeper look. Use the time selector to zoom in on the anomaly, then use the 'Top Anomaly Influencers' table to identify the specific users, hosts, and actions driving the unusual activity.
attributes.title: [PM-6, CCI-000211] ML - Reportable Incidents Anomaly Score Trend

attributes.description:
This visualization displays the maximum anomaly score over time for the job monitoring security events.

**Why this visualization works:** It provides a clear timeline of when anomalous spikes in security events are occurring. Instead of relying on fixed thresholds, the ML model identifies unusual increases relative to the learned baseline, helping to detect subtle but significant incidents that might otherwise be missed.

**How an analyst can use it:** Look for spikes in the anomaly score. A high score (e.g., above 75) indicates a significant deviation from normal activity. Use the time selector to isolate these periods and cross-reference with the 'Top Anomaly Influencers' table to see which hosts, users, or rules are associated with the anomaly.
attributes.title: [PM-6, CCI-000210, 000211] ML - Security Events Anomaly Score Trend

attributes.description:
This table provides a detailed breakdown of the entities influencing the anomaly scores for security events.

**Why this visualization works:** It moves beyond just knowing 'when' an anomaly occurred to understanding 'what' or 'who' caused it. By pinpointing the specific hosts, users, or processes contributing to an unusual spike in security events, it allows for rapid, targeted investigation.

**How an analyst can use it:** Use this table to identify the primary drivers behind any significant anomalies seen in the trend chart. Sort by the max anomaly score to prioritize the most impactful influencers. An analyst can then pivot their investigation by filtering on a specific host, user, or process to understand the full scope of the potential incident.
attributes.title: [PM-6, CCI-000210, 000211] ML - Top Security Event Anomaly Influencers

attributes.description: This visualization displays the health status of Information Security Performance Reporting for CCI-000211. The status is determined by the maximum anomaly score from associated Machine Learning jobs that detect unusual increases in key security events and reportable incidents. A high score indicates a significant deviation from the baseline, suggesting potential performance issues or threats that require investigation and reporting.

56c2a0ca-e9fb-451a-b4d3-c09342c80ef6.label: [CCI-000211] Reporting on Information Security Measures of Performance

attributes.title: [CCI-000211] Reporting on Information Security Measures of Performance ML

attributes.description:
This visualization provides a quick summary of the total number of high-priority security incidents that would typically be included in a performance report[cite: 10]. [cite_start]This includes failed events and high-severity anomalies [cite: 10][cite_start].

**Why this visualization works:** It gives an immediate, high-level count of reportable incidents[cite: 11]. [cite_start]This single metric is a powerful KPI for security performance reporting, showing the volume of significant events that required attention [cite: 11][cite_start].

**How an analyst can use it:** Use this number as a headline metric in security reports[cite: 12]. [cite_start]Track this number over time to demonstrate the effectiveness of security controls[cite: 12]. [cite_start]A decreasing number may indicate improved security posture, while a spike would be a key finding to report and explain[cite: 13].

query.query: event.outcome:failure OR (event.dataset:*.anomaly AND anomaly_score >= 75) OR ((event.action:("user_password_changed" OR "file_deleted" OR "registry_key_deleted" OR "privilege-escalation" OR "critical-process-terminated") OR event.type:("deletion" OR "creation") OR (event.category:"authentication" AND event.type:"user")) AND event.outcome:success AND NOT (user.name:("system" OR "administrator" OR "NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM" OR "LocalSystem" OR "NetworkService" OR "*$" OR "org_authorized_user") OR user.group.name:("Domain Admins" OR "org_admin_group")))

attributes.title: [CCI-000211] Total Reportable Incidents

attributes.description:
This table provides a detailed breakdown of security events, which can be directly included in performance reports[cite: 7]. [cite_start]It summarizes event counts by host, user, and event category [cite: 7][cite_start].

**Why this visualization works:** It offers granular metrics that are essential for reporting on information security measures of performance[cite: 8]. [cite_start]It allows stakeholders to see where security events are originating from and what types of activities are most prevalent [cite: 8][cite_start].

**How an analyst can use it:** Use this table to identify top-talking hosts, users generating the most security events, and the most common event categories[cite: 9]. [cite_start]This information can be exported and included in regular security briefings and performance reports to demonstrate security posture and highlight areas of concern[cite: 9].

query.query: ((file.path:("/var/log/audit/*" OR "C:\Windows\System32\winevt\Logs\*") OR file.extension:("log" OR "evtx" OR "json" OR "xml" OR "conf" OR "cfg" OR "ini") OR process.name:("auditd" OR "wevtutil" OR "auditpol" OR "journalctl" OR "powershell.exe" OR "cmd.exe" OR "bash" OR "wmic" OR "sc.exe" OR "reg.exe" OR "net.exe" OR "taskkill.exe" OR "schtasks.exe" OR "at.exe" OR "psexec.exe") OR event.module:("auditbeat" OR "winlogbeat" OR "filebeat" OR "sysmon" OR "osquery" OR "zeek" OR "suricata") OR cloud.service.name:("CloudTrail" OR "Azure Activity Logs" OR "GCP Audit Logs" OR "Kubernetes")) AND (event.category:("file" OR "process" OR "registry" OR "network" OR "iam" OR "configuration" OR "authentication" OR "malware" OR "intrusion_detection") OR event.type:("access" OR "modification" OR "deletion" OR "creation" OR "change" OR "start" OR "end" OR "info" OR "error" OR "connection" OR "protocol" OR "user" OR "group" OR "policy" OR "service_control" OR "execution" OR "rename" OR "permission_change" OR "ownership_change"))) AND NOT ((user.name:("system" OR "administrator" OR "NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM" OR "LocalSystem" OR "NetworkService" OR "*$" OR "org_admin_user") OR user.group.name:("Domain Admins" OR "org_admin_group")) AND event.outcome:success)

attributes.title: [CCI-000211] Detailed Security Performance Metrics

attributes.description:
This table provides a detailed breakdown of the entities influencing the anomaly scores for reportable incidents.

**Why this visualization works:** It directly connects anomalies to the specific actors and actions involved. This is essential for incident response and reporting, as it provides the 'who, what, and where' needed to understand the scope and nature of a potential security incident quickly.

**How an analyst can use it:** When the trend chart shows a spike, this table is the next step. Identify the top influencing users, hosts, and event actions. For example, a high score associated with a specific user and the 'file_deleted' action on a critical host is a high-priority event that requires immediate investigation to determine if it meets the criteria for a reportable incident.
attributes.title: [PM-6, CCI-000211] ML - Top Reportable Incident Anomaly Influencers

attributes.description: This section provides visualizations designed to be exported or reported on, helping to assess and communicate information security measures of performance, thereby satisfying CCI-000211. These visualizations summarize key security metrics, event trends, and anomaly detection results, which can be used to generate scheduled reports via Kibana's reporting features. To perform a component-level assessment, utilize the global filters at the top of the Kibana dashboard. Apply filters like 'host.name: <your_component_name>' to focus on a specific system's performance metrics.

### How to Assess Component Compliance:
To perform a component-level compliance assessment, utilize the global filters at the top of the Kibana dashboard. Apply a filter such as `host.name: "your_component_name"` to isolate the anomaly score for a specific system. An abnormally high score for a specific component indicates a potential surge in vulnerabilities, threats, or compliance failures that needs immediate attention and a potential POA&M update, as required by **CCI-000265**.

### Score Interpretation:
* **Green (0-24):** Normal operations.
* **Yellow (25-74):** Minor anomaly detected; warrants review.

attributes.title: [CCI-000266] CA-5

attributes.description:
Why and When: Use this visualization to get a quick, high-level overview of which components are sending audit data. It's the first place to look to ensure that a system's logs are being collected at all. A component not appearing here indicates a critical failure in log collection or forwarding.

How to Use: Each bar represents a unique log source, identified by 'host.name'. The height of the bar indicates the total number of log events received in the selected time frame. Look for hosts that you expect to see. If a host is missing, begin troubleshooting its logging configuration.
attributes.title: CCI-000171] Audit Log Volume by Source

attributes.description:
Why and When: This chart provides the 'analysis' capability required by the control. Use it to understand the character of events coming from a specific component or the entire system. It helps you move beyond 'are there logs?' to 'what are the logs about?'. It's particularly useful for spotting anomalies; for example, a sudden spike in 'authentication' or 'network' events on a server that doesn't normally have them.

How to Use: The donut chart shows the proportional breakdown of event categories and types. Click on a slice to filter the entire dashboard by that specific category or type. When filtered to a single component, this chart should provide a stable, predictable pattern of activity. Deviations from that baseline warrant further investigation.
attributes.title: [CCI-000171] Audit Event Actions by Type

attributes.description:
Why and When: This table is the core of 'on-demand audit review'. When you need to see the actual log messages for a component, this is where you look. It provides the ground-truth evidence for compliance and forensic analysis.

How to Use: After filtering the dashboard to a single component, use this table to review its latest audit events. The columns provide key context like the timestamp, the action performed, and the user involved. Use the KQL search bar at the top of the Kibana page to perform detailed searches within these results, which fulfills the 'on-demand analysis and reporting' requirement.
attributes.title: [CCI-000171] Recent Audit Events

attributes.description:
**Why and When:** This table provides the context for anomalies where a rare action occurred on a specific host (supporting CCI-000171 and CCI-001910). It's the primary tool for understanding why the ML job flagged the behavior as anomalous.

**How to Use:** After identifying an anomaly in the trend chart, filter this table to that time. The `partition_field_value` column shows the host where the anomaly occurred, and the `by_field_value` shows the specific `event.action` that was rare for that host. Check the associated `influencers.user.name` and `influencers.process.name` to understand the full context and determine if the action was legitimate.
attributes.title: [AU-12, CCIs 000171, 001910] ML - Detailed Rare Action by Host Anomalies

attributes.title: [CCI-000171]

attributes.description:
**Why and When:** This table is your primary tool for investigating rare audit configuration change anomalies identified by the trend chart. It provides the necessary context to determine if a change was an authorized risk adjustment (compliant with CCI-000172) or a potential security incident.

**How to Use:** After identifying a time of interest, filter this table to that timeframe. The `by_field_value` column will tell you the exact `event.action` that was anomalous. Examine the `influencers.user.name` and `influencers.process.command_line` fields to understand who made the change and how. This information is critical for verifying the change against change management records.
attributes.title: [AU-12, CCI-000172] ML - Detailed Rare Config Change Anomalies

attributes.description: This visualization displays the health status for CCI-000172, adjusting audit levels in response to risk, based on the anomaly scores from the associated ML job. A high score indicates rare or unusual audit configuration changes that may require investigation.
attributes.title: [CCI-000172] Audit Generation - Adjusting Audit Levels in Response to Risk ML

attributes.description:
**Why and When:** This table provides the most direct evidence of compliance. [cite: 295] It captures the specific events where an administrator or automated process actively changed the audit configuration on a system. [cite_start]Look for these events immediately following a known change in risk. [cite: 295, 296]

**How an Analyst Can Use It:**
1. [cite_start]Use the time filter to narrow your search to the period just after the risk event occurred. [cite: 297]
2. [cite_start]Filter by `host.name` to focus on the relevant systems. [cite: 298]
3. Examine the results for processes like `auditctl` (Linux rule loading), `auditpol.exe` (Windows policy change), or modifications to key configuration files (e.g., `filebeat.yml`).
4. [cite_start]The `user.name`, `process.command_line`, and `@timestamp` fields provide a clear record of who made what change and when. [cite: 299] [cite_start]This is your 'smoking gun' evidence. [cite: 300]

query.query: ((event.category:(configuration OR policy) AND event.type:(change OR creation OR update))) OR (winlog.event_id:(4719 OR 4715 OR 4902 OR 4907 OR 1102)) OR (process.name:(auditctl OR augenrules OR auditpol.exe OR wevtutil.exe OR reg.exe OR sc.exe) AND event.category:process AND event.type:start) OR (process.name:powershell.exe AND process.command_line:(*Set-AuditRule* OR *New-NetFirewallRule* OR *Set-NetFirewallRule* OR *-ExecutionPolicy*)) OR (file.path:("/etc/audit/rules.d/*" OR "/etc/audit/auditd.conf" OR "/etc/rsyslog.conf" OR "/etc/syslog-ng.conf" OR "*filebeat.yml" OR "*winlogbeat.yml" OR "*auditbeat.yml" OR "*osquery.conf") AND event.action:(file_change OR file_create OR file_modify OR file_rename)) OR (registry.path:("HKLM\\SYSTEM\\CurrentControlSet\\Services\\EventLog\\*" OR "HKLM\\SOFTWARE\\Policies\\Microsoft\\Windows\\EventLog\\*" OR "HKLM\\SOFTWARE\\Elastic\\*") AND event.type:(change OR creation OR deletion)) OR ((cloud.service.name:CloudTrail AND event.action:UpdateTrail) OR (cloud.service.name:("GCP Audit Logs" OR "Azure Activity Logs") AND event.action:(*.update OR *.write OR *.create OR *.patch) AND (resource.type:(*audit* OR *log* OR *monitor*) OR event.summary:(*audit* OR *log* OR *monitor* OR *diagnostic*))))

attributes.title: [CCI-000172] Audit Policy & Configuration Changes

attributes.description:
**Why and When:** This visualization demonstrates a qualitative shift in audit focus. [cite_start]If a new risk involves file integrity, you should see an increase in `file_modify` or `file_access` events. [cite: 290] [cite_start]This shows that the audit adjustment was not just about volume, but about collecting more relevant data. [cite: 291]

**How an Analyst Can Use It:**
1. [cite_start]Select a time range *before* the risk event. [cite: 292] [cite_start]Note the percentage breakdown of the top event actions. [cite: 292]
2. [cite_start]Select a time range *after* the risk event. [cite: 293]
3. Compare the two breakdowns. [cite_start]A significant change in the distribution of event actions (e.g., `file_change` events becoming more prominent) provides strong evidence that the audit focus was adjusted to meet the new risk. [cite: 294]

attributes.title: [CCI-000172] Audit Event Type Breakdown (Before vs. After)

attributes.description:
This control requires demonstrating that the level of audit review, analysis, and reporting is adjusted in response to a change in risk. [cite_start]These visualizations help you gather evidence of such adjustments. [cite: 282]

**Analyst Guidance:**
Compliance with CCI-000172 is demonstrated by correlating an external risk change (e.g., a new threat actor, a critical vulnerability disclosure) with an internal change in audit posture. [cite_start]Your task is to prove this connection. [cite: 283]

1.  [cite_start]**Identify a Risk Event:** Pinpoint a specific date/time when your organization recognized a change in risk that should have prompted an adjustment in auditing. [cite: 283]
2.  [cite_start]**Filter for Components:** Use the dashboard's global filters (e.g., `host.name: <your_component_name>` or `data_stream.dataset: <your_dataset>`) to focus on the systems or services affected by the risk change. [cite: 284]
3.  **Analyze Trends:** Use the 'Audit Event Volume Trend' visualization to see if there was a noticeable increase in log volume for the affected components after the risk event. [cite_start]A sustained increase is evidence of enhanced logging. [cite: 285, 286]
4.  **Verify Policy Changes:** The 'Audit Policy & Configuration Changes' table is your most direct evidence. [cite_start]Look for events immediately following the risk event that show new audit rules being loaded (`auditctl`), policies being applied (`auditpol.exe`), or configuration files being modified. [cite: 286, 287]
5.  **Compare Event Types:** Use the 'Audit Event Type Breakdown' to compare the distribution of event types *before* and *after* the risk event. [cite_start]A change in the distribution (e.g., a shift from monitoring logins to monitoring file integrity) demonstrates a qualitative adjustment to auditing. [cite: 288, 289]

attributes.title: [CCI-000172]

attributes.description:
Why this visualization works: It provides the critical, actionable intelligence needed to respond to a potential PII data breach. By listing the specific users, source IPs, file paths, and processes associated with an anomaly, it allows analysts to immediately understand the scope and nature of the unusual access, which is essential for assessing likelihood and impact.

How an analyst can use it: When a PII access anomaly is flagged, this table is your first stop. Sort by 'anomaly_score' to address the most severe deviations first. Examine the influencers to answer key questions: Which user ('influencers.user.name') accessed the data? From where ('influencers.source.ip')? What specific file or URL ('influencers.file.path', 'influencers.url.path') was accessed? This detailed context is vital for a swift and effective investigation.
attributes.title: [CCI-004620] ML - Detailed PII Access Anomalies

attributes.description: This visualization displays the health status for CCI-004620 by monitoring the highest anomaly scores from its associated Machine Learning job, which detects unusual access patterns to PII. A high anomaly score indicates a significant deviation from the baseline, suggesting an increased likelihood or impact of a privacy incident.
attributes.title: [CCI-004620] Determine Likelihood and Impact for PII ML

attributes.description:
This visualization breaks down PII access events by the user who initiated them.

**Why this visualization works:**
It provides an immediate, at-a-glance view of which users are the most active in accessing PII. This is crucial for identifying potential insider threats, compromised accounts, or simply users whose roles grant them a high level of data access. Understanding this distribution helps quantify risk, as an organization can focus its oversight on the users and roles with the highest activity.

**How an analyst can use it:**
- Look for unexpected names in the top users. If a user from a department with no business need for PII access appears, it warrants immediate investigation.
- Monitor for significant shifts in the distribution. If a user suddenly becomes much more active, their account could be compromised or their behavior may have changed.
- Click on a slice of the pie to filter the entire dashboard by that user, allowing for a deep dive into their specific PII access activities.

query.query: file.path:(*pii* OR *ssn* OR *customer* OR *personal*) AND event.type:access

attributes.title: [CCI-004620] Top Users Accessing PII

attributes.description:
This visualization displays a time-series trend of access events targeting data sources identified as containing PII.

**Why this visualization works:**
It provides a dynamic view of how frequently PII is being accessed, allowing analysts to establish a baseline of normal activity. Deviations from this baseline, such as sudden spikes or a sustained increase in access, are immediately visible. This directly helps in assessing the **likelihood** of a privacy incident; higher-than-normal access frequency increases that likelihood.

**How an analyst can use it:**
- Monitor the chart for significant spikes in access events. A sudden increase could indicate a data exfiltration attempt, an automated script malfunctioning, or a compromised account.
- Use the time range selector to zoom in on periods of high activity for further investigation in the detailed table.
- Observe the trend over weeks or months to identify gradual increases in access that may warrant a review of access policies.

query.query: (file.path:(*pii* OR *ssn* OR *customer* OR *personal*) OR url.path:(*/api/users* OR */account* OR */profile*) OR database.table:(*users* OR *clients* OR *patients*)) AND event.category:(file OR web OR database OR iam) AND event.type:(access OR info)

attributes.title: [CCI-004620] PII Access Trend (converted)

attributes.description:
This table provides a granular, event-by-event log of every time a PII data source was accessed.

**Why this visualization works:**
This detailed view is critical for both incident investigation and risk assessment. It moves beyond 'if' PII was accessed to 'who, what, where, and when'. This information is the primary input for determining the potential **impact** of a privacy incident. For example, access to a database table containing millions of user records has a much higher impact than access to a single user's file.

**How an analyst can use it:**
- When a spike is observed on the trend chart, filter this table to that time range to see the specific events.
- Sort by `user.name` to track the activity of a specific individual or service account.
- Sort by `file.path` or `url.path` to see if a specific PII resource is being disproportionately targeted.
- Use the `source.ip` to identify the origin of the access request, which can help detect unauthorized access from external or unusual internal locations.

query.query: (file.path:(*pii* OR *ssn* OR *customer* OR *personal*) OR url.path:(*/api/users* OR */account* OR */profile*) OR database.table:(*users* OR *clients* OR *patients*)) AND event.category:(file OR web OR database OR iam) AND event.type:(access OR info)

attributes.title: [CCI-004620] Detailed PII Access Events

attributes.description:
This section provides visualizations to help you assess compliance with CCI-004620. The goal is to conduct a risk assessment by determining the likelihood and impact of adverse effects on individuals from the processing of Personally Identifiable Information (PII).

**How an analyst can use it for component-level assessment:**
1.  Utilize the global filters at the top of the dashboard to isolate a specific component (e.g., `host.name: <your_component_name>` or `tags: <your_asset_tag>`).
2.  **Assess Likelihood:** Observe the 'PII Access Trend' visualization for the frequency and volume of access events. Frequent or anomalous spikes in access by certain users or from specific locations can indicate a higher likelihood of a privacy incident.
3.  **Assess Impact:** Use the 'Detailed PII Access Events' table and the 'Top PII Resources Accessed' chart to understand the scope of PII access. A high count of records accessed or access to particularly sensitive data repositories indicates a higher potential impact.
4.  **Investigate Anomalies:** The visualizations highlight who is accessing what, and when. Any access by unauthorized users or from unexpected locations should be cross-referenced with system and application logs to determine the nature of the event and whether it constitutes a privacy violation. The data gathered here forms the factual basis for your risk assessment report.
attributes.title: [CCI-004620] Determine Likelihood and Impact for PII

**How an analyst can use it for component-level assessment:**
1.  Utilize the global filters at the top of the dashboard to isolate a specific component (e.g., `host.name: <your_component_name>` or `tags: <your_asset_tag>`).
2.  **Assess Likelihood:** Observe the 'PII Access Trend' visualization for the frequency and volume of access events. Frequent or anomalous spikes in access by certain users or from specific locations can indicate a higher likelihood of a privacy incident.

4.  **Investigate Anomalies:** The visualizations highlight who is accessing what, and when. attributes.title: [RA-3 CCI-004620]

attributes.description:
**Why this visualization works:** This visualization displays the maximum anomaly score over time for the job monitoring security report execution events. It helps to quickly identify any unusual patterns in how security reports are being run or generated. This could include unauthorized users attempting to execute reports, or a sudden change in the frequency or volume of report executions, which might indicate a compliance gap related to report availability or integrity. 

**How an analyst can use it:** Look for spikes in the anomaly score. A high score suggests an unexpected or suspicious report execution event. Use the time selector to pinpoint these periods and investigate the specific `user.name`, `kibana.report.name`, and `host.name` associated with the anomaly to determine if the execution was authorized and consistent with security policies.
attributes.title: [CA-7, CCI-003875] ML - Security Report Execution Anomaly Trend

attributes.description: This section provides visualizations to help you assess compliance with CCI-003875, focusing on monitoring security report execution events. This visualization displays the health status of security report execution based on anomaly scores from the associated ML job. To perform a component-level compliance assessment, utilize the global filters at the top of the Kibana dashboard. Apply filters like 'host.name: <your_component_name>' or 'user.name: <user_name>' to focus on specific systems or users. Observe the trends and individual anomaly events. A high anomaly score indicates a significant deviation from the established baseline, which could signify unauthorized report executions, failures in report generation, or attempts to manipulate reporting, all of which warrant immediate investigation.
attributes.title: [CA-7, CCI-003875] Security Report Execution Monitoring ML

attributes.description:
This table provides a detailed breakdown of each security report execution event.

**Why and When It's Used:**
This view is essential for detailed auditing and investigation.
When the trend chart shows an anomaly or you need to verify specific reporting activities, this table provides the ground truth.
It allows you to see the exact time, user, and report name for each execution.

**How an Analyst Can Use It:**
- Sort by 'user.name' to review all reports run by a specific individual.
- Sort by 'kibana.report.name' to see how often a particular report is generated.
- Use this table to confirm that authorized personnel are running the correct reports.
If you see an unfamiliar user or report name, it warrants an investigation.

attributes.title: CA-7, CCI-003875

attributes.description:
**Why and When:** Use this trend chart to visually verify that security reports are being generated consistently over time.
A flat line or significant gaps may indicate a failure in the reporting process, violating the 'organization-defined frequency' requirement.
Review this chart periodically (e.g., weekly, monthly) to match the observed generation frequency against the required schedule for key security reports.

**How:** Hover over the chart to see report counts for specific time intervals.
Use the time picker to analyze specific periods (e.g., the last 30 days).
The chart is broken down by report name, allowing you to filter for a single, critical report to analyze its specific trend.

query.query: (event.category:iam AND event.type:access AND event.action:(*report* OR *export* OR *download*)) AND (kibana.report.name:(*security* OR *state* OR *compliance* OR *audit* OR *vulnerability*) OR process.args:(*--output* OR *--format*report* OR *--export*))

attributes.title: [CA-7 / CCI-000279] Security Report Generation Trend

attributes.description: This section provides visualizations to help you assess compliance with CCI-000279, focusing on the timely generation and delivery of security-related information. This visualization displays the health status of security report generation based on anomaly scores from the associated ML job. To perform a component-level compliance assessment, utilize the global filters at the top of the Kibana dashboard. Apply filters like 'host.name: <your_component_name>' or 'user.name: <user_name>' to focus on a specific system or user. Observe the trends and individual anomaly events. A high anomaly score (e.g., 75 or above) indicates a significant deviation from the established baseline and warrants immediate investigation, as it could signify issues with report availability or unauthorized attempts to suppress reporting.
attributes.title: [CA-7, CCI-000279] Security Report Generation Status ML

attributes.description:
**Why this visualization works:** This visualization displays the maximum anomaly score over time for the job monitoring security report generation events. It helps detect unusual behavior that might indicate issues with the timely generation of security-related information, such as a sudden drop in reports or an unexpected spike. 

**How an analyst can use it:** Look for spikes in the anomaly score. A high score indicates a significant deviation from normal activity. Use the time selector to isolate these periods and investigate the underlying causes. This can help identify potential failures in automated reporting or unauthorized attempts to interfere with report generation.
attributes.title: [CA-7, CCI-000279] ML - Report Generation Anomaly Trend (converted)

attributes.description:
**Why and When:** This table provides the ground-truth evidence for each report generation event.
Use it to investigate specific activities, verify which user generated a report at a precise time, and confirm the outcome.
It is essential for drilling down from a trend chart or conducting a spot-check on a specific day's reporting activity.

**How:** Sort by any column to organize the data.
For instance, sort by 'user.name' to see all reports generated by a specific person or service account.
Filter by 'event.outcome:failure' to quickly identify and investigate any issues with the reporting system.
This table is critical for confirming that reports are being generated by the correct, authorized personnel or roles as defined in your organization's policy.

attributes.description:
**Why and When:** This chart provides a quick overview of which users or service accounts are generating reports.
It is useful for verifying that only authorized personnel are performing reporting functions.
Use this as a high-level check to spot unauthorized activity or to confirm that automated reporting accounts are functioning as expected.

**How:** Review the chart to ensure all users listed are authorized to generate reports.
An unexpected name in this list warrants immediate investigation. Click on any slice to filter the entire dashboard for that specific user, allowing for a deeper analysis of all reports they have generated.
